topics.title: Quality Attributes of Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT758 - FOOD SAFETY AND SANITATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Attributes of Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Food Quality

In the context of food science and the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, **food quality** is defined as the composite of those characteristics that differentiate individual units of a food product and have significance in determining the degree of acceptability of that product by the consumer. It is a multi-dimensional construct that encompasses all the attributes that influence a food item's value to a user, including sensory properties, nutritional composition, functional traits, safety, and shelf-life.

Mathematically, food quality can be expressed as a vector function:

$$Q = f(A, N, S, F, T, Sh)$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Overall Quality
- $A$ = Appearance attributes (color, size, shape, gloss)
- $N$ = Nutritional quality
- $S$ = Safety/Wholesomeness
- $F$ = Flavor profile (taste + aroma)
- $T$ = Texture (mouthfeel)
- $Sh$ = Shelf-life stability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Quality attributes are broadly classified into **visible/physical**, **invisible/compositional**, and **sensory** parameters. Understanding the inter-relationship between these is fundamental to Module 1.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

**Intuitive Explanation:** Think of food quality like a **report card for a meal**. Just as a student is evaluated on multiple subjects (math, science, language, art, behavior), food is evaluated on multiple "subjects" — color (how it looks), taste (how it flavors), texture (how it feels), nutrition (how it nourishes), and safety (how safe it is to eat). A student scoring well only in math is "incomplete" in their education, just as a food that is delicious but unsafe (e.g., contaminated ice cream) fails the overall quality test even if it scores 10/10 on taste.

### 1.3 Classification of Food Quality Attributes (KTU Board Framework)

The KTU 2024 curriculum groups quality attributes into the following primary categories:

| # | Category | Examples | Measurement Approach |
|---|----------|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Sensory/Organoleptic** | Color, flavor, taste, texture, aroma | Human panels, electronic sensors |
| 2 | **Nutritional** | Proteins, vitamins, minerals, fiber | Wet lab chemistry, spectroscopy |
| 3 | **Safety** | Microbial load, toxins, contaminants | Microbiology, HPLC, ELISA |
| 4 | **Shelf-life/Stability** | Rancidity, moisture, color change | Accelerated storage tests |
| 5 | **Functional** | WHC, OHC, foaming, emulsification | Instrumental rheology |
| 6 | **Processing-related** | Yield, throughput, uniformity | Process engineering metrics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Sensory Quality:** Sensory quality refers to the perceived characteristics of food as experienced by the human senses — sight (vision), smell (olfaction), taste (gustation), touch (somesthesis), and hearing (audition during mastication).

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Food Quality (KTU Mnemonic)

To remember the KTU-prescribed framework, use the **AST** acronym:
- **A** — *Acceptability* (does the consumer want it?)
- **S** — *Safety* (will it harm the consumer?)
- **T** — *Trueness-to-Standard* (does it meet specifications?)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Quality Attribute

#### 2.1.1 **Color** (Visual Attribute)

Color is often the first quality parameter judged by the consumer — *"we eat with our eyes first."*

- **Why it matters:** Color signals freshness, ripeness, processing adequacy, and absence of spoilage. It influences purchase decisions before the product is tasted.
- **How it forms:**
  - **Natural pigments:** Chlorophyll (green), Carotenoids (orange/yellow), Anthocyanins (red/blue/purple), Betalains (red beet), Myoglobin (red meat).
  - **Non-enzymatic browning (Maillard reaction):** Between reducing sugars and amino acids.
  - **Enzymatic browning:** PPO enzyme action on phenols (e.g., cut apple browning).
- **Measurement:** Tristimulus colorimetry — L*, a*, b* system (CIE Lab).

**Hunter L\*a\*b\* coordinates:**
- **L\*** = Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)
- **a\*** = Redness (+) to Greenness (−)
- **b\*** = Yellowness (+) to Blueness (−)

#### 2.1.2 **Flavor** (Taste + Aroma)

Flavor is the **combined perception** of gustatory (taste buds) and olfactory (aroma receptors) stimuli.

- **Five basic tastes:** Sweet, Sour, Salty, Bitter, Umami.
- **Aroma compounds:** Volatiles detected by olfactory epithelium — over 7000 known in foods.
- **Flavor Off-Flavor (FO/OF):** Undesirable flavor deviation due to oxidation, microbial spoilage, or chemical contamination.

#### 2.1.3 **Texture**

Texture is the **rheological and structural** manifestation of food as perceived by mechanical, tactile, and visual receptors.

**Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) parameters** (Bourne, 1978 framework):
- **Hardness** — force at maximum compression
- **Cohesiveness** — strength of internal bonds
- **Springiness** — elastic recovery
- **Adhesiveness** — work needed to overcome sticky forces
- **Chewiness** = Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness
- **Gumminess** = Hardness × Cohesiveness

#### 2.1.4 **Nutritional Quality**

Refers to the content and bioavailability of essential nutrients:
- **Macro-nutrients:** Carbohydrates, proteins, lipids
- **Micro-nutrients:** Vitamins (A, B-complex, C, D, E, K), minerals (Ca, Fe, Zn, I)
- **Bioactive compounds:** Polyphenols, flavonoids, carotenoids, omega-3 fatty acids

**Nutritional Quality Index (NQI):**

$$\text{NQI} = \frac{\text{Actual Nutrient Content}}{\text{Recommended Daily Allowance (RDA)}} \times 100$$

#### 2.1.5 **Safety Quality**

Freedom from:
- **Biological hazards:** Pathogens (*Salmonella*, *E. coli*, *Listeria*, *Staph. aureus*)
- **Chemical hazards:** Pesticide residues, heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg, As), mycotoxins
- **Physical hazards:** Glass, metal, bone fragments, stones
- **Allergens:** The "Big 9" — milk, egg, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soy, sesame

#### 2.1.6 **Shelf-life**

The period during which a product retains acceptable sensory, nutritional, and safety qualities under defined storage conditions.

**Accelerated Shelf-life Test (ASLT) — Q₁₀ / Arrhenius model:**

$$Q_{10} = \frac{\text{Rate at } (T+10)^{\circ}\text{C}}{\text{Rate at } T^{\circ}\text{C}}$$

A Q₁₀ of 2 means reaction rate doubles for every 10°C rise.

**Storage Stability Equation:**

$$\text{SLF (Storage Life Factor)} = \frac{\text{Time to reach quality limit at elevated } T}{\text{Time to reach quality limit at ambient } T}$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quality Vector | $Q = f(A, N, S, F, T, Sh)$ | Dimensionless index | Overall quality assessment |
| Color Difference | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(L_2-L_1)^2 + (a_2-a_1)^2 + (b_2-b_1)^2}$ | ΔE units | Color change quantification |
| Chewiness | $Ch = H \times Co \times Sp$ | N·m or g·mm | TPA texture analysis |
| NQI | $\text{NQI} = \frac{\text{Actual}}{\text{RDA}} \times 100$ | % | Nutritional labeling |
| Q₁₀ Model | $Q_{10} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Ratio (typically 2-4) | Accelerated shelf-life |
| Arrhenius Rate | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Reaction kinetics |
| Water Activity Safety Limit | $a_w \le 0.60$ (molds), $0.85$ (yeast), $0.91$ (bacteria) | Unitless | Microbial growth prediction |
| Peroxide Value | $\text{PV} = \frac{(S-B) \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | meq O₂/kg | Lipid oxidation |
| TVBN (Total Volatile Basic Nitrogen) | $\text{TVBN} = \frac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 14}{W}$ | mg N/100g | Seafood/fish spoilage |
| TBA Number | $\text{TBA} = A_{532} \times 7.8$ | mg MDA/kg | Secondary oxidation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever asked about quality attributes, ALWAYS structure the answer using: (1) **Definition** → (2) **Measurement method** → (3) **Factors affecting** → (4) **Industrial significance**. This 4-step pattern is the KTU valuation gold-standard.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Industry

| Sector | Quality Attribute Focus | Industry Application |
|---|---|---|
| Dairy | Fat %, SNF, microbial count | FSSAI grading of milk |
| Bakery | Volume, crumb texture, color | Consumer acceptance of bread |
| Meat & Poultry | Color (bloom), WHC, tenderness | Slaughter & cold-chain QA |
| Beverages | Brix, acidity, color stability | Soft drink & juice QC |
| Cereals | Cooking time, gelatinization | RTE (Ready-to-Eat) foods |
| Fats & Oils | PV, FFA, iodine value | Refined oil certification |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Color Difference Calculation (ΔE) in CIE Lab Space

In food quality analysis, when comparing two food samples (e.g., fresh tomato vs. stored tomato), color difference is computed using the **CIE 1976 L\*a\*b\* Euclidean distance**.

**Step 1: Measure Tristimulus Values**

A colorimeter measures each sample to obtain three coordinates:
- $(L_1^*, a_1^*, b_1^*)$ — Reference sample (e.g., fresh-cut apple)
- $(L_2^*, a_2^*, b_2^*)$ — Test sample (e.g., apple after 7 days storage)

**Step 2: Compute Individual Coordinate Differences**

$$\Delta L^* = L_2^* - L_1^*$$

$$\Delta a^* = a_2^* - a_1^*$$

$$\Delta b^* = b_2^* - b_1^*$$

**Step 3: Apply Euclidean Distance Formula**

Because color space is **3-dimensional**, the total color difference is the magnitude of the difference vector:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta E^* &= \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(L_2^* - L_1^*)^2 + (a_2^* - a_1^*)^2 + (b_2^* - b_1^*)^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation Scale (KTU Reference)**

| ΔE Range | Perceptible Difference | Examiner's Note |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 1 | Not perceptible to human eye | Trace difference |
| 1 – 2 | Perceptible only on close observation | Acceptable for many products |
| 2 – 3.5 | Perceptible at a glance | Noticeable change |
| 3.5 – 5 | Clearly perceptible difference | Quality concern |
| > 5 | Strong color difference | Reject / reformulation needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The CIE Lab space is designed to be **perceptually uniform**, meaning a numerical change in any direction corresponds to approximately equal perceived color change. This is why ΔE is the gold standard for food color quality.

### 3.2 Derivation: Nutritional Quality Index (NQI) — Worked Example

A breakfast cereal claims "100% RDA of Vitamin C per serving." The actual content measured in lab is 42 mg per serving, while the RDA for an adult is 75 mg/day.

**Step 1: Identify the Variables**

$$\text{Actual Nutrient Content} = 42 \text{ mg}$$

$$\text{RDA} = 75 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the NQI Formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NQI} &= \frac{\text{Actual Nutrient Content}}{\text{RDA}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{42 \text{ mg}}{75 \text{ mg}} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Result**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NQI} &= \frac{42}{75} \times 100 \\
&= 0.56 \times 100 \\
&= 56\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Industrial Interpretation**

The cereal delivers only **56% of the claimed RDA**, so it is **non-compliant** with the label claim. Quality control would mandate reformulation, and the labeling would need to be corrected to "56% RDA of Vitamin C per serving."

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Quality Attributes

The following matrix summarizes the **inter-relationships** between major food quality attributes — a key analytical tool expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

| Attribute ↓ / Affects → | Appearance | Flavor | Texture | Nutrition | Safety | Shelf-life |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Time** | ↓ (fade) | ↓ (off-flavor) | ↓ (soften/harden) | ↓ (vitamin loss) | ↓ (microbial rise) | ↓ (endpoint) |
| **Temperature ↑** | ↑ browning | ↑ Maillard | ↑ softening | ↑ degradation | ↑ pathogen risk | ↓ |
| **Water Activity (a_w) ↑** | ↑ microbial | ↑ souring | ↑ sticky | ↓ (leaching) | ↓ (spoilage) | ↓ |
| **Oxygen ↑** | ↑ oxidation browning | ↑ rancidity | ↑ lipid effects | ↑ PUFA loss | ↓ (mold) | ↓ |
| **pH shift** | ↑ pigment change | ↑ sour/bitter | ↑ protein denaturation | ↓ mineral bioavailability | ↑ *Clostridium* risk | ↓ |
| **Processing Heat** | ↑ Maillard | ↑ cooked flavor | ↑ starch gelatinize | ↑ digestibility; ↓ vitamins | ↓ pathogens (positive) | ↑ |
| **Packaging (Modified Atmosphere)** | ↑ retention | ↑ retention | ↑ retention | ↑ retention | ↑ (N₂ flush) | ↑↑ |

> **Legend:** ↑ = Increase / improvement, ↓ = Decrease / deterioration

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensory vs. Instrumental Quality Evaluation

| Parameter | Sensory Evaluation | Instrumental Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| Color | Visual panel (Hedonic scale) | Colorimeter (L\*a\*b\*) |
| Texture | Mouthfeel panel (TPA by panel) | Texture Analyzer (TPA) |
| Flavor | Trained tasting panel | GC-MS, E-nose |
| Aroma | Sniff panel (Olfactometry) | Electronic nose (MOS sensors) |
| Appearance | Visual scoring | Image analysis (computer vision) |
| Microbiological | — | Plate count, PCR, ELISA |
| Cost | Moderate (panel time) | High (instrument cost) |
| Speed | Slow (training needed) | Fast (seconds) |
| Objectivity | Subjective bias possible | Highly objective |
| KTU Question Weight | Common in 3-mark | Common in 14-mark |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define sensory quality of food and list its parameters.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Sensory quality of food refers to the perceived characteristics of food as experienced by the five human senses — **sight, smell, taste, touch, and hearing**. These are the attributes that determine a consumer's first impression and final acceptance of a food product.

The six major parameters of sensory quality are:
1. **Appearance** (size, shape, gloss, uniformity)
2. **Color** (natural and induced pigments)
3. **Flavor** (basic tastes + aroma compounds)
4. **Texture** (rheological and structural properties)
5. **Aroma** (volatile compounds perceived by smell)
6. **Mouthfeel** (tactile sensations during chewing)

*(3 marks — full credit for complete listing and definition)*

---

#### **Question 2: What is water activity and why is it important in food quality?** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Water activity (a_w)** is defined as the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a food substrate to the partial vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature:

$$a_w = \frac{P}{P_0} = \frac{\text{ERH}}{100}$$

Where ERH = Equilibrium Relative Humidity.

It is a critical food quality parameter because:
- It governs **microbial growth** — bacteria need a_w ≥ 0.91, yeast ≥ 0.85, molds ≥ 0.60.
- It influences **chemical reactions** — Maillard browning, lipid oxidation, enzymatic activity.
- It determines **texture** — crispiness, dryness, caking.
- It controls **shelf-life** — lower a_w = longer shelf-life.

*(3 marks — formula, microbial limits, and 2 quality implications)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory choice with (b) as alternative**

**[a] Describe in detail the classification of food quality attributes with suitable examples. Explain how nutritional quality is measured in food products.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Classification of Food Quality Attributes (7 marks):**

Food quality attributes are classified into three primary categories:

**1. Sensory / Organoleptic Attributes (Visual, Gustatory, Olfactory):**
- **Appearance:** Size, shape, gloss, surface texture, absence of defects.
- **Color:** Determined by pigments (chlorophyll, carotenoids, anthocyanins, myoglobin); measured using CIE L\*a\*b\* system.
- **Flavor:** Combined taste (sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami) and aroma (>7000 volatile compounds).
- **Texture:** Hardness, cohesiveness, springiness, chewiness, adhesiveness; analyzed using Texture Profile Analysis (TPA).
- **Aroma:** Volatiles detected by olfactory receptors; measured by GC-MS or E-nose.

**2. Invisible / Compositional Attributes:**
- **Nutritional Quality:** Macronutrients (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) and micronutrients (vitamins, minerals). Measured by proximate analysis (AOAC methods), spectrophotometry, atomic absorption spectroscopy.
- **Safety Quality:** Absence of biological (pathogens), chemical (pesticides, heavy metals), and physical (glass, metal) hazards. Tested via microbiological plating, ELISA, HPLC, metal detectors.
- **Shelf-life:** Product stability over time; predicted using Q₁₀ model and Arrhenius equation.

**3. Processing-related Attributes:**
- **Yield, throughput, energy efficiency, and process uniformity.**

*(7 marks — proper classification, examples, and measurement methods)*

**Part (b) — Measurement of Nutritional Quality (7 marks):**

Nutritional quality is measured using the following techniques:

| Nutrient | Analytical Method | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture | Oven drying (105°C to constant weight) | AOAC 934.01 |
| Protein | Kjeldahl method (N × 6.25) | AOAC 991.20 |
| Fat | Soxhlet extraction | AOAC 920.39 |
| Ash | Muffle furnace (550°C) | AOAC 942.05 |
| Fiber | Acid-alkali digestion | AOAC 991.43 |
| Vitamins | HPLC, UV-Vis spectrophotometry | AOAC 985.33 |
| Minerals | Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS) | AOAC 985.35 |
| Energy | Bomb calorimeter | AOAC 971.10 |

The **Nutritional Quality Index (NQI)** is used to assess whether a food delivers its claimed nutrient content:

$$\text{NQI} = \frac{\text{Actual Nutrient Content}}{\text{RDA}} \times 100$$

If NQI < 100%, the product is **non-compliant** with the label claim.

**Industrial Application:** Nutritional labeling under FSSAI Regulations, 2020 — every packaged food in India must declare energy, protein, fat, saturated fat, carbohydrates, sugar, and sodium content per 100 g/100 mL or per serving.

*(7 marks — analytical methods table, NQI formula, and FSSAI linkage)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative choice**

**[a] Explain the concept of Texture Profile Analysis (TPA). Describe in detail the sensory evaluation methods used in food quality assessment.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Texture Profile Analysis (7 marks):**

TPA, developed by **Bourne (1978)**, is an instrumental double-compression test that mimics the act of chewing. A food sample is compressed twice (typically to 30–75% of original height) using a Texture Analyzer, and the resulting force-time curve yields the following parameters:

| Parameter | Definition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** | Peak force during first compression | Resistance to bite |
| **Cohesiveness** | Ratio of work in 2nd compression to 1st | Internal bond strength |
| **Springiness** | Distance recovered between bites | Elastic recovery |
| **Adhesiveness** | Negative area after first bite | Stickiness to palate |
| **Chewiness** | Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness | Energy to chew solids |
| **Gumminess** | Hardness × Cohesiveness | Energy to chew semi-solids |
| **Resilience** | Area upstroke / area downstroke (1st compression) | Elastic recovery rate |

**Mathematical expression for chewiness:**

$$\text{Chewiness} = H \times Co \times Sp$$

Where $H$ = Hardness, $Co$ = Cohesiveness, $Sp$ = Springiness.

TPA is widely used in **bakery products** (bread firmness), **dairy** (yogurt gel strength), and **meat** (tenderness quantification).

*(7 marks — TPA curve parameters, mathematical formulation, and applications)*

**Part (b) — Sensory Evaluation Methods (7 marks):**

Sensory evaluation uses trained human panels to measure food quality attributes subjectively. It is classified into four major types:

**1. Discriminative Tests (Difference detection):**
- **Triangle Test:** 3 coded samples (2 identical, 1 different) — panelist identifies the odd one.
- **Duo-Trio Test:** Reference + 2 test samples — panelist matches one test to the reference.
- **Paired Comparison:** 2 samples — panelist identifies difference or preference.

**2. Descriptive Tests (Quantify attributes):**
- **Flavor Profile Method:** Trained panel describes aroma and flavor characteristics.
- **Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA):** Panel scores intensity of each attribute on a line scale.
- **Texture Profile Method (human):** Sensory analog to instrumental TPA.

**3. Hedonic Tests (Consumer preference):**
- **9-Point Hedonic Scale:** Panelist rates liking from 1 (dislike extremely) to 9 (like extremely).

**4. Specialized Tests:**
- **Time-Intensity (TI):** Perception over time after ingestion.
- **Free-Choice Profiling:** Panelists use their own descriptors.

**KTU Industrial Application:** Major food companies (Nestlé, ITC, Britannia) maintain in-house sensory panels for product development, shelf-life studies, and competitor benchmarking.

*(7 marks — classification, example tests, and industrial application)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in KTU Valuation — Lose 2-3 Marks Each:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "sensory" with "nutritional" attributes** — Color and flavor are *sensory*; vitamins and minerals are *nutritional*. Mixing them costs structure marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in formulas (e.g., ΔE, NQI, PV). KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.
> 3. **Not mentioning FSSAI/Codex linkage** in nutritional/safety answers — students lose 1-2 marks for missing regulatory anchoring.
> 4. **Writing "taste" and "flavor" as synonyms** — they are NOT the same. Taste = 5 basic modalities on the tongue; Flavor = taste + aroma + chemesthesis.
> 5. **Skipping the Q₁₀ or Arrhenius equation** in shelf-life answers — this is a *must-mention* in 14-mark questions on quality attributes.
> 6. **No diagram/table** in 14-mark answers → KTU board deducts up to 2 marks for poor presentation. Always include at least one structured table or flow diagram.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Core Definition:** Food quality = composite of attributes determining consumer acceptability, safety, and nutritional value.

✅ **AST Acronym:** **A**cceptability, **S**afety, **T**rueness-to-Standard — the three pillars of KTU quality framework.

✅ **Six Sensory Parameters:** Appearance, Color, Flavor, Texture, Aroma, Mouthfeel.

✅ **Five Basic Tastes:** Sweet, Sour, Salty, Bitter, Umami (the fifth taste, identified by Kikunae Ikeda in 1908).

✅ **CIE L\*a\*b\* System:** Universal color space — L\* (lightness), a\* (red-green), b\* (yellow-blue). ΔE > 2 indicates noticeable color change.

✅ **TPA Equation:** Chewiness = Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness.

✅ **Water Activity Limits (must memorize):** Bacteria ≤ 0.91, Yeast ≤ 0.85, Molds ≤ 0.60.

✅ **Q₁₀ Model:** Reaction rate doubles for every 10°C rise (Q₁₀ ≈ 2 for many food reactions).

✅ **Arrhenius Equation:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ — basis of accelerated shelf-life testing.

✅ **Big 9 Allergens (FSSAI-mandated labeling):** Milk, egg, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soy, sesame.

✅ **NQI Formula:** $\text{NQI} = \frac{\text{Actual Nutrient}}{\text{RDA}} \times 100$ — must be ≥ 100% to meet label claim.

✅ **Key Safety Hazards:** Biological (pathogens), Chemical (pesticides, mycotoxins, heavy metals), Physical (glass, metal, bone).

✅ **FSSAI Regulation Linkage:** All nutritional claims in India must comply with FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) labeling regulations, 2020.

✅ **Industrial Quality Control Tools:** HACCP, ISO 22000, FSSC 22000, Codex Alimentarius standards.

> **Final KTU Mnemonic for Module 1 Quality Attributes — "SAFE-CAN"**
> **S**afety → **A**ppearance → **F**lavor → **E**xcellence → **C**olor → **A**roma → **N**utrition
> *Use this in last-minute revision!*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Size and Shape
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT758 - FOOD SAFETY AND SANITATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Size and Shape

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1: SIZE AND SHAPE — CORE TECHNICAL OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Safety and Sanitation**, the *size* and *shape* of food materials, ingredients, particulates, and contaminants are fundamental **physical characteristics** that govern every downstream process — from handling, processing, packaging, and preservation to microbial contamination risk and cleaning efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Particle Size** is defined as the *smallest sieve aperture* through which a particle will pass, OR the *equivalent spherical diameter* of an irregularly shaped particle, typically expressed in **micrometers (µm)** or **millimeters (mm)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Particle Shape** is the geometric form of an individual particle, described qualitatively (spherical, cubic, flaky, fibrous, needle-like) or quantitatively using dimensionless **shape factors** (e.g., sphericity, aspect ratio, roundness).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Salt vs. Sugar" Intuition

Imagine you are a sanitation inspector in a spice factory. You are handed two white powders — one is **table salt** and the other is **powdered sugar**.

- **Salt** crystals: small, uniform, **cubic**, sharp-edged → same size, same shape.
- **Sugar** crystals: irregular, varied in **size and shape** — some blocky, some needle-like.

Even though both are edible white powders, the **size and shape** dictate:
1. How dust they generate (respirable hazard).
2. How easily microbes lodge in their crevices (sanitation risk).
3. How they flow through pipes (choking/blockage risk).
4. How well they can be washed/cleaned during CIP (Clean-In-Place).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of food particles like people entering a cinema hall. *Size* decides which door they can fit through (sieve), and *shape* decides whether they line up neatly (flowability) or jam at the gate (bridging in silos).

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (Syllabus Highlights)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Standard Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Particle Size (Sieve Aperture) | $D_p$ | mm or µm | Smallest mesh opening the particle passes through |
| Equivalent Spherical Diameter | $D_e$ | m | Diameter of a sphere having the same volume as the particle |
| Sphericity | $\phi_s$ | dimensionless | Ratio of surface area of equivalent sphere to actual particle surface area |
| Aspect Ratio | $AR$ | dimensionless | Ratio of longest to shortest dimension |
| Surface-to-Volume Ratio | $S/V$ | m⁻¹ | Available surface per unit volume — critical for cleaning |
| Bulk Density | $\rho_b$ | kg/m³ | Mass of particles per unit bulk volume |
| True Density | $\rho_p$ | kg/m³ | Mass per unit actual particle volume (no voids) |
| Porosity | $\varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Fraction of void space in bulk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, memorize: **Sphericity (φs)**, **Equivalent Diameter (De)**, and **Sauter Mean Diameter (Dsm)** — these are the most frequently tested concepts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Particle Size

Particle size is *not a single value* for a food material. Food powders, grains, and particulates are **polydisperse systems** — meaning they contain particles of many different sizes. Hence, we must describe them using **size distributions** and **mean diameters**.

### 2.1.1 Equivalent Diameters — The "Five Faces" of a Particle

Since real food particles are irregular, engineers define several **equivalent diameters**, each useful for different applications:

| Equivalent Diameter | Definition | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume Diameter, $D_v$** | Diameter of sphere with same volume as particle | Mass calculations, density |
| **Surface Diameter, $D_s$** | Diameter of sphere with same surface area as particle | Heat/mass transfer |
| **Stokes Diameter, $D_{st}$** | Diameter of sphere with same settling velocity | Sedimentation, air classification |
| **Sieve Diameter, $D_{A}$** | Smallest square aperture particle can pass through | Sieve analysis |
| **Projected Area Diameter, $D_a$** | Diameter of circle with same projected area as particle | Microscopy, image analysis |

---

## 2.2 Particle Shape — Quantitative Descriptors

### 2.2.1 Sphericity (φₛ)

Sphericity is the **most important shape descriptor** in food engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sphericity** of a particle is the ratio of the surface area of a sphere having the *same volume* as the particle to the *actual surface area* of the particle.

$$
\phi_s = \frac{\text{Surface area of equivalent sphere}}{\text{Actual surface area of particle}} = \frac{\pi D_v^2}{S_p}
$$

Where:
- $D_v$ = volume-equivalent diameter
- $S_p$ = actual surface area of particle

**Bounds:** $0 < \phi_s \leq 1$
- $\phi_s = 1$ → perfect sphere
- $\phi_s \rightarrow 0$ → highly elongated/irregular

### 2.2.2 Roundness and Aspect Ratio

$$
AR = \frac{L}{B}
$$

Where $L$ = longest dimension, $B$ = breadth (perpendicular to L).

---

## 2.3 Mean Diameters — Why They Matter

Since food powders contain particles of many sizes, we calculate a **representative mean diameter** depending on the property of interest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of mean depends on the application: use **Sauter Mean Diameter** for reactions, **mass-mean diameter** for conveying, etc.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi_s = \dfrac{\pi D_v^2}{S_p}$ | Sphericity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $D_{sm} = \dfrac{\sum n_i D_i^3}{\sum n_i D_i^2}$ | **Sauter Mean Diameter** (surface-volume mean) | m |
| 3 | $\bar{D}_{vw} = \dfrac{\sum w_i}{\sum (w_i / D_i)}$ | Volume-surface (Sauter) mean (mass basis) | m |
| 4 | $\bar{D}_{am} = \dfrac{\sum n_i D_i}{\sum n_i}$ | Arithmetic mean diameter (number basis) | m |
| 5 | $\bar{D}_{gm} = \left(\sum x_i \ln D_i\right)$ | Geometric mean diameter | m |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_p}$ | Porosity of bulk solids | dimensionless |
| 7 | $S/V = \dfrac{6}{\phi_s \cdot D_p}$ | Specific surface area for non-spherical particle | m⁻¹ |
| 8 | $AR = L/B$ | Aspect ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $K = \dfrac{D_p}{D_{\text{min}}}$ | Bulkiness factor (shape factor) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\ln \sigma_g = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum w_i (\ln D_i - \ln \bar{D}_{gm})^2}{\sum w_i}}$ | Geometric standard deviation | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> The **Sauter Mean Diameter** ($D_{sm}$) is the **single most-asked formula** in KTU Module 1 of Food Safety & Sanitation — both in Part A and Part B.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Sanitation & Cleaning:** Smaller particles have higher $S/V$ ratio → more surface to clean → more detergent/contact time needed.
2. **Microbial Contamination:** Smaller particle size = more crevices and larger surface = more niches for microbial attachment (e.g., *Salmonella* in cocoa powder, *Cronobacter* in infant formula).
3. **Flowability in Silos:** Irregular shape (low $\phi_s$) causes bridging and rat-holing → cross-contamination risk in food storage.
4. **Thermal Processing:** Heat transfer rate $\propto 1/D_p$ → smaller particles heat faster (critical in pasteurization of flour, milk powder).
5. **CIP (Clean-In-Place) Systems:** Pipe diameter and pump pressure selection depend on particle size of food being processed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Sphericity (φₛ)

**Starting Premise:** For a sphere of diameter $D_v$ (volume-equivalent), the surface area is:

$$
S_{sphere} = \pi D_v^2
$$

**Definition:** Sphericity is the ratio of the surface area of this *equivalent sphere* to the *actual* surface area of the irregular particle ($S_p$):

$$
\phi_s = \frac{S_{sphere}}{S_p} = \frac{\pi D_v^2}{S_p}
$$

**Rearranging** to express the actual surface area of a particle:

$$
S_p = \frac{\pi D_v^2}{\phi_s}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **specific surface area** (surface per unit volume) of a non-spherical particle becomes:

$$
a_s = \frac{S_p}{V_p} = \frac{\pi D_v^2 / \phi_s}{\frac{\pi}{6} D_v^3} = \frac{6}{\phi_s \cdot D_v}
$$

> This is the foundational equation used in all heat and mass transfer calculations involving food particulates.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Sauter Mean Diameter ($D_{sm}$)

**Problem Setup:** Consider $N$ particles with sizes $D_1, D_2, D_3, \ldots, D_N$ and counts $n_1, n_2, n_3, \ldots, n_N$.

**Goal:** Find a single diameter that gives the **same total surface area per unit volume** as the mixture.

**Step 1:** Total volume of all particles:

$$
V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D_i^3
$$

**Step 2:** Total surface area of all particles:

$$
A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \cdot \pi D_i^2
$$

**Step 3:** Specific surface area of the mixture:

$$
a_{s,mixture} = \frac{A_{total}}{V_{total}} = \frac{\sum n_i \pi D_i^2}{\sum n_i \frac{\pi}{6} D_i^3}
$$

**Step 4:** If the mixture is represented by a single equivalent sphere of diameter $D_{sm}$, its specific surface area is:

$$
a_{s,equiv} = \frac{\pi D_{sm}^2}{\frac{\pi}{6} D_{sm}^3} = \frac{6}{D_{sm}}
$$

**Step 5:** Equate (3) and (4):

$$
\frac{6}{D_{sm}} = \frac{\sum n_i D_i^2}{\sum n_i \frac{D_i^3}{6}}
$$

**Step 6:** Cross-multiply and simplify:

$$
6 \cdot \sum n_i \frac{D_i^3}{6} = D_{sm} \cdot \sum n_i D_i^2
$$

$$
\sum n_i D_i^3 = D_{sm} \cdot \sum n_i D_i^2
$$

**Step 7:** Final KTU formula:

$$
\boxed{D_{sm} = \frac{\sum n_i D_i^3}{\sum n_i D_i^2}}
$$

> This is the **Sauter Mean Diameter** — the **volume-surface mean diameter** — used in heat/mass transfer correlations for food particulates.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Question:** A food powder sample has the following particle size distribution (mass basis):

| Sieve Size (µm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|---|---|
| 1000 | 10 |
| 710 | 25 |
| 500 | 35 |
| 355 | 20 |
| 250 | 10 |

**Find:** (i) Mass-mean diameter, (ii) Sauter mean diameter.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute mean diameter of each fraction (geometric mean of upper and lower sieve):

$$
D_1 = \sqrt{1000 \times 710} = 842.6 \, \mu m
$$
$$
D_2 = \sqrt{710 \times 500} = 595.8 \, \mu m
$$
$$
D_3 = \sqrt{500 \times 355} = 421.3 \, \mu m
$$
$$
D_4 = \sqrt{355 \times 250} = 297.9 \, \mu m
$$
$$
D_5 = \sqrt{250 \times 0} = 125 \, \mu m \quad (\text{take lower as half the smallest})
$$

**Step 2:** Compute mass fractions $x_i = w_i / \sum w_i$:

$$
\sum w_i = 100 \text{ g}, \quad x_1 = 0.10, \ x_2 = 0.25, \ x_3 = 0.35, \ x_4 = 0.20, \ x_5 = 0.10
$$

**Step 3:** Mass-mean diameter:

$$
\bar{D}_{w} = \sum x_i D_i = (0.10)(842.6) + (0.25)(595.8) + (0.35)(421.3) + (0.20)(297.9) + (0.10)(125)
$$

$$
\bar{D}_{w} = 84.26 + 148.95 + 147.46 + 59.58 + 12.50 = 452.75 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 4:** Sauter mean diameter (mass basis):

$$
D_{sm} = \frac{1}{\sum (x_i / D_i)} = \frac{1}{(0.10/842.6) + (0.25/595.8) + (0.35/421.3) + (0.20/297.9) + (0.10/125)}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{0.0001187 + 0.0004196 + 0.0008308 + 0.0006714 + 0.0008000} = \frac{1}{0.0028405}
$$

$$
D_{sm} \approx 352.05 \, \mu m
$$

> **Key insight:** The Sauter mean diameter is *smaller* than the arithmetic mass-mean diameter because it is *weighted heavily by finer particles* (which contribute more surface area). This is critical for cleaning and sanitation calculations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

The following block diagram maps the *complete ecosystem* of size and shape characterization methods used in food safety and sanitation:

## 4.3 Effect of Particle Size on Sanitation Decisions
