<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Sphericity and state its significance in food sanitation.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Sphericity ($\phi_s$) is the ratio of the surface area of a sphere having the same volume as the particle to the actual surface area of the particle:

$$
\phi_s = \frac{\pi D_v^2}{S_p}
$$

- **1 mark** for the correct definition and formula.
- **1 mark** for stating the bounds: $0 < \phi_s \leq 1$.
- **1 mark** for significance: sphericity affects flowability, cleanability, and microbial adhesion in food powders — particles with low sphericity have higher surface area, harbor more microbes, and are harder to clean during CIP.

---

### **Question 2: What is Sauter Mean Diameter? Why is it preferred over arithmetic mean in food processing?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Sauter Mean Diameter** ($D_{sm}$) is the diameter of a sphere that has the same volume-to-surface area ratio as the entire particle population:

$$
D_{sm} = \frac{\sum n_i D_i^3}{\sum n_i D_i^2}
$$

- **1 mark** for definition and formula.
- **1 mark** for explaining that $D_{sm}$ represents the *surface-area-weighted* mean — gives more weight to fine particles.
- **1 mark** for the engineering preference: $D_{sm}$ is directly used in heat/mass transfer correlations (e.g., Nusselt number, Sherwood number) for food particles in drying, pasteurization, and frying. Arithmetic mean ignores the *disproportionate effect* of fines on surface phenomena.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Particle Size and Shape in Food Powders — A Comprehensive Study**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q. from Module 1]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Explain the various methods of particle size analysis used in the food industry. Discuss the principles of sieve analysis and laser diffraction techniques.*

**Model Solution:**

**Method 1: Sieve Analysis (3 Marks)**

- The oldest and most common method for granular food materials (sugar, salt, flour).
- A stack of standard sieves (e.g., 1000 µm, 500 µm, 250 µm, 125 µm, 75 µm) is arranged in descending order of aperture size.
- A known mass of sample is placed on the top sieve and shaken mechanically for a fixed time.
- The **mass retained** on each sieve is measured, and cumulative mass percent *passing* is plotted vs. sieve size on a log-log graph.
- The particle size is reported as the **sieve diameter** — the smallest aperture through which the particle passes.
- *Limitations:* Not suitable for cohesive powders (cocoa, milk powder) or particles below 44 µm.

**Method 2: Laser Diffraction (2 Marks)**

- Modern technique for fine food particles (1 µm to 1000 µm).
- A laser beam is passed through a dispersed particle suspension; the *angular distribution* of scattered light is measured.
- **Mie theory** (or Fraunhofer approximation) is used to compute the *volume-equivalent diameter* distribution.
- Advantage: fast, reproducible, and works for wet and dry samples.

**Method 3: Microscopy & Image Analysis (2 Marks)**

- Direct visual observation under optical/electron microscope.
- The projected area is measured → *projected area diameter* ($D_a$).
- Modern image analyzers can also extract *sphericity*, *aspect ratio*, and *circularity* simultaneously.
- Used for *Staphylococcus aureus* cluster studies, *Aspergillus* spore shapes, etc.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Derive the Sauter Mean Diameter formula and explain its use in determining the cleaning cycle time of a CIP system for a milk powder plant.*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 Marks):** See Section 3.2 of these notes. The final result is:

$$
D_{sm} = \frac{\sum n_i D_i^3}{\sum n_i D_i^2}
$$

**Application in CIP (3 Marks):**

- The CIP cleaning time is directly proportional to the **specific surface area** of the residue:

$$
t_{CIP} \propto \frac{S}{V} = \frac{6}{\phi_s \cdot D_{sm}}
$$

- Smaller $D_{sm}$ → larger $S/V$ → longer cleaning time.
- For a milk powder plant processing 50 µm particles, $S/V \approx 120,000 \text{ m}^{-1}$, requiring substantially longer rinse + caustic + acid cycles than for 500 µm sugar crystals.
- Therefore, $D_{sm}$ is a **critical input parameter** in CIP cycle validation under HACCP and ISO 22000 frameworks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Equivalent Diameters and Shape Factors — Theory and Application**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Internal Choice]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Define equivalent diameter. Explain the different types of equivalent diameters used for food particles.*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** An equivalent diameter is the diameter of a *sphere* that has the same property (volume, surface area, projected area, settling velocity, etc.) as the irregular particle.

**Types (6 Marks):**

1. **Volume Diameter ($D_v$):** Sphere with same *volume* as particle. Used in mass balance and density calculations. (1.5 Marks)
2. **Surface Diameter ($D_s$):** Sphere with same *surface area*. Used in heat/mass transfer where interfacial area matters. (1.5 Marks)
3. **Stokes Diameter ($D_{st}$):** Sphere with same *terminal settling velocity* under Stokes flow. Used in sedimentation, air classification of flour, dust. (1.5 Marks)
4. **Sieve Diameter ($D_A$):** Smallest square mesh the particle can pass through. Used in standard sieve analysis. (1 Mark)
5. **Projected Area Diameter ($D_a$):** Sphere with same *projected area* as the particle's silhouette. Used in microscopy and image analysis. (0.5 Mark)

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*A food powder has the following sieve analysis data:*

| Sieve (µm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|---|---|
| 1000 | 5 |
| 500 | 25 |
| 250 | 40 |
| 125 | 20 |
| 75 | 10 |

*Calculate (i) Mass-mean diameter, (ii) Sauter mean diameter, and (iii) the specific surface area assuming sphericity = 0.85.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Mean size of each fraction (Geometric mean of adjacent sieves)** (1 Mark)

$$
D_1 = \sqrt{1000 \times 500} = 707.1 \, \mu m
$$
$$
D_2 = \sqrt{500 \times 250} = 353.6 \, \mu m
$$
$$
D_3 = \sqrt{250 \times 125} = 176.8 \, \mu m
$$
$$
D_4 = \sqrt{125 \times 75} = 96.8 \, \mu m
$$
$$
D_5 = 75/2 = 37.5 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 2: Total mass = 100 g, mass fractions** (1 Mark)

$$
x_1 = 0.05, \ x_2 = 0.25, \ x_3 = 0.40, \ x_4 = 0.20, \ x_5 = 0.10
$$

**Step 3: Mass-mean diameter** (1.5 Marks)

$$
\bar{D}_w = \sum x_i D_i = (0.05)(707.1) + (0.25)(353.6) + (0.40)(176.8) + (0.20)(96.8) + (0.10)(37.5)
$$

$$
= 35.36 + 88.39 + 70.72 + 19.37 + 3.75 = 217.59 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 4: Sauter mean diameter** (2 Marks)

$$
D_{sm} = \frac{1}{\sum (x_i / D_i)} = \frac{1}{(0.05/707.1) + (0.25/353.6) + (0.40/176.8) + (0.20/96.8) + (0.10/37.5)}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{7.07\times10^{-5} + 7.07\times10^{-4} + 2.26\times10^{-3} + 2.07\times10^{-3} + 2.67\times10^{-3}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{7.78 \times 10^{-3}} = 128.5 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 5: Specific surface area with $\phi_s = 0.85$** (1.5 Marks)

$$
a_s = \frac{6}{\phi_s \cdot D_{sm}} = \frac{6}{0.85 \times 128.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 54,820 \, m^{-1}
$$

This high $S/V$ ratio indicates a powder that will require **extensive CIP cleaning** in any food processing line.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing $D_{sm}$ with arithmetic mean:** Many students compute $\sum n_i D_i / \sum n_i$ (which is the *number-mean diameter*). The Sauter mean is *cubic-over-squared* — remember the formula $\sum n_i D_i^3 / \sum n_i D_i^2$.
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean of sieve sizes** instead of geometric mean for the size of each fraction — this is a 1-mark deduction guaranteed.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Always express diameters in *meters* (not µm) when computing $S/V$ in m⁻¹. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for inconsistent units.
> 4. **Ignoring sphericity:** The specific surface area formula for a non-spherical particle is $\frac{6}{\phi_s D_p}$, not $\frac{6}{D_p}$.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert % mass to mass fractions** (divide by 100) before substitution.

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Topics for KTU 2024 Module 1 — "Size and Shape":**

- ✅ **Particle Size Definition:** Smallest sieve aperture OR volume-equivalent sphere diameter.
- ✅ **Particle Shape Definition:** Qualitative (visual) and quantitative (sphericity, aspect ratio).
- ✅ **Sphericity Formula:** $\phi_s = \pi D_v^2 / S_p$; range $(0, 1]$.
- ✅ **Sauter Mean Diameter:** $D_{sm} = \sum n_i D_i^3 / \sum n_i D_i^2$ — *most important formula in this module*.
- ✅ **Arithmetic Mean Diameter:** $\bar{D}_{am} = \sum n_i D_i / \sum n_i$ — simple, but not used in transfer correlations.
- ✅ **Geometric Mean Diameter:** Used in log-normal distributions of ground foods.
- ✅ **Mass-Mean Diameter:** Weighted mean using mass fractions — preferred when sieve data is in mass.
- ✅ **Specific Surface Area:** $S/V = 6 / (\phi_s D_p)$ — directly drives CIP cleaning time.
- ✅ **Porosity:** $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b / \rho_p$ — affects airflow, drying, and microbial penetration.
- ✅ **Sieving Procedure:** Arrange sieves in descending order, shake 10–15 min, weigh fractions.
- ✅ **Equivalent Diameter Types:** $D_v$, $D_s$, $D_{st}$, $D_A$, $D_a$ — know the property each is based on.
- ✅ **Aspect Ratio:** $AR = L/B$ — used for grains, cereals, and pasta shape classification.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Specific:** Always compute $D_{sm}$ on the *mass basis* using $\bar{D}_{vw} = \sum w_i / \sum (w_i / D_i)$.
- ✅ **Sanitation Linkage:** Smaller particles = higher $S/V$ = more cleaning time = more CIP cost.
- ✅ **Microbial Linkage:** Lower sphericity = more crevices = more microbial adhesion in food powders.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Mnemonic:** *"Size is for Sieve, Shape is for Sphere, Sauter is for Surface, and Sphericity is for Sanitation."* — Use this in the last 60 seconds before the KTU exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colour and Gloss
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT758 - FOOD SAFETY AND SANITATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Colour and Gloss

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Food Colour?

**Colour** is a psychophysical attribute of food quality that is perceived by the human visual system when light of a specific wavelength distribution interacts with a food matrix and is subsequently interpreted by the brain. According to the **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage)** standard, colour is a three-dimensional quantity defined by three independent variables: the spectral power distribution of the illuminant, the reflectance (or transmittance) characteristics of the object, and the spectral sensitivity of the observer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Colour is governed by the **Trivariance of Vision** — meaning three independent factors (Light Source + Object + Observer) must be specified to fully describe any colour.

In food science and technology, colour is considered one of the **primary sensory quality attributes** because it directly influences:
- Consumer perception of freshness
- Acceptance and purchase decisions
- Detection of spoilage or processing defects

### 1.2 What is Gloss?

**Gloss** is the optical property of a food surface that describes its ability to reflect light in a specular (mirror-like) direction. It is quantified as the **ratio of the luminous flux reflected from a surface at a specified angle to the incident luminous flux**, expressed in **Gloss Units (GU)**. The standard reference angle, as defined by **ASTM D523** and **ISO 2813**, is **60°**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Gloss (ASTM D523 Standard):**
> *"Gloss is the degree to which a surface's appearance reflects the specular component of light."*
> 
> Standard Reference: **Gloss = 100 GU at a polished black glass surface with refractive index 1.567**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mirror, Matte & Traffic Light" Model

Imagine standing in front of three different surfaces:

1. **A shiny new car (Glossy surface):** Light rays strike the surface and bounce off at exactly the same angle they came in — like a perfect mirror. This is **specular reflection**, and we say the surface has **high gloss**.

2. **A chalkboard (Matte surface):** Light rays scatter in all directions equally. There is no "shine" — only a uniform brightness. This is **diffuse reflection**, and the surface has **low gloss** (close to 0 GU).

3. **A traffic signal (Colour perception):** The light bulb emits white light. The red plastic lens absorbs blue and green wavelengths but reflects the red wavelengths back to your eye. The **observer's eye** then interprets this reflected light as "RED."

**Therefore:**
- **Gloss** = depends on the **angle** of reflection (geometry of surface).
- **Colour** = depends on the **wavelength** of reflection (spectral selectivity).

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Colour Perception

| Pillar | Role | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Light Source (Illuminant)** | Emits energy across wavelengths | D65 (Daylight), A (Tungsten), CWF (Cool White Fluorescent) |
| **Object (Food Matrix)** | Selectively absorbs/transmits/reflects | Tomato (absorbs green, reflects red), Milk (reflects all uniformly → white) |
| **Observer (Standard Eye)** | Converts light to nerve signals | 2° Standard Observer (CIE 1931), 10° Standard Observer (CIE 1964) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Illuminant D65** represents average noon daylight with a correlated colour temperature of approximately **6504 K** and is the **KTU-preferred reference illuminant** for food colour evaluation.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of Light:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- **Visible Wavelength Range:** $\lambda = 380 \text{ nm} - 780 \text{ nm}$
- **Standard Observer Reference Field:** 2° (small samples) or 10° (large samples, KTU preferred)
- **Standard Reference Gloss Angle:** **60°** (ASTM D523 / ISO 2813)
- **High-Gloss Surface:** GU > 70 at 60°
- **Matte Surface:** GU < 10 at 60°
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Colour Measurement

Colour measurement in food science relies on three principal colorimetric systems:

#### 2.1.1 The Munsell Colour System (Visual / Comparative)
Developed by **Albert H. Munsell (1905)**, it organises colour into three perceptual attributes:
- **Hue (H):** The qualitative attribute (red, yellow, green, blue, purple)
- **Value (V):** The lightness/darkness on a scale of 0 (black) to 10 (white)
- **Chroma (C):** The saturation or colourfulness from 0 (neutral grey) outward

**Logic Step 1:** Hue is arranged in a circular hue wheel (5 principal hues × 10 steps = 100 hues).
**Logic Step 2:** Value is arranged vertically (white at top, black at bottom).
**Logic Step 3:** Chroma is arranged radially outward from the neutral axis.

#### 2.1.2 The CIE XYZ Tristimulus System
The **CIE 1931 Standard** mathematically defines colour using three virtual primary stimuli:
- **X** — Red contribution
- **Y** — Green contribution (also represents luminance)
- **Z** — Blue contribution

These are computed by integrating the product of:
1. The illuminant spectral power distribution $S(\lambda)$
2. The object reflectance $R(\lambda)$
3. The observer colour-matching functions $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$

#### 2.1.3 The CIE L\*a\*b\* (CIELAB) System
The most widely adopted system in food science, providing **perceptually uniform** colour space:
- **L\*** — Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)
- **a\*** — Red/Green axis (+a\* = red, −a\* = green)
- **b\*** — Yellow/Blue axis (+b\* = yellow, −b\* = blue)

#### 2.1.4 The Hunter L, a, b System
An alternative rectangular colour space developed by **Richard S. Hunter (1942)**:
- **L** — Lightness (Hunter, different scale from L*)
- **a** — Red/Green axis
- **b** — Yellow/Blue axis

### 2.2 Theoretical Framework of Gloss Measurement

#### 2.2.1 Geometric Optics of Reflection
When incident light strikes a surface, two types of reflection occur:
1. **Specular Reflection** — Light reflects at an angle equal to the angle of incidence ($\theta_i = \theta_r$). Dominant in glossy surfaces.
2. **Diffuse Reflection** — Light scatters in all directions due to surface roughness. Dominant in matte surfaces.

#### 2.2.2 Classification of Gloss by Angle
- **High Gloss (60° Gloss > 70 GU):** Use **20° geometry** for better discrimination
- **Semi-Gloss (10–70 GU at 60°):** Use **60° geometry** (universal reference)
- **Matte (60° Gloss < 10 GU):** Use **85° geometry** for better differentiation

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Colour and Gloss

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet** — All critical formulas for the board examination are consolidated below. Memorise the constants and functional forms.

#### 2.3.1 CIE Tristimulus Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\, R(\lambda)\, \bar{x}(\lambda)\, d\lambda \\
Y &= k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\, R(\lambda)\, \bar{y}(\lambda)\, d\lambda \\
Z &= k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\, R(\lambda)\, \bar{z}(\lambda)\, d\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the normalising constant:
$$
k = \frac{100}{\int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\, \bar{y}(\lambda)\, d\lambda}
$$

#### 2.3.2 Conversion — XYZ to CIELAB

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^{*} &= 116 \cdot f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16 \\
a^{*} &= 500 \cdot \left[ f\!\left(\frac{X}{X_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) \right] \\
b^{*} &= 200 \cdot \left[ f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Z}{Z_n}\right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the non-linear transfer function:
$$
f(t) = \begin{cases} t^{1/3} & \text{if } t > \left(\frac{6}{29}\right)^{3} \\[4pt] \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{29}{6}\right)^{2} t + \frac{4}{29} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

And $X_n, Y_n, Z_n$ are tristimulus values of the reference white.

#### 2.3.3 Colour Difference — Total $\Delta E^*_{ab}$

$$
\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{(L^{*}_{2} - L^{*}_{1})^{2} + (a^{*}_{2} - a^{*}_{1})^{2} + (b^{*}_{2} - b^{*}_{1})^{2}}
$$

**Interpretation Table:**

| $\Delta E^{*}_{ab}$ Range | Perceived Difference |
|---|---|
| $< 1$ | Not perceptible to the human eye |
| $1 - 2$ | Perceptible only on close observation |
| $2 - 3.5$ | Clearly perceptible |
| $3.5 - 5$ | Noticeable difference |
| $> 5$ | Strong colour difference |

#### 2.3.4 Derived CIELAB Quantities

**Chroma (saturation):**
$$
C^{*} = \sqrt{(a^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{2}}
$$

**Hue angle:**
$$
h_{ab} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{b^{*}}{a^{*}}\right) \quad \text{(in degrees, } 0^{\circ} \text{ = red, } 90^{\circ} \text{ = yellow)}
$$

**Whiteness Index (WI, Berger):**
$$
WI = 100 - \sqrt{(100 - L^{*})^{2} + (a^{*})^{2} + (b^{*})^{2}}
$$

**Browning Index (BI):**
$$
BI = \frac{100 \cdot (x - 0.31)}{0.17}
$$
where:
$$
x = \frac{(a^{*} + 1.75 L^{*})}{(5.645 L^{*} + a^{*} - 3.012 b^{*})}
$$

#### 2.3.5 Gloss Formula

$$
G = \frac{\Phi_{s}}{\Phi_{i}} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $G$ = Gloss in **Gloss Units (GU)**
- $\Phi_{s}$ = Specularly reflected luminous flux
- $\Phi_{i}$ = Incident luminous flux

**Reference Calibration:** Polished black glass with refractive index $n_d = 1.567$ → $G = 100 \text{ GU}$

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Colour & Gloss Measurement

In the food industry, these systems are practically applied for:
1. **Quality Control:** Batch-to-batch colour consistency in tomato ketchup, edible oils, beverages
2. **Shelf-life Studies:** Tracking browning index of bread, fried products
3. **Process Optimisation:** Monitoring colour development in baking, frying, roasting
4. **Adulteration Detection:** Detecting colour deviations in saffron, turmeric, honey
5. **Packaging Selection:** Matching gloss of packaging films to brand standards
6. **Regulatory Compliance:** Meeting FSSAI colour specifications for synthetic dyes
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Computing CIELAB Coordinates from XYZ

**Problem Statement:** Given a food sample with measured tristimulus values $X = 65.20$, $Y = 70.80$, $Z = 55.40$, and reference white $X_n = 80.00$, $Y_n = 85.00$, $Z_n = 90.00$, compute the CIELAB coordinates $(L^{*}, a^{*}, b^{*})$.

**Step 1 — Compute the dimensionless ratios:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{X}{X_n} &= \frac{65.20}{80.00} = 0.8150 \\
\frac{Y}{Y_n} &= \frac{70.80}{85.00} = 0.8329 \\
\frac{Z}{Z_n} &= \frac{55.40}{90.00} = 0.6156
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the threshold condition:** Since all ratios $> (6/29)^{3} \approx 0.008856$, the cubic root branch applies.

**Step 3 — Compute $f(Y/Y_n)$ for $L^{*}$:**
$$
f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) = (0.8329)^{1/3} = 0.9410
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $f(X/X_n)$ for $a^{*}$:**
$$
f\!\left(\frac{X}{X_n}\right) = (0.8150)^{1/3} = 0.9341
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $f(Z/Z_n)$ for $b^{*}$:**
$$
f\!\left(\frac{Z}{Z_n}\right) = (0.6156)^{1/3} = 0.8497
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate $L^{*}$:**
$$
L^{*} = 116 \times 0.9410 - 16 = 109.16 - 16 = 93.16
$$

**Step 7 — Calculate $a^{*}$:**
$$
a^{*} = 500 \times (0.9341 - 0.9410) = 500 \times (-0.0069) = -3.45
$$

**Step 8 — Calculate $b^{*}$:**
$$
b^{*} = 200 \times (0.9410 - 0.8497) = 200 \times 0.0913 = 18.26
$$

**Final Result:** $(L^{*}, a^{*}, b^{*}) = (93.16, -3.45, 18.26)$

**Interpretation:** High $L^{*}$ indicates a light-coloured sample; negative $a^{*}$ indicates slight green tint; positive $b^{*}$ indicates a yellow tint — characteristic of a pale yellow food such as clarified butter or light honey.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Computing Total Colour Difference $\Delta E^{*}_{ab}$

**Problem Statement:** Two batches of mango pulp have the following CIELAB coordinates:
- Batch 1: $(L^{*}_{1}, a^{*}_{1}, b^{*}_{1}) = (68.5, 22.4, 58.6)$
- Batch 2: $(L^{*}_{2}, a^{*}_{2}, b^{*}_{2}) = (70.2, 24.8, 60.1)$

Compute $\Delta E^{*}_{ab}$ and interpret.

**Step 1 — Compute the difference vector:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L^{*} &= L^{*}_{2} - L^{*}_{1} = 70.2 - 68.5 = 1.7 \\
\Delta a^{*} &= a^{*}_{2} - a^{*}_{1} = 24.8 - 22.4 = 2.4 \\
\Delta b^{*} &= b^{*}_{2} - b^{*}_{1} = 60.1 - 58.6 = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Square each difference:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(\Delta L^{*})^{2} &= (1.7)^{2} = 2.89 \\
(\Delta a^{*})^{2} &= (2.4)^{2} = 5.76 \\
(\Delta b^{*})^{2} &= (1.5)^{2} = 2.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the squared differences:**
$$
\Sigma = 2.89 + 5.76 + 2.25 = 10.90
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root:**
$$
\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{10.90} = 3.30
$$

**Final Result:** $\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = 3.30$

**Interpretation:** This falls in the "noticeable difference" range (3.5–5 is clearly noticeable). Sensory panel would likely detect the difference, but it may be commercially acceptable.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Hue Angle and Chroma Computation

**Problem Statement:** A tomato paste sample has $L^{*} = 38.2$, $a^{*} = 32.5$, $b^{*} = 24.1$. Compute the chroma $(C^{*})$ and hue angle $(h_{ab})$.

**Step 1 — Compute $C^{*}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C^{*} &= \sqrt{(32.5)^{2} + (24.1)^{2}} \\
C^{*} &= \sqrt{1056.25 + 580.81} \\
C^{*} &= \sqrt{1637.06} = 40.46
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio $b^{*}/a^{*}$:**
$$
\frac{b^{*}}{a^{*}} = \frac{24.1}{32.5} = 0.7415
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $h_{ab}$:**
$$
h_{ab} = \tan^{-1}(0.7415) = 36.6^{\circ}
$$

**Final Result:** $C^{*} = 40.46$, $h_{ab} = 36.6^{\circ}$

**Interpretation:** Hue angle between 0° and 90° indicates the colour falls in the red-orange region — consistent with ripe tomato paste. High chroma confirms vivid colour saturation.

---

### 3.4 Worked Derivation 4: Gloss Unit Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A polished chocolate bar surface reflects $4.5 \text{ lm}$ of luminous flux when illuminated by a source producing $10.0 \text{ lm}$ of incident flux, measured at 60°. Calculate the gloss in GU.

**Step 1 — Apply the gloss formula:**
$$
G = \frac{\Phi_{s}}{\Phi_{i}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$
G = \frac{4.5}{10.0} \times 100 = 45 \text{ GU}
$$

**Final Result:** $G = 45 \text{ GU}$

**Interpretation:** Since $10 < G < 70$ at 60°, this is classified as a **semi-gloss surface** — typical of a tempered chocolate surface.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU Comparative Summary Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The following matrix is heavily tested in KTU exams. Master the contrasts.

| Parameter | Munsell System | CIE XYZ System | CIE L\*a\*b\* System | Hunter Lab System |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Empirical / Visual | Tristimulus (physical) | Perceptually uniform | Rectangular approximation |
| **Dimensions** | H, V, C | X, Y, Z | L\*, a\*, b\* | L, a, b |
| **Uniformity** | Non-uniform | Non-uniform | Approximately uniform | Non-uniform |
| **Primary Use** | Visual matching, atlas | Reference calculation | Food colour science | Industrial QC |
| **Year** | 1905 | 1931 | 1976 | 1942 |
| **White Point Required** | No | Yes ($X_n, Y_n, Z_n$) | Yes ($X_n, Y_n, Z_n$) | Yes |
| **Lightness Range** | 0–10 (Value) | Y (0–100) | L\* (0–100) | L (0–100) |

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Gloss Measurement Geometries

| Geometry Angle | Use Case | Application in Food |
|---|---|---|
| **20°** | High-gloss surfaces | Polished confectionery, glazed fruits |
| **45°** | Ceramic / paper | Less common in food |
| **60°** | Universal reference | Edible oil films, packaging |
| **75°** | Low-gloss surfaces | Matte coatings |
| **85°** | Very matte surfaces | Bread crust, porous snacks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Trivariance of Colour Perception

This diagram illustrates the three independent variables that must converge to produce a colour perception.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source D65] --> D[Colour Stimulus]
    B[Food Matrix Reflectance] --> D
    C[Standard Observer 2 deg 10 deg] --> D
    D --> E[Spectral Response]
    E --> F[Brain Interpretation]
    F --> G[Perceived Colour]

    node1[Illuminant Spectral Power Distribution] --> D
    node2[Food Spectral Reflectance] --> D
    node3[Observer Colour Matching Functions] --> D
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — CIELAB Workflow Pipeline

This shows the sequential pipeline from raw reflectance data to final CIELAB coordinates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Preparation] --> B[Spectrophotometer Measurement]
    B --> C[Raw Reflectance vs Wavelength]
    C --> D[Integration with Illuminant and Observer]
    D --> E[Tristimulus Values X Y Z]
    E --> F[Apply CIE L a b Equations]
    F --> G[Output L star a star b star]
    G --> H[Derive C star h ab WI BI]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Gloss Measurement Optical Setup

This diagram depicts the geometric arrangement of a typical glossmeter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source] --> B[Incident Beam at Theta I]
    B --> C[Sample Surface]
    C --> D[Specularly Reflected Beam at Theta R]
    D --> E[Detector at Angle]
    E --> F[Photocell Output]
    F --> G[Glossmeter Display in GU]

    node1[Reference Standard Polished Black Glass] -.-> G
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Classification of Optical Reflection Phenomena

This flowchart differentiates the two fundamental reflection modes responsible for gloss perception.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Light on Food Surface] --> B{Surface Roughness}
    B -->|Smooth less than lambda| C[Specular Reflection]
    B -->|Rough greater than lambda| D[Diffuse Reflection]
    C --> E[High Gloss Greater than 70 GU]
    D --> F[Low Gloss Matte Less than 10 GU]
    E --> G[Mirror Like Appearance]
    F --> H[Uniform Brightness]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Food Colour Decision Matrix

This is a functional block-level architecture mapping how colour decisions are made in a food industry QC lab.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receipt] --> B[Sample Sub sampling]
    B --> C[Calibrated Instrument Check]
    C --> D[Reference White Tile]
    D --> E[Measure Tristimulus XYZ]
    E --> F{Compute Colour Space}
    F --> G[CIELAB Output]
    F --> H[Hunter Lab Output]
    G --> I[Compute Delta E star]
    H --> J[Compute Browning Index]
    I --> K{Within Specification}
    J --> K
    K -->|Yes| L[Accept Batch]
    K -->|No| M[Reject or Reprocess]
```

### 4.6 Schematic Summary Table — Optical Instruments Used

| Instrument | Measures | Geometry / Principle | Food Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spectrophotometer** | Spectral reflectance | $380 \text{ nm} - 780 \text{ nm}$ scan | Tomato paste, ketchup |
| **Tristimulus Colorimeter** | Direct $X, Y, Z$ | Filter-based | Beverage QC |
| **Glossmeter** | Specular reflectance | 20° / 60° / 85° | Chocolate bars, packaging |
| **Haze Meter** | Transmission clarity | Integrating sphere | Edible oils, beverages |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Trivariance of Vision" as it applies to food colour measurement. List the three independent variables involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Trivariance of vision states that a colour can be fully specified only when three independent variables are defined simultaneously.

**Three Variables (1.5 marks):**
1. **Light Source (Illuminant)** — e.g., D65, A, CWF — provides the spectral energy distribution.
2. **Object (Food Sample)** — provides the spectral reflectance or transmittance.
3. **Observer (Standard Eye)** — the 2° or 10° standard observer defined by CIE, providing the colour-matching functions $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$.

Without specifying all three, any reported colour is **incomplete and ambiguous** — a critical KTU valuation point.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Gloss? Define Gloss Units (GU) and state the standard reference geometry used for gloss measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition of Gloss (1 mark):** Gloss is the optical property of a surface describing its ability to reflect light specularly (mirror-like). It is the ratio of specularly reflected luminous flux to the incident luminous flux.

**Gloss Units Definition (1 mark):** Gloss is expressed in **Gloss Units (GU)**, where $G = (\Phi_s / \Phi_i) \times 100$. A perfectly polished black glass with refractive index $1.567$ is calibrated to **100 GU**.

**Standard Reference Geometry (1 mark):** **60°** is the universal reference angle per **ASTM D523** and **ISO 2813**. For high-gloss surfaces, 20° is preferred; for matte surfaces, 85° is preferred.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified]`

**Attempt both parts:**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **CIE L\*a\*b\* colour space system** with proper mathematical equations. Describe the physical meaning of each coordinate and outline the procedure to compute $L^{*}, a^{*}, b^{*}$ from XYZ tristimulus values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A banana sample is measured under the D65 illuminant with a 10° standard observer, giving tristimulus values $X = 72.5$, $Y = 78.4$, $Z = 45.8$. The reference white has $X_n = 80.0$, $Y_n = 85.0$, $Z_n = 90.0$. Calculate the **CIELAB coordinates, Chroma ($C^{*}$), and Hue Angle ($h_{ab}$)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — CIE L\*a\*b\* System (7 marks)

**1. Introduction (2 marks):**
The CIE L\*a\*b\* (CIELAB) system was developed by the **Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage** in **1976**. It is a three-dimensional, approximately **perceptually uniform** colour space, meaning that equal numerical differences correspond approximately to equal perceived colour differences — a critical advantage for food quality control.

**2. The Three Coordinates (3 marks):**

| Coordinate | Range | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **L\*** | 0 (black) – 100 (white) | Lightness; independent of hue |
| **a\*** | -128 (green) – +127 (red) | Red-green opponent dimension |
| **b\*** | -128 (blue) – +127 (yellow) | Yellow-blue opponent dimension |

The opponent nature of $a^{*}$ and $b^{*}$ is biologically motivated by the **opponent process theory of vision** (Hering, 1878).

**3. Mathematical Equations (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L^{*} &= 116 \cdot f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16 \\
a^{*} &= 500 \cdot \left[ f\!\left(\frac{X}{X_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) \right] \\
b^{*} &= 200 \cdot \left[ f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Z}{Z_n}\right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The transfer function $f(t)$ incorporates a non-linear correction to mimic human vision's non-linear response.

**4. Computation Procedure (additional marking):**
**Step 1:** Measure spectral reflectance $R(\lambda)$ using a spectrophotometer.
**Step 2:** Compute $X, Y, Z$ via integration with illuminant and observer functions.
**Step 3:** Apply the conversion equations above using the appropriate white point $(X_n, Y_n, Z_n)$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
$X = 72.5$, $Y = 78.4$, $Z = 45.8$
$X_n = 80.0$, $Y_n = 85.0$, $Z_n = 90.0$

**Step 1 — Compute the ratios (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{X}{X_n} &= \frac{72.5}{80.0} = 0.9063 \\
\frac{Y}{Y_n} &= \frac{78.4}{85.0} = 0.9224 \\
\frac{Z}{Z_n} &= \frac{45.8}{90.0} = 0.5089
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply cube root transfer function (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(0.9063) &= (0.9063)^{1/3} = 0.9677 \\
f(0.9224) &= (0.9224)^{1/3} = 0.9732 \\
f(0.5089) &= (0.5089)^{1/3} = 0.7980
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $L^{*}$ (1 mark):**
$$
L^{*} = 116 \times 0.9732 - 16 = 112.89 - 16 = 96.89
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $a^{*}$ (1 mark):**
$$
a^{*} = 500 \times (0.9677 - 0.9732) = 500 \times (-0.0055) = -2.75
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $b^{*}$ (1 mark):**
$$
b^{*} = 200 \times (0.9732 - 0.7980) = 200 \times 0.1752 = 35.04
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Chroma $C^{*}$ (0.5 marks):**
$$
C^{*} = \sqrt{(-2.75)^{2} + (35.04)^{2}} = \sqrt{7.56 + 1227.80} = \sqrt{1235.36} = 35.15
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Hue Angle $h_{ab}$ (0.5 marks):**
$$
h_{ab} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{35.04}{-2.75}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-12.74) = -85.51^{\circ}
$$

*Note: Since $a^{*} < 0$ and $b^{*} > 0$, the true hue angle lies in the second quadrant and should be reported as* $h_{ab} = 180^{\circ} - 85.51^{\circ} = 94.49^{\circ}$.

**Final Answer:** $L^{*} = 96.89$, $a^{*} = -2.75$, $b^{*} = 35.04$, $C^{*} = 35.15$, $h_{ab} \approx 94.5^{\circ}$.

**Interpretation:** The hue angle near $90°$ and high $b^{*}$ confirm a yellow colour, consistent with ripe banana pulp.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]`

**Attempt both parts:**

**(a)** Differentiate between **specular and diffuse reflection**. With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **glossmeter**, and describe the role of the **three standard measurement geometries** (20°, 60°, 85°). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food scientist evaluates the gloss of three packaging films A, B, and C at 60°. The incident flux is $8.0 \text{ lm}$ for each sample. The specularly reflected fluxes are $6.8 \text{ lm}$, $3.2 \text{ lm}$, and $0.6 \text{ lm}$, respectively. **(7 marks)**

Compute the gloss of each film in GU and classify them. Discuss which geometry (20° or 85°) would provide better discrimination between the lowest and highest gloss films.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Specular vs Diffuse & Glossmeter Principle (7 marks)

**1. Specular Reflection (2 marks):**
Occurs when light strikes a **smooth surface** (roughness $<< \lambda$) and reflects at an angle equal to the angle of incidence, following the **law of reflection**: $\theta_i = \theta_r$. All reflected rays are parallel — characteristic of glossy surfaces.

**2. Diffuse Reflection (2 marks):**
Occurs when light strikes a **rough surface** (roughness $>> \lambda$) and scatters in **all directions** uniformly, following **Lambert's cosine law**. The surface appears equally bright from all viewing angles — characteristic of matte surfaces.

**3. Glossmeter Working Principle (2 marks):**
A **glossmeter** consists of:
- A **light source** producing a beam at fixed geometry $\theta_i$
- A **sample holder**
- A **photodetector** positioned at the specular angle $\theta_r = \theta_i$
- A **calibration standard** (polished black glass, $n_d = 1.567$, $G = 100 \text{ GU}$)

The gloss value is computed as the ratio of detector response to that of the calibrated standard.

**4. Standard Geometries (1 mark):**
- **20°** → High-gloss surfaces (e.g., polished confectionery)
- **60°** → Universal reference geometry
- **85°** → Matte / very-low-gloss surfaces (e.g., bread crust)

---

#### Part (b) — Gloss Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
$\Phi_i = 8.0 \text{ lm}$ (constant)
$\Phi_s$ (A) = $6.8 \text{ lm}$
$\Phi_s$ (B) = $3.2 \text{ lm}$
$\Phi_s$ (C) = $0.6 \text{ lm}$

**Step 1 — Compute Gloss for Film A (1.5 marks):**
$$
G_A = \frac{6.8}{8.0} \times 100 = 85.0 \text{ GU}
$$
Classification: **High gloss** ($G > 70$).

**Step 2 — Compute Gloss for Film B (1.5 marks):**
$$
G_B = \frac{3.2}{8.0} \times 100 = 40.0 \text{ GU}
$$
Classification: **Semi-gloss** ($10 < G < 70$).

**Step 3 — Compute Gloss for Film C (1.5 marks):**
$$
G_C = \frac{0.6}{8.0} \times 100 = 7.5 \text{ GU}
$$
Classification: **Matte** ($G < 10$).

**Step 4 — Geometry Selection (2.5 marks):**
The 60° geometry is the universal reference but provides limited discrimination between very high and very low gloss surfaces. To improve discrimination:
- Between Film A ($85 \text{ GU}$) and Film C ($7.5 \text{ GU}$), the **20° geometry** would better resolve the high-gloss range, providing **better discrimination** between Film A and a hypothetical 100 GU standard.
- The **85° geometry** would better resolve the low-gloss range, providing **better discrimination** between Film C and a hypothetical 0 GU standard.

Since Film A has $G > 70$ at 60°, it falls in the high-gloss category — the **20° geometry** is recommended for more precise measurement of Film A. To compare Film C, the **85° geometry** is preferred. Thus, **neither single geometry perfectly spans the entire range**; multiple geometries should be used for full characterisation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the $\times 100$ multiplier** in the gloss formula. Marks deducted if you write $G = \Phi_s / \Phi_i$ without converting to GU.
> 2. **Confusing $L$ with $L^{*}$** — Hunter's L and CIELAB's L\* use different formulas. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not specifying the white point** $X_n, Y_n, Z_n$ when computing CIELAB — 1 mark penalty.
> 4. **Sign errors in hue angle quadrant correction** — when $a^* < 0$, the hue angle must be adjusted by $180°$.
> 5. **Using the wrong geometry angle** for a given gloss range — glossmeters require geometry-appropriate calibration.
> 6. **Forgetting units** — GU for gloss, dimensionless for $L^*, a^*, b^*$, degrees for hue angle.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Colour and Gloss**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Colour requires **three independent variables**: Illuminant, Object, Observer (Trivariance)
- ☐ **Standard Illuminant D65** = average noon daylight (6504 K), KTU-preferred
- ☐ **CIE Standard Observers**: 2° (CIE 1931) and 10° (CIE 1964)
- ☐ **Visible spectrum**: $380 \text{ nm}$ (violet) – $780 \text{ nm}$ (red)

**Colorimetric Systems:**
- ☐ **Munsell** — Hue, Value, Chroma — visual / atlas-based
- ☐ **CIE XYZ** — Tristimulus, non-uniform — basis for all conversions
- ☐ **CIELAB** — Perceptually uniform — most used in food science
- ☐ **Hunter Lab** — Industrial alternative, non-uniform

**Critical Formulas:**
- ☐ $L^* = 116 f(Y/Y_n) - 16$
- ☐ $a^* = 500 [f(X/X_n) - f(Y/Y_n)]$
- ☐ $b^* = 200 [f(Y/Y_n) - f(Z/Z_n)]$
- ☐ $\Delta E^*_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$
- ☐ $C^* = \sqrt{(a^*)^2 + (b^*)^2}$
- ☐ $h_{ab} = \tan^{-1}(b^*/a^*)$
- ☐ $G = (\Phi_s/\Phi_i) \times 100$ in GU

**Derived Indices:**
- ☐ **Whiteness Index (WI)** — white foods (rice, sugar, milk powder)
- ☐ **Browning Index (BI)** — thermal processing (bread crust, fried foods)
- ☐ **Hue Angle** — quadrant correction required

**Gloss:**
- ☐ **Specular reflection** → glossy; **Diffuse reflection** → matte
- ☐ **ASTM D523 / ISO 2813** standards
- ☐ **Standard geometry: 60°**; High gloss → 20°; Matte → 85°
- ☐ **Calibration reference**: Polished black glass, $n_d = 1.567$, $G = 100 \text{ GU}$

**Practical Interpretation:**
- ☐ $\Delta E^*_{ab} < 1$ → not perceptible
- ☐ $\Delta E^*_{ab} > 5$ → strong difference
- ☐ $h_{ab} \approx 0°$ → red; $90°$ → yellow; $180°$ → green; $270°$ → blue

**Industrial Applications:**
- ☐ QC of ketchup, edible oil, beverages, saffron, turmeric
- ☐ Shelf-life studies via BI tracking
- ☐ Packaging gloss matching to brand standards
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Texture - Visual and Objectively Measurable Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT758 - FOOD SAFETY AND SANITATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Texture - Visual and Objectively Measurable Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Food Texture?

**Texture** is one of the most important sensory attributes of food, defined as the manifestation of the **structural and mechanical properties** of a food product, perceived through **sight, touch (kinesthetic), and sound**. It encompasses everything our senses detect about how a food feels in the mouth, how it deforms, how it fractures, and how it flows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (ISO 5492:2008 aligned):**
> *"Texture is the totality of those mechanical, geometrical, and surface attributes of a product perceptible by means of mechanical, tactile, and visual receptors."*

Texture is broadly classified into two major evaluation domains:

| Domain | Mode of Evaluation | Tools Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sensory Texture** | Perceived by human senses (mouthfeel, handfeel, visual cues) | Trained human panel |
| **Objective/Instrumental Texture** | Measured by calibrated instruments | Texture Analyzer, Viscometer, Rheometer |

---

## 1.2 Visual Attributes of Texture

Visual texture attributes are perceived **without physical contact** with the food. They rely on the way light interacts with the food's surface and the structural patterns visible to the eye.

### 🔹 Key Visual Texture Attributes:

1. **Surface Appearance** – smoothness, glossiness, dullness, or roughness
2. **Shape & Form** – geometric regularity (round, irregular, fibrous, granular)
3. **Structural Patterns** – porous (bread), crystalline (sugar), fibrous (meat), layered (pastry)
4. **Color Uniformity** – mottling, streaks, spots indicating heterogeneity
5. **Defect Indicators** – cracks, wrinkles, lumps, air pockets

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine biting into a fresh apple. *Before* you even touch it, you can *predict* its crunchiness from the shiny, taut skin and the firm, rounded shape. The brain reads **visual cues** as a proxy for textural expectation. This is the power of **visual texture** — it sets up the expectation *before* mastication begins.

---

## 1.3 Objectively Measurable Attributes

These are **instrument-based, quantitative** measurements of textural properties. They replace subjective human perception with **numerical, reproducible** data — essential for **Quality Control (QC)**, **R&D**, and **regulatory compliance** in food industries.

### 🔹 Primary Objective Texture Parameters (from Texture Profile Analysis - TPA):

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Definition (Simple) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hardness** | $H$ | $\text{N}$ or $\text{g}$ | Force required to compress the food |
| **Cohesiveness** | $C$ | Dimensionless | How well the food holds together |
| **Adhesiveness** | $A$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{s}$ | Sticky/adhering nature |
| **Springiness** | $S$ | $\text{m}$ or $\vert$ | Ability to recover shape after compression |
| **Gumminess** | $G$ | $\text{N}$ | Energy needed to disintegrate a semi-solid |
| **Chewiness** | $Ch$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ | Energy to chew a solid food |
| **Resilience** | $R$ | Dimensionless | Speed of recovery from deformation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Relevance:** Objective texture measurement ensures **batch-to-batch consistency** in manufactured foods, which directly impacts **consumer satisfaction**, **shelf-life prediction**, and **regulatory compliance** with standards like FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius, and ISO.

---

## 1.4 Why Both Visual AND Objective Attributes Matter

In modern food quality assurance (KTU Module 1 emphasis), both attribute types work together:

- **Visual attributes** drive **first-impression consumer acceptance** (the "buy" decision)
- **Objective attributes** drive **product specification compliance** (the "trust" decision)

Together, they form the **Quality Triangle** of food texture: **Sensory ↔ Instrumental ↔ Consumer Acceptance**.

---

## 1.5 Standard Units and Reference Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Values (Industry Standards):**
> - **Standard test speed (TPA):** $1 \text{ mm/s}$ (compression) and $0.5 \text{ mm/s}$ (withdrawal)
> - **Standard strain:** $30\% - 75\%$ of original sample height
> - **Standard probe geometry:** $\text{Cylinder}, \text{Spherical}, \text{Conical}$
> - **International standard:** **ISO 11036:1994** (Sensory analysis — Texture profile)
> - **ASTM Standards:** $\text{F36}$ (compressibility), $\text{F2600}$ (puncture resistance)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Framework of Texture Measurement

Food texture is a **multi-dimensional property** that emerges from the **microstructure** of food — its cellular arrangement, moisture content, fat distribution, protein network, and air voids. When external force is applied, the food responds with measurable mechanical behavior.

### 🔹 The Three Pillars of Texture Science:

**Pillar 1 — Mechanical (Force-Based) Properties**
- Hardness, Adhesiveness, Cohesiveness, Gumminess, Chewiness
- Measured by applying a controlled **force** and recording **deformation response**

**Pillar 2 — Geometrical (Shape-Based) Properties**
- Particle size, shape, orientation, surface roughness
- Measured via **image analysis** or **direct geometric measurement**

**Pillar 3 — Surface (Topographical) Properties**
- Smoothness, moisture perception, oiliness, grittiness
- Measured via **tribology** and **surface profilometry**

---

## 2.2 The Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) Theory

The TPA is the **gold standard** of objective texture measurement, developed by **Szczesniak (1963)** and later refined by **Bourne (1978)**. It simulates **two bites** of a human chewing process by performing **two successive compression cycles** on a food sample.

### 🔹 Step-by-Step Logic of a TPA Test:

1. **Sample Loading** — A food sample of known dimensions is placed on the platform
2. **First Compression** — Probe descends at constant speed, compresses sample to a target strain (typically **30% – 75%**)
3. **Probe Withdrawal** — Probe retracts; adhesiveness is measured from the negative force area
4. **Rest Period** — A defined pause (typically **5 s**) allows partial recovery
5. **Second Compression** — Probe descends again; springiness and resilience are measured
6. **Data Acquisition** — Force-time (or force-distance) curve is generated and analyzed

---

## 2.3 The Seven Classical TPA Parameters — Detailed

### (i) Hardness
- **Definition:** Peak force during the **first compression** cycle
- **Mathematical expression:** $H = F_{\text{peak},1}$

### (ii) Cohesiveness
- **Definition:** Ratio of the **positive force area** during the second compression to that of the first
- **Mathematical expression:** $C = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$
- **Interpretation:** $C \to 1$ means highly cohesive; $C \to 0$ means crumbly/fragile

### (iii) Adhesiveness
- **Definition:** Negative force area representing the work required to **pull the probe away** from the sample
- **Mathematical expression:** $A_{\text{adhesion}} = \int_{t_{\text{withdraw}}} F_{\text{negative}} \, dt$

### (iv) Springiness
- **Definition:** Distance recovered by the sample between the end of first compression and the start of second compression
- **Mathematical expression:** $S = \dfrac{d_{\text{end,1}}}{d_{\text{end,1}}} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_0}$

### (v) Gumminess
- **Mathematical expression:** $G = H \times C$

### (vi) Chewiness
- **Mathematical expression:** $Ch = G \times S = H \times C \times S$

### (vii) Resilience
- **Mathematical expression:** $R = \dfrac{A_{1f}}{A_{1i}}$
- (Ratio of upstroke area to downstroke area in first compression)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it appears in nearly every KTU exam paper!**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Food Example |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Hardness ($H$) | $H = F_{\text{peak,1}}$ | $\text{N}$ | Biscuit, Carrot |
| 2 | Cohesiveness ($C$) | $C = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | dimensionless | Cheese, Jelly |
| 3 | Adhesiveness ($A$) | $A = \int F_{\text{neg}} \, dt$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{s}$ | Peanut Butter, Honey |
| 4 | Springiness ($S$) | $S = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_0}$ | $\text{m}$ or $\vert$ | Sponge Cake, Marshmallow |
| 5 | Gumminess ($G$) | $G = H \times C$ | $\text{N}$ | Gummy Candy, Cheese |
| 6 | Chewiness ($Ch$) | $Ch = H \times C \times S$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ | Steak, Toffee |
| 7 | Resilience ($R$) | $R = \dfrac{A_{1f}}{A_{1i}}$ | dimensionless | Bread Crust |
| 8 | Fracturability ($Fr$) | $Fr = F_{\text{first peak}}$ | $\text{N}$ | Chips, Crackers |
| 9 | Shear Stress ($\tau$) | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Meat tenderness |
| 10 | Strain ($\varepsilon$) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | dimensionless | All foods |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Food Industry

| Application Area | Texture Parameter Used | Industry Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bakery** | Hardness, Springiness | Bread freshness testing |
| **Dairy** | Hardness, Cohesiveness, Adhesiveness | Cheese, Yogurt, Ice cream grading |
| **Confectionery** | Hardness, Gumminess, Chewiness | Candy quality control |
| **Meat & Poultry** | Shear Force (Warner-Bratzler) | Tenderness grading |
| **Fruits & Vegetables** | Firmness, Puncture Force | Ripeness and harvest timing |
| **Beverages** | Viscosity, Flow Index | Juice, Sauce rheology |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Texture Force Equation

### 🔹 Problem Setup:
A food sample of height $L_0$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is compressed by a probe. We want to relate the **applied force** $F$ to the resulting **deformation** $\Delta L$, deriving the **engineering stress and strain** relationship.

---

### Step 1: Define the Geometric Quantities

Let:
- Original sample height: $L_0$ (m)
- Cross-sectional area: $A$ (m²)
- Force applied: $F$ (N)
- Deformation (change in height): $\Delta L$ (m)

### Step 2: Define Engineering Strain

The **engineering strain** is the fractional change in length:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}
$$

**Explanation:** Strain is dimensionless. A sample of height $10 \text{ mm}$ compressed by $2 \text{ mm}$ has a strain of $0.2$ (or $20\%$).

### Step 3: Define Engineering Stress

The **engineering stress** is the force per unit cross-sectional area:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}
$$

**Explanation:** Stress has units of Pascals ($\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$).

### Step 4: Derive the Linear Elastic Region (Hooke's Law for Foods)

For **low strain** (typically $\varepsilon < 0.05$), many foods behave as **Hookean elastic solids** in a linear region:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

where $E$ is the **apparent modulus of elasticity** (also called the **Young's modulus** for foods).

**Explanation:** Substituting the definitions:

$$
\dfrac{F}{A} = E \cdot \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}
$$

### Step 5: Solve for Force as a Function of Deformation

$$
F = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{L_0} \cdot \Delta L
$$

**Explanation:** This shows that in the linear region, the force is **directly proportional** to deformation. The slope of the $F$ vs. $\Delta L$ plot equals $\dfrac{E \cdot A}{L_0}$.

### Step 6: Generalize to the Stiffness Equation

$$
k = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{L_0}
$$

where $k$ is the **stiffness** ($\text{N/m}$). Therefore:

$$
F = k \cdot \Delta L
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Work Done in Compression (Energy-Based Parameters)

### Step 1: Define Work (Energy)

Work done in compressing a food sample is the **area under the force-deformation curve**:

$$
W = \int_{0}^{\Delta L_{\max}} F(\Delta L) \, d(\Delta L)
$$

### Step 2: For a Linear Region

In the linear Hookean region, $F = k \cdot \Delta L$:

$$
W = \int_{0}^{\Delta L_{\max}} k \cdot \Delta L \, d(\Delta L)
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Integral

$$
W = k \cdot \left[ \dfrac{(\Delta L)^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{\Delta L_{\max}}
$$

$$
W = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot (\Delta L_{\max})^2
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $k = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{L_0}$

$$
W = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{E \cdot A}{L_0} \cdot (\Delta L_{\max})^2
$$

**Explanation:** This is the **energy stored** in the food during compression. It is directly measurable from the **positive area** $A_1$ of the TPA force-time curve and forms the basis for **chewiness** calculations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Chewiness from Primary Parameters

### Step 1: Start from Definitions

- $H$ = Hardness (peak force)
- $C$ = Cohesiveness (dimensionless)
- $S$ = Springiness (dimensionless ratio of distances)

### Step 2: Define Gumminess

Gumminess is the energy required to disintegrate a **semi-solid** food to a steady state of swallowing:

$$
G = H \times C
$$

### Step 3: Define Chewiness

Chewiness is the energy required to chew a **solid** food to a state ready for swallowing:

$$
Ch = G \times S
$$

### Step 4: Expand the Chewiness Equation

$$
Ch = H \times C \times S
$$

### Step 5: Dimensional Analysis

- $[H] = \text{N}$
- $[C] = \text{dimensionless}$
- $[S] = \text{m}$

Therefore:

$$
[Ch] = \text{N} \cdot \text{m} = \text{Joule (energy)}
$$

This confirms that chewiness has the **correct dimensions of energy**, as expected.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Problem (KTU Exam Style):**
> A cylindrical food sample ($L_0 = 20 \text{ mm}$, $A = 314 \text{ mm}^2$) was subjected to a TPA test. The data obtained is:
> - Peak Force in 1st compression: $F_{\text{peak}} = 45 \text{ N}$
> - Positive area in 1st compression: $A_1 = 320 \text{ N}\cdot\text{mm}$
> - Positive area in 2nd compression: $A_2 = 256 \text{ N}\cdot\text{mm}$
> - Distance at end of 1st compression: $d_1 = 6.0 \text{ mm}$
> - Distance at start of 2nd compression: $d_2 = 4.0 \text{ mm}$
> - Springiness distance: $d_0 = 8.0 \text{ mm}$

### Solution:

**Hardness:** $H = F_{\text{peak}} = 45 \text{ N}$

**Cohesiveness:** $C = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{256}{320} = 0.80$

**Springiness:** $S = \dfrac{d_1}{d_0} = \dfrac{6.0}{8.0} = 0.75$

**Gumminess:** $G = H \times C = 45 \times 0.80 = 36 \text{ N}$

**Chewiness:** $Ch = G \times S = 36 \times 0.75 = 27 \text{ N}\cdot\text{mm}$

**Strain at first compression peak:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{20 - 6}{20} = 0.70 = 70\%$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

This diagram shows the operational flow of a Texture Profile Analysis on a Texture Analyzer instrument.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### **Q1. Define the term "food texture" as per ISO 5492:2008. List any four visual attributes of food texture with one food example each.**

**Model Answer:**

According to **ISO 5492:2008**, food texture is defined as *"the totality of those mechanical, geometrical, and surface attributes of a product perceptible by means of mechanical, tactile, and visual receptors."*

**Four visual attributes with examples:**

| Attribute | Description | Food Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Smoothness | Even, polished surface | Chocolate bar |
| Granularity | Presence of visible particles | Granola, Sugar crystals |
| Porosity | Visible air pockets | Bread slice |
| Fibrousness | Visible parallel strands | Cooked chicken breast |

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between sensory texture evaluation and objective texture measurement. State two advantages of objective methods.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sensory Evaluation | Objective Measurement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Evaluator** | Trained human panel | Calibrated instrument |
| **Output** | Descriptive, qualitative | Numerical, quantitative |
| **Reproducibility** | Subject to human variability | Highly reproducible |
| **Cost** | Lower initial, higher per test | Higher initial, lower per test |
| **Standardization** | Difficult | Easy (ISO/ASTM standards) |

**Two advantages of objective methods:**
1. **Reproducibility** — Same sample gives same result across labs
2. **Quantitative** — Provides numerical values for QC specifications and shelf-life modeling

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**a)** Explain the principle of Texture Profile Analysis (TPA). With a neat labeled diagram of the force-time curve, describe the determination of **Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Adhesiveness, Gumminess, and Chewiness**. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A TPA test on a cheese sample yields the following data: $F_{\text{peak}} = 28 \text{ N}$, $A_1 = 180 \text{ N}\cdot\text{mm}$, $A_2 = 126 \text{ N}\cdot\text{mm}$, $d_0 = 6.0 \text{ mm}$, $d_1 = 4.5 \text{ mm}$. Calculate Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, and Chewiness. Comment on the texture quality. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (a):**

**Principle of TPA:** Texture Profile Analysis simulates **two successive bites** of human mastication by performing **two compression cycles** on a food sample using a calibrated Texture Analyzer. The force-time curve generated provides quantitative data for seven textural parameters.

**Procedure:**
1. Sample of known dimensions is placed on the instrument platform
2. Probe descends at a constant test speed (typically $1 \text{ mm/s}$)
3. First compression compresses sample to a target strain (30% – 75%)
4. Probe retracts; negative force area gives adhesiveness
5. After a 5-second rest, second compression is performed
6. The resulting force-time curve is analyzed for TPA parameters

**Force-Time Curve Labeled Diagram:**

```
Force (N)
   |
   |    *H (Peak 1)
   |   /\
   |  /  \           *H' (Peak 2)
   | /    \         /\
   |/      \       /  \
   |        \     /    \    /  Time
   |  A1  ___\___/      \__/__ →
   |       ↑   ↑    A2
   |       ↑   ↑
   |      First  Second
   |      Compress  Compress
   |__________|_________|____
              ↑  ←  Adhesiveness
              |    (negative area)
              ← Springiness recovery →
```

**Parameter Determination:**

- **Hardness ($H$):** Peak force in the first compression = $F_{\text{peak,1}}$
- **Cohesiveness ($C$):** $C = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ (ratio of positive areas)
- **Adhesiveness ($A$):** Magnitude of negative force area during probe withdrawal
- **Springiness ($S$):** $S = \dfrac{\text{Recovered distance}}{\text{Original compression distance}}$
- **Gumminess ($G$):** $G = H \times C$
- **Chewiness ($Ch$):** $Ch = H \times C \times S$

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $F_{\text{peak}} = 28 \text{ N}$
- $A_1 = 180 \text{ N}\cdot\text{mm}$
- $A_2 = 126 \text{ N}\cdot\text{mm}$
- $d_0 = 6.0 \text{ mm}$ (original compression distance)
- $d_1 = 4.5 \text{ mm}$ (recovered distance)

**Step 1: Hardness**

$$
H = F_{\text{peak}} = 28 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Cohesiveness**

$$
C = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{126}{180} = 0.70
$$

**Step 3: Springiness**

$$
S = \dfrac{d_1}{d_0} = \dfrac{4.5}{6.0} = 0.75
$$

**Step 4: Gumminess**

$$
G = H \times C = 28 \times 0.70 = 19.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5: Chewiness**

$$
Ch = G \times S = 19.6 \times 0.75 = 14.7 \text{ N}\cdot\text{mm}
$$

**Comment on Texture Quality:**
- $H = 28 \text{ N}$ → Moderately hard cheese
- $C = 0.70$ → Good internal cohesion (does not crumble)
- $S = 0.75$ → Excellent springiness (rubbery, elastic)
- $G = 19.6 \text{ N}$ → Moderately gummy
- $Ch = 14.7 \text{ N}\cdot\text{mm}$ → Moderate chewiness

**Conclusion:** The cheese exhibits **good body, elasticity, and structural integrity**, suitable for a semi-hard variety (e.g., Mozzarella or Cheddar).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**a)** Discuss in detail the **visual attributes of food texture** with suitable examples. Explain how these attributes influence consumer purchase decisions. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Explain the working principle of a **Texture Analyzer** with a labeled block diagram. List the different probes used and state their applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (a):**

**Visual Texture Attributes** are perceived through **sight only**, without physical contact. They play a critical role in **consumer first-impression** evaluation at the point of purchase.

**Detailed Discussion:**

1. **Surface Appearance** — The way the food surface reflects light indicates its textural nature. Glossy → likely moist/oily (chocolate, glazed donut). Matte → likely dry (biscuit, dry pasta).

2. **Shape and Form** — Geometric regularity tells the brain about structural integrity. Round, smooth apple → firm and crunchy. Wrinkled, shriveled prune → soft, chewy, dehydrated.

3. **Structural Patterns** — Visible microstructures suggest texture:
   - **Porous:** Bread, sponge cake (soft, airy)
   - **Fibrous:** Meat, asparagus (chewy)
   - **Layered:** Puff pastry, croissant (flaky)
   - **Crystalline:** Sugar, salt (granular, hard)

4. **Color Uniformity** — Mottling, streaks, or uneven color suggest heterogeneous texture (e.g., marbled meat, swirled ice cream).

5. **Defects** — Cracks, lumps, and air pockets signal textural defects that reduce consumer acceptance.

**Influence on Consumer Decisions:**
- Studies show **70% of purchase decisions** for fresh produce are made based on visual cues
- Visual texture creates **expectation** of mouthfeel; mismatch leads to dissatisfaction
- Color-shapes-texture combinations (e.g., golden-brown = crispy) are used in **food packaging design** to influence perception

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (b):**

**Working Principle of a Texture Analyzer:**

A Texture Analyzer is a **mechanical testing instrument** that applies a controlled force or deformation to a food sample and measures the resulting response. It essentially replicates human biting, chewing, cutting, or piercing actions.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Load Cell Force Sensor] --> N2[Signal Amplifier]
    N2 --> N3[Analog to Digital Converter]
    N3 --> N4[Microprocessor and Data Logger]
    N4 --> N5[Computer with Software]
    N5 --> N6[Display of Force Time Curve]

    M1[Motor Drive System] --> M2[Crosshead / Probe Holder]
    M2 --> M3[Probe Contacts Sample]
    M3 --> N1

    S1[Sample on Platform] --> M3

    style N1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style N4 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style M1 fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style S1 fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
```

**Different Probes and Applications:**

| Probe Type | Geometry | Application | Food Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cylindrical (Flat)** | Flat disc | Compression, TPA | Cheese, Gel, Bread |
| **Spherical (Ball)** | Round ball | Puncture, penetration | Fruits, Vegetables |
| **Conical** | Cone-shaped | Penetration of soft samples | Yogurt, Pudding |
| **Warner-Bratzler Blade** | V-shaped blade | Shear test | Meat tenderness |
| **Kramer Shear Cell** | Multi-blade | Multi-sample shear | Vegetables, Snacks |
| **Wire Cutter / Blade** | Thin blade | Cutting test | Biscuit, Pastry |
| **Needle Probe** | Thin needle | Penetration of soft solids | Butter, Margarine |
| **Three-Point Bend Rig** | Two supports + load nose | Bending fracture | Snack bars, Sticks |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST KTU STUDENTS MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Springiness with Elasticity** — Springiness is the *recovered distance* ratio, not the engineering modulus of elasticity. Do not mix them up.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to use AREAS for Cohesiveness** — Cohesiveness is $A_2 / A_1$, **NOT** $F_2 / F_1$ (force ratio). This is the most common error.
>
> 3. **Missing units in final answers** — Always state the unit (N, N·mm, dimensionless) explicitly.
>
> 4. **Writing "Gumminess = Hardness × Springiness"** — WRONG! Correct: $G = H \times C$. Chewiness is the one with three factors.
>
> 5. **Not drawing the force-time curve** — In TPA questions worth 7 marks, **a labeled diagram is mandatory**. Skipping the diagram can cost **2–3 marks** easily.
>
> 6. **Mixing visual with sensory texture** — Visual = sight only. Sensory/Mouthfeel = tactile + taste. Keep them separate in your answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

- **Texture** = Mechanical + Geometrical + Surface attributes (ISO 5492:2008)
- **Visual attributes** are perceived **without contact** (sight only)
- **Objective attributes** are measured by **instruments** (quantitative, reproducible)
- **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** is the gold standard for objective measurement
- **TPA uses TWO compression cycles** with a 5-second rest
- **Seven TPA Parameters:** $H, C, A, S, G, Ch, R$
- **Key Equations:**
  - $C = A_2 / A_1$
  - $G = H \times C$
  - $Ch = H \times C \times S$
  - $R = A_{1f} / A_{1i}$
  - $\sigma = F / A$
  - $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$
  - $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ (linear region)
- **Common Probes:** Cylindrical, Spherical, Conical, Warner-Bratzler, Kramer Shear Cell
- **Standards:** ISO 5492:2008, ISO 11036:1994, ASTM F36, F2600
- **Hardness units:** Newtons (N)
- **Cohesiveness & Resilience:** Dimensionless
- **Gumminess units:** Newtons (N)
- **Chewiness units:** N·mm (energy)
- **Springiness:** Ratio of distances (no unit, expressed as decimal or percentage)
- **Engineering stress:** $\sigma = F/A$ in Pascals (Pa)
- **Engineering strain:** $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ (dimensionless)
- **Visual cues drive purchase decisions**; **objective data drives quality control**
- **Always draw the force-time curve** in TPA-based exam questions
- **Most common error in exam:** Confusing $A_2/A_1$ with $F_2/F_1$ for cohesiveness

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aroma of Foods - Introductory Ideas, Formation and Chemistry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT758 - FOOD SAFETY AND SANITATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aroma of Foods - Introductory Ideas, Formation and Chemistry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Aroma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aroma (Odor):** Aroma is the perception of volatile chemical compounds by the **olfactory receptor neurons** located in the **olfactory epithelium** of the nasal cavity, occurring either orthonasally (through the nostrils during sniffing) or retronasally (through the back of the throat during chewing and swallowing). It is a **chemoreception event** triggered by molecules with molecular weights typically in the range of **30–300 Da** and vapor pressures sufficient to exist in the gaseous phase at ambient or body temperature (**~37 °C**).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, aroma is regarded as a **sub-modality of flavor**, distinct from the basic tastes (sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami) which are detected by **taste buds (gustatory receptors)** on the tongue. While taste provides only **5 qualitative sensations**, aroma delivers thousands of distinct odor notes — this is why a strawberry and a raspberry "taste different" even though both are predominantly sweet.

## 1.2 Distinguishing Aroma, Flavor, and Taste

| Sensory Modality | Receptor Location | Stimulus Type | # of Recognizable Qualities | Pathway |
|------------------|-------------------|---------------|------------------------------|---------|
| **Taste (Gustation)** | Taste buds (tongue, soft palate) | Non-volatile, water-soluble compounds | **5** (sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami) | Tongue → Brainstem → Insular cortex |
| **Aroma (Olfaction)** | Olfactory epithelium (nasal cavity) | Volatile, lipophilic compounds | **>10,000** distinct odorants | Olfactory nerve CN-I → Limbic system |
| **Flavor** | Combined (taste + aroma + chemesthesis + vision) | Integration of all chemical senses | Composite perception | Multi-modal cortical integration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Flavor ≠ Aroma.** Flavor is the *integrated multisensory experience* of taste + aroma + mouthfeel (chemesthesis) + visual cues. Aroma contributes roughly **75–95 % of what we perceive as flavor** in most foods. This is why food loses its "flavor" during a common cold — the nasal passages are blocked, and aroma molecules cannot reach the olfactory epithelium.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Musical Orchestra" Model

Imagine a symphony orchestra playing in a concert hall:
- The **taste buds** are like the **rhythm section** — bass drum, snare, hi-hat. There are only **five instruments** in this section, providing the foundational beat (sweet/sour/salty/bitter/umami).
- The **olfactory receptors** are the **full string, woodwind, brass, and percussion sections** — **hundreds of unique instruments**, each capable of producing thousands of distinct tones.
- The **aroma compounds** are the **musical notes** (volatile molecules) released from the food (the score).
- The **brain (olfactory cortex)** is the **conductor**, integrating the signals into a coherent perception.

Just as removing the violins would change the symphony dramatically, removing even a single aroma compound can dramatically alter food perception — even if the basic taste remains unchanged. This is why a coffee bean and a roasted peanut have entirely different "flavors" despite both being predominantly bitter.

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics in Aroma Science

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Odor Detection Threshold (ODT):** The lowest concentration of a compound at which **50 % of a sensory panel** can detect a difference from a blank (odorless) reference. Typical range: **ppb (parts per billion) to ppt (parts per trillion)**.
> - **Recognition Threshold (RT):** The concentration at which the odor **quality can be identified** (usually 2–10× higher than ODT).
> - **Vapor Pressure (P°):** Determines the volatility of a compound at a given temperature. Units: **Pa** or **mmHg**. Higher P° → more molecules escape into headspace → stronger aroma.
> - **Henry's Law Constant (H):** Governs the partitioning of volatile compounds between the food matrix and the gas phase. Units: **Pa·m³/mol**.

The **aroma profile** of a food is therefore a function of: (i) the identity and concentration of volatile molecules, (ii) their volatility and partitioning behavior, and (iii) the sensitivity of human olfactory receptors to those specific molecules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Classes of Food Aroma Compounds

Aroma compounds in foods belong to a wide array of chemical families. Their functional groups determine volatility, odor character, and reactivity.

### 2.1.1 Major Chemical Classes

1. **Aldehydes (R–CHO)**
   - Highly volatile, low molecular weight, often responsible for **green, grassy, fruity** notes.
   - Examples: **Hexanal** (green/grassy — from lipid oxidation), **Benzaldehyde** (almond/cherry), **(E)-2-Nonenal** (cucumber, cardboard off-flavor in aged beer).

2. **Ketones (R–CO–R')**
   - Often sweet, fruity, or **buttery** notes.
   - Examples: **Diacetyl** (2,3-butanedione — buttery/butterscotch), **1-Octen-3-one** (mushroom), **Damascenone** (cooked apple, rose).

3. **Alcohols (R–OH)**
   - Generally higher thresholds, contribute to **fruity, floral, fusel** notes.
   - Examples: **Ethanol** (alcohol), **1-Hexanol** (green, herbal), **Linalool** (floral, citrus — coriander, basil).

4. **Esters (R–COO–R')**
   - Most important class for **fruity aromas** (banana, pear, apple, pineapple).
   - Examples: **Ethyl butyrate** (pineapple), **Isoamyl acetate** (banana), **Ethyl 2-methylbutyrate** (apple).

5. **Acids (R–COOH)**
   - Pungent, rancid, cheesy or sweaty notes at high concentrations.
   - Examples: **Acetic acid** (vinegar), **Butyric acid** (rancid butter), **Isovaleric acid** (sweaty feet).

6. **Sulfur-Containing Compounds (R–S–R', thiols, thioesters)**
   - Extremely low thresholds (**ppt level**); powerful impact compounds.
   - Examples: **Dimethyl sulfide (DMS)** (cooked cabbage, corn), **4-Mercapto-4-methylpentan-2-one** (blackcurrant catty), **2-Methyl-3-furanthiol** (roasted meat).

7. **Pyrazines**
   - Formed via **Maillard reaction**; responsible for **roasted, nutty, earthy** notes.
   - Examples: **2,5-Dimethylpyrazine** (roasted peanut), **2-Acetyl-1-pyrroline** (popcorn, white bread crust).

8. **Pyridines, Pyrroles, Oxazoles, Thiazoles**
   - Heterocyclic Maillard products contributing to **meaty, bready, roasted** notes.

9. **Terpenes & Terpenoids**
   - Plant-derived isoprenoid compounds; **citrus, pine, herbal, floral** notes.
   - Examples: **Limonene** (lemon/orange), **α-Pinene** (pine), **Menthol** (mint).

10. **Lactones (cyclic esters)**
    - **Peach, coconut, creamy, peach-like** notes from γ- and δ-lactones.
    - Example: **γ-Decalactone** (peach), **δ-Decalactone** (creamy/dairy).

## 2.2 The Four Major Pathways of Aroma Formation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **formation pathways** of aroma compounds. Memorize the four key routes below.

### Pathway 1: **Enzymatic Pathways (in Fresh Plant & Animal Tissues)**
- **Lipoxygenase (LOX) pathway** in legumes, fruits, and vegetables:
  - Polyunsaturated fatty acids (linoleic, linolenic acid) → **hydroperoxides** (via LOX) → cleaved by **hydroperoxide lyase (HPL)** to **C6/C9 aldehydes** (hexanal, (Z)-3-hexenal = "leaf aldehyde").
- **Alliinase** in garlic/onion:
  - Alliin → allicin → diallyl thiosulfinate (pungent aroma of cut garlic).
- **β-Glucosidase** in stone fruits and tea:
  - Releases bound aglycones (e.g., linalool from glycoside precursors) upon tissue damage.

### Pathway 2: **Maillard Reaction (Non-Enzymatic Browning)**
- Occurs between **reducing sugars** (glucose, fructose) and **free amino groups** of amino acids/peptides/proteins during heating (typically >**140 °C** at low moisture).
- Three stages:
  1. **Early stage:** Amadori rearrangement products (no color/aroma).
  2. **Intermediate stage:** Deoxyosones, furfurals, **pyrazines**, carbonyls.
  3. **Final stage:** **Melanoidins** (brown polymers), **heterocyclic aroma compounds** (pyrroles, thiophenes, thiazoles, pyridines, oxazoles).
- The **Strecker degradation** (a sub-pathway): α-Amino acids are converted into **Strecker aldehydes** (one C-atom shorter than the parent amino acid) — e.g., **phenylalanine → phenylacetaldehyde** (flowery/honey-like), **methionine → methional** (boiled potato).

### Pathway 3: **Lipid Oxidation (Auto-oxidation & Enzymatic)**
- **Unsaturated fatty acids** undergo oxidation to form:
  - **Hydroperoxides** (primary products) → aldehydes, ketones, alcohols, hydrocarbons.
  - **Secondary products** (e.g., **hexanal, 2,4-decadienal, pentanal**) cause **rancid, green, metallic** off-flavors.
- Critical in **oils, dairy, meat, fish, cereals** (especially during storage).

### Pathway 4: **Fermentation (Microbial Metabolism)**
- **Lactic acid bacteria, yeasts, molds** generate esters, alcohols, acids, diacetyl, etc.
- Diacetyl (buttery) in dairy, esters in beer/wine, 4-vinyl guaiacol (clove) in Belgian beers by *Saccharomyces* yeasts.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative relation a KTU student must master for this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Odor Activity Value (OAV)** | $OAV = \dfrac{C_{food}}{OT_{compound}}$ | $C_{food}$ = conc. in food; $OT$ = odor threshold | Dimensionless | OAV ≥ 1 → compound contributes to aroma |
| 2 | **Rancimat Induction Period** (oil stability) | $t_{ind} \propto \dfrac{[Antioxidant]}{k_{ox}}$ | $k_{ox}$ = oxidation rate constant | hours | Used for shelf-life prediction of oils |
| 3 | **Henry's Law (headspace partitioning)** | $C_{gas} = H \cdot C_{aq}$ | $H$ = Henry's constant; $C_{aq}$ = aq. concentration | Pa·m³/mol | Governs aroma release from liquid foods |
| 4 | **Raoult's Law (in food matrix)** | $P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{0}$ | $x_i$ = mole fraction; $P_i^0$ = vapor pressure of pure $i$ | Pa | Modified by activity coefficients in non-ideal foods |
| 5 | **Vapor Pressure (Antoine Equation)** | $\ln P^0 = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | $A,B,C$ = Antoine constants; $T$ = temperature (°C or K) | Pa or mmHg | Use for predicting volatility at any $T$ |
| 6 | **Threshold Dilution (Charm Analysis)** | $TD = \dfrac{C_{food}}{OT_{compound}}$ | (Same as OAV, but for AEDA) | Dimensionless | Flavor Dilution (FD) factor in GC-O |
| 7 | **Arrhenius for Aroma Degradation** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $E_a$ = activation energy; $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K | 1/s, 1/day | Models loss of aroma during storage/heat |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Markdown Pitfall:** When writing OAV or any absolute value expression in your answer sheet, never use `|C|` (vertical pipes) inside a markdown table. In your notebook, write $\lvert C \rvert$ or $C_{food}$ explicitly. KTU valuation scripts will not penalize notation in written exams, but presentation matters.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flavor Encapsulation:** Spray-drying and coacervation techniques exploit Henry's Law and volatility to **encapsulate aroma compounds in maltodextrin/guar-gum matrices** to prevent evaporation during storage.
- **Aroma Recovery:** Distillation, pervaporation, and supercritical CO₂ extraction rely on differences in vapor pressure and partition coefficients.
- **Shelf-Life Prediction:** OAV decreases over time due to oxidation; kinetic modeling using Arrhenius equations allows shelf-life estimation of aroma-active compounds.
- **Sensory Mapping:** Food technologists construct **spider plots / polar plots** linking OAV values to sensory descriptors — used routinely in product development at F&B companies.
