# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define HACCP. List the seven principles of HACCP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):**
> **HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points)** is a systematic, science-based food safety management system that identifies, evaluates, and controls hazards (biological, chemical, physical) significant to food safety, as defined by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (WHO/FAO)**.

> **Seven Principles (2 marks — 1 mark for listing, 1 mark for accuracy):**
> 1. Conduct a **Hazard Analysis** and identify preventive measures.
> 2. Determine the **Critical Control Points (CCPs)**.
> 3. Establish **Critical Limits**.
> 4. Establish **Monitoring** procedures.
> 5. Establish **Corrective Actions**.
> 6. Establish **Verification** procedures.
> 7. Establish **Record-Keeping and Documentation**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **What is the difference between ISO 9000 and ISO 9001?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | ISO 9000 | ISO 9001 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Title** | Fundamentals and vocabulary | Requirements |
| **Purpose** | Defines **concepts, terms, and principles** for QMS | Specifies **mandatory QMS requirements** for certification |
| **Certification** | **Not certifiable** | **Certifiable** by accredited third-party bodies |
| **Role** | Supports understanding | Auditable standard |

> **Key Takeaway (1 mark):** **ISO 9000 is the "glossary"** of QMS terms, while **ISO 9001 is the "rulebook"** that an organization must comply with to achieve certification.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> (a) Describe the **ISO 9000 series of standards** in detail. Discuss the **eight quality management principles** as per ISO 9000:2000. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Explain the **seven principles of HACCP** as laid down by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission**. Construct a **CCP decision tree** and explain each step. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — ISO 9000 Series and Eight QMS Principles (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **ISO 9000 series** is a family of international quality management standards published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**. It provides a generic framework that any organization — manufacturing, service, food, or software — can adopt to ensure **consistent quality** and **customer satisfaction**.

**The Three Core Standards (2 marks):**
- **ISO 9000 — Fundamentals and vocabulary:** Establishes a common QMS vocabulary, ensuring unambiguous communication.
- **ISO 9001 — Requirements:** The **only certifiable** standard. Defines the requirements for a QMS.
- **ISO 9004 — Quality of an organization:** Provides guidance for **sustained success** beyond ISO 9001.

**Eight Quality Management Principles — ISO 9000:2000 (4 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Customer focus** — understanding present and future customer needs.
2. **Leadership** — unity of purpose and direction.
3. **Involvement of people** — full participation at all levels.
4. **Process approach** — managing activities as interrelated processes.
5. **System approach to management** — identifying, understanding, and managing interrelated processes as a system.
6. **Continual improvement** — a permanent organizational objective.
7. **Factual approach to decision-making** — decisions based on data and information analysis.
8. **Mutually beneficial supplier relationships** — enhances value-creating ability.

---

#### Part (b) — Seven HACCP Principles + CCP Decision Tree (7 marks)

**Introduction to Codex Principles (1 mark):**
The seven principles of HACCP were codified by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (1993, revised 2003)** as the global standard for food safety.

**The Seven Principles (3 marks — ~½ mark each):**

| No. | Principle | Key Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Hazard Analysis | Identify biological, chemical, physical hazards + preventive measures |
| 2 | Determine CCPs | Use Codex decision tree to identify Critical Control Points |
| 3 | Critical Limits | Set measurable safety thresholds (T, pH, time, a_w) |
| 4 | Monitoring | Define who/what/when/how for real-time observation |
| 5 | Corrective Actions | Predefined response when critical limit is breached |
| 6 | Verification | Confirm HACCP system effectiveness (audits, testing) |
| 7 | Record-Keeping | Maintain documentation for traceability and review |

**CCP Decision Tree (3 marks):**
- **Q1** — Do preventive control measures exist? *No* → Modify process. *Yes* → Q2.
- **Q2** — Is this step specifically designed to eliminate/reduce the hazard? *Yes* → **CCP**. *No* → Q3.
- **Q3** — Could contamination reach unacceptable levels? *No* → Not a CCP. *Yes* → Q4.
- **Q4** — Will a subsequent step eliminate/reduce it? *Yes* → Not a CCP. *No* → **CCP**.

**Conclusion:** Any step that is a **CCP** must have critical limits, monitoring, corrective action, and verification applied to it.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
> (a) Differentiate between the **ISO 9000 series** and the **HACCP system**. Discuss how these two systems can be **integrated** through the **ISO 22000** framework. **(7 marks)**
> (b) A dairy plant is producing **pasteurized milk**. Apply the **HACCP system** to identify the **hazards, CCPs, critical limits, monitoring procedures, corrective actions, verification, and record-keeping** requirements for this process. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — ISO 9000 vs. HACCP & ISO 22000 Integration (7 marks)

**Difference between ISO 9000 and HACCP (3 marks):**

| Parameter | ISO 9000 | HACCP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Focus** | Customer satisfaction, quality | Food safety, hazard prevention |
| **Scope** | Generic (any industry) | Food industry specific |
| **Approach** | Process-based (PDCA / HLS) | Hazard-based (7 principles) |
| **Hazards** | Quality defects | Biological, chemical, physical |
| **Certification** | Yes (ISO 9001) | No (covered by ISO 22000) |
| **Origin** | ISO (1987) | Pillsbury/NASA (1960s); Codex (1993) |
| **Documentation** | Quality manual | HACCP plan + CCP records |
| **Foundation** | Process discipline | PRPs (GMP, GHP, sanitation) |

**Integration through ISO 22000:2018 (4 marks):**
- **ISO 22000** is the **Food Safety Management System (FSMS)** standard that combines:
  1. **PRPs (Prerequisite Programmes)** — operational conditions needed before HACCP.
  2. **HACCP Plan** — 7 Codex principles.
  3. **QMS requirements** — borrowed from ISO 9001:2015 HLS structure (Clauses 4–10).
- It applies the **PDCA cycle** at both system and operational level.
- **Benefits of integration:**
  - Single audit for **quality + food safety**.
  - Common structure with **ISO 9001 and ISO 14001** (HLS).
  - Reduced duplication, lower audit cost.
  - **Continual improvement** across all processes.

---

#### Part (b) — HACCP Application to Pasteurized Milk (7 marks)

**Process Flow:** Raw milk reception → Filtration → Standardization → Pasteurization (72 °C × 15 s) → Cooling (≤ 5 °C) → Packaging → Cold storage

**HACCP Plan Elements (7 marks — 1 mark each section):**

**(1) Hazard Analysis (Principle 1):**
- Biological: *Salmonella*, *Listeria*, *E. coli*, *Brucella*.
- Chemical: Antibiotic residues, aflatoxin M1, cleaning agents (CIP).
- Physical: Metal, dirt, hair.

**(2) CCPs Identified (Principle 2):**
- **CCP-1:** Raw milk reception (antibiotic/contaminant testing).
- **CCP-2:** Pasteurization (heat treatment).

**(3) Critical Limits (Principle 3):**
- CCP-1: Antibiotic test = **Negative** (≤ MRL).
- CCP-2: **T ≥ 72 °C for t ≥ 15 s** (operational target: 75 °C for 15 s for safety margin).

**(4) Monitoring (Principle 4):**
- CCP-1: **Every batch** — rapid antibiotic test (Delvo) by trained QC technician.
- CCP-2: **Continuous** — digital probe + chart recorder with audible alarm at T < 71.5 °C.

**(5) Corrective Actions (Principle 5):**
- CCP-1 deviation: Reject the milk lot, return to supplier, audit farm.
- CCP-2 deviation: Divert to reprocessing, hold product, run phosphatase test (ALP).

**(6) Verification (Principle 6):**
- Daily plate count, weekly phosphatase test, monthly CIP validation, annual HACCP revalidation.

**(7) Record-Keeping (Principle 7):**
- CCP monitoring logs, deviation logs, calibration records, training records, audit reports (retained for at least the product shelf life + 1 year).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ISO 9000 with ISO 9001 (–2 marks):** ISO 9000 is the *vocabulary/fundamentals* standard, **not certifiable**. Only **ISO 9001** is certifiable. Many students write "ISO 9000 is the certifiable standard" — this is **incorrect** and will cost full marks on the comparison question.
>
> 2. **Listing only 5 or 6 HACCP principles (–1 to –2 marks):** The **Codex 7 principles are non-negotiable**. The most common omission is **Principle 6 (Verification)**. Memorize using the acronym **"HACCP-HALCCVR"** or the **Seven P's**.
>
> 3. **Confusing CCP with Hazard (–2 marks):** A **CCP is a step (location) where a hazard is controlled**, not the hazard itself. Many students write "Salmonella is a CCP" — wrong. *Salmonella* is a hazard; *the pasteurization step* is the CCP.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Decision Tree in 14-mark questions (–3 marks):** KTU expects the **Codex CCP Decision Tree** (Q1–Q4) to be drawn **or** described step-by-step. Writing only the seven principles without the decision-tree application will lead to mark deductions.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Prerequisites (PRPs) in HACCP (–1 mark):** HACCP **cannot work in isolation**. Always state that **GMP, GHP, and sanitation (PRPs)** are the **foundation** on which HACCP is built.
>
> 6. **Wrong year/version citation (–1 mark):** Use **"ISO 9000:2015"** (current) or **"ISO 9000:2000"** (legacy 8 principles). Do **not** mix principles from different versions within the same answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — KTU 2024 Module 1**

### ISO 9000 Series — Key Points
- The **ISO 9000 series** is published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**.
- The series has **3 core standards**: **ISO 9000** (vocabulary), **ISO 9001** (requirements, certifiable), **ISO 9004** (sustained success, not certifiable).
- **ISO 9000:2000** defines **8 QMS principles**; **ISO 9000:2015** defines **7 QMS principles** + **HLS** (10-clause structure).
- **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** is the core cycle of ISO 9001.
- ISO 9000 is **generic, voluntary, and applicable to any industry**; it is **not industry-specific**.

### HACCP — Key Points
- **HACCP = Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points**.
- Developed in the **1960s** by **Pillsbury, NASA, and the US Army**; codified by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (WHO/FAO)** in 1993.
- **7 Principles** (Codex): **H**azard analysis, **A**CCP, **L**imits, **M**onitoring, **C**orrective action, **V**erification, **R**ecords.
- **12 implementation steps** = 5 preparatory + 7 principles.
- **CCP Decision Tree:** Q1 → Q2 → (Q3 → Q4) — know the logic flow.
- **Critical Limits** are **measurable** (T, pH, a_w, time, concentration).
- **HACCP requires PRPs (GMP, GHP, sanitation)** as the foundation.
- **HACCP is not certifiable by ISO** — covered by **ISO 22000**.

### Integration — Key Points
- **ISO 22000:2018** = **PRPs + HACCP + ISO 9001 PDCA structure** = integrated FSMS.
- ISO 22000 uses the **HLS** (same 10-clause structure as ISO 9001, ISO 14001).
- **FSSAI** (India) mandates **HACCP-based FSMS** for high-risk food businesses.
- The integrated approach allows **single audit, lower cost, and continual improvement** across quality, safety, and environment.

### Exam Day Quick-Recall
- **First thing in a 14-mark HACCP question:** State the **7 Principles** (1 mark).
- **Always draw a process flow** before applying the decision tree (1 mark).
- **Always state PRPs** before launching into HACCP (1 mark).
- **Cite Codex Alimentarius** as the source — adds academic weight.
- **Use a tabular format** for ISO 9000 vs. HACCP comparisons — fetches full marks faster.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Codex Alimentarius Protocols for Export
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT758 - FOOD SAFETY AND SANITATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Codex Alimentarius Protocols for Export # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Codex Alimentarius** (Latin for "Food Code") is an internationally recognized collection of **standards, guidelines, codes of practice, and recommendations** adopted by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** — a joint body of the **Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO)** and the **World Health Organization (WHO)** of the United Nations — pertaining to food safety, food quality, consumer protection, and fair practices in the international food trade. The Codex Alimentarius is the single most important international reference point for food standards. With respect to **export protocols**, the Codex defines the procedural, hygienic, microbiological, chemical, and labelling framework that a country (exporter) must satisfy so that its food consignments are accepted at the importing country's port of entry without rejection, detention, or destruction. ### Key Statutory Metrics in Codex Export Protocols - **CAC Founding Year:** **1963** - **Current Member Composition:** **189 Member Countries + 1 Member Organization (European Union)** = **190 total members** as of 2024 - **Total Codex Standards Adopted:** Over **200 commodity standards** and **4,000+ maximum levels** for food additives, contaminants, pesticide residues, veterinary drugs, and food hygiene criteria. - **Reference Standards Body under WTO/SPS Agreement:** Codex standards serve as the **benchmark for dispute settlement** under the **Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (SPS Agreement, 1995)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Analogy: "The Universal Passport for Food"** Imagine you are travelling internationally with your pet dog. Different countries demand different vaccination certificates, microchip standards, quarantine durations, and document formats. To simplify your life, suppose there existed **one master health passport** issued by an internationally trusted body — every country's customs accepts it without question. **The Codex Alimentarius is exactly that "master passport" for food.** When India (or any country) exports food: - The **Codex Standards** tell the Indian exporter *what limits to follow* (e.g., maximum aflatoxin in groundnuts = **15 µg/kg** for further processing, **10 µg/kg** for direct human consumption as per CXS 193). - The **Codex Codes of Practice** tell them *how to operate* (e.g., CAC/RCP 1-1969: General Principles of Food Hygiene). - The **Codex Guidelines** provide *interpretive latitude* (e.g., risk management recommendations for specific commodity groups). So while the importing country (say, the EU or USA) has its own stricter or equivalent regulations, the **Codex serves as the international lingua franca** — the common legal-scientific language accepted under WTO trade law. ## 1.3 Why Codex Alimentarius Matters for Export | Aspect | Role in Export | |---|---| | **WTO/SPS Compliance** | Codex standards are presumed safe; deviations must be scientifically justified. | | **Dispute Resolution** | WTO panels use Codex standards to judge trade disputes. | | **Harmonization** | Eliminates need to comply with 200 different national standards. | | **Market Access** | Many bilateral trade agreements require Codex equivalence. | | **Consumer Confidence** | Acts as a globally trusted seal of food safety. | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of Codex Alimentarius — The Three-Tier Document Hierarchy The Codex Alimentarius Commission organizes its outputs into a strict hierarchical structure. For export protocols, every food consignment must be traced through this three-tier framework. ### Tier 1: Codex Standards (CXS / Codex Stan) A **Codex Standard** is a legally recognized, vertically specific regulation for a commodity. Examples: - **CXS 1-1985:** General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods - **CXS 193-1995:** General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed - **CXS 150-1985:** Standard for Food Grade Salt - **CXS 73-1981:** Standard for Canned Baby Foods - **CXS 12-1981:** Standard for Honey (relevant for India's honey export to EU/US) ### Tier 2: Codes of Practice (CAC/RCP) A **Code of Practice** defines *operational hygiene and process control*. They are technology-neutral, meaning they specify *what outcome* must be achieved, not *which equipment* must be used. Examples: - **CAC/RCP 1-1969 (Rev. 2020):** General Principles of Food Hygiene — covers **Primary Production → Processing → Distribution → Consumer** - **CAC/RCP 52-2003:** Code of Hygienic Practice for Refrigerated Packaged Foods with Extended Shelf Life - **CAC/RCP 57-2004:** Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables - **CAC/RCP 8-1976:** Code of Hygienic Practice for Processed Meat and Poultry Products ### Tier 3: Guidelines (CAC/GL) **Guidelines** are non-binding recommendations that provide interpretive assistance. Examples: - **CAC/GL 21-1997:** Principles for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria for Foods - **CAC/GL 30-1999 (Rev. 2014):** Principles and Guidelines for the Conduct of Food Import and Export Inspection and Certification Systems - **CAC/GL 19-1995:** Criteria for the Establishment of Methods of Analysis for Codex Purposes ## 2.2 The Five Foundational Principles Underlying Codex Export Protocols > [!NOTE] > **The "Five-Pillar Foundation" of Codex Standards** 1. **Risk Analysis Framework (RAF)** — A structured 3-step scientific process: *Risk Assessment → Risk Management → Risk Communication*. Based on the **Risk Analysis Principles adopted in 2003**. 2. **HACCP (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point)** — Codex formally adopted HACCP in **1993 (ALINORM 93/13A)** as the international reference for hazard control in food processing. The HACCP system has **7 principles** + **5 preliminary tasks (D1–D5)** + **12 implementation steps**. 3. **Good Hygienic Practice (GHP)** — Foundational prerequisite programmes covering personnel hygiene, premise design, equipment, sanitation, and pest control. 4. **Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)** — Process control discipline covering traceability, lot identification, recall, and documentation. 5. **Transparency & Equivalence** — All Codex decisions are made through transparent, member-driven consensus. Under the SPS Agreement, importing countries must accept Codex-equivalent measures from exporting countries. ## 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet — Key Codex Numbers and Limits > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD TABLE — Memorize these for KTU Examinations** | Codex Reference | Subject | Key Limit / Rule | |---|---|---| | **CXS 193-1995** | Contaminants in food | **Lead in fruits/veg = 0.1 mg/kg**; **Aflatoxin B1 in peanuts = 5 µg/kg (ready-to-eat)** | | **CXS 150-1985** | Food grade salt | Sodium chloride ≥ **97%** on dry basis; heavy metals ≤ **1 mg/kg** | | **CAC/RCP 1-1969** | General Principles of Food Hygiene | Defines **8 GHP sections + HACCP logic sequence** | | **CAC/GL 21-1997** | Microbiological criteria | Establishes **2-class** and **3-class sampling plans** | | **CAC/GL 30-1999** | Import/Export Inspection & Certification | Defines **10 design principles** for national food control systems | | **CAC/RCP 57-2004** | Fresh produce hygiene | Defines **8 control measures** for fresh fruit/vegetable safety | | **CXS 1-1985** | Labelling | Mandates **name of food, ingredients list, net contents, name & address, country of origin, lot identification, date marking, instructions for use** | > **Note on MRLs (Maximum Residue Limits):** Codex also publishes **MRLs for pesticides** through the **Codex Committee on Pesticide Residues (CCPR)** and **MRLs for veterinary drugs** through the **Codex Committee on Residues of Veterinary Drugs in Foods (CCRVDF)**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Codex Compliance - **Food Process Engineering:** HACCP-driven plant layout design with **CCP segregation zones**, **air-pressure cascades** (15 Pa positive in high-risk rooms), and **unidirectional flow of materials/personnel**. - **Cold Chain Engineering:** Compliance with CAC/RCP 52 requires temperature-controlled rooms capable of holding **≤ 4 °C for chilled** and **≤ -18 °C for frozen** products with continuous data logging. - **Packaging Engineering:** Codex MRLs for metals drive **food-grade stainless steel selection (SS 304 / SS 316)** and migration testing under **21 CFR / EU Regulation 1935/2004 / CXS general standard on packaging**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Export Protocol Workflow & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Codex Export Compliance Workflow — 10 Sequential Steps > [!NOTE] > **Framework: From Primary Production to Port-of-Entry Clearance** The Codex export compliance process can be broken into a deterministic 10-step sequence. For KTU, you must be able to enumerate and explain each step. ### Step 1: Primary Production Compliance **Goal:** Ensure raw material sourcing meets **CAC/RCP 1-1969, Section IV (Primary Production)** requirements. - Water used for irrigation / aquaculture: must meet **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality (4th ed.)** microbiological criteria. - Pesticide application: must respect **Codex MRLs as per CCPR database**. - Animal feed: must respect **Codex MRLs for veterinary drugs** (e.g., chloramphenicol = **Banned** in all exported animal-origin foods). ### Step 2: Establishment Design & Facilities **Goal:** Comply with **CAC/RCP 1-1969, Section VI (Establishment: Design and Facilities)**. - Internal structure: **Smooth, impervious, washable, non-toxic** materials. - Floor drainage: **minimum gradient 1:60** to prevent standing water. - Lighting: **≥ 540 lux** at inspection points; **≥ 220 lux** elsewhere. - Ventilation: Adequate to prevent condensation and airborne contamination. ### Step 3: HACCP Plan Implementation (12 Codex Steps) The Codex Alimentarius Commission endorsed HACCP through **ALINORM 93/13A (1993)**. The complete implementation sequence is: $$ \text{Codex HACCP Implementation} = \underbrace{D1 \rightarrow D2 \rightarrow D3 \rightarrow D4 \rightarrow D5}_{\text{5 Preliminary Tasks}} \rightarrow \underbrace{P1 \rightarrow P2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow P7}_{\text{7 Principles}} $$ Where: - **D1** = Assemble HACCP team - **D2** = Describe product (raw materials, composition, processing, packaging, distribution) - **D3** = Identify intended use and consumer - **D4** = Construct flow diagram (process steps) - **D5** = On-site verification of flow diagram - **P1** = Hazard analysis (biological, chemical, physical) - **P2** = Determine CCPs using the **Codex Decision Tree** (4-question logic) - **P3** = Establish critical limits (e.g., **75 °C for ≥ 1 min** for milk pasteurization) - **P4** = Establish monitoring procedures (who, what, when, how, frequency) - **P5** = Establish corrective actions - **P6** = Establish verification procedures (audit, microbiological testing) - **P7** = Establish documentation and record-keeping ### Step 4: Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SSOPs) **Goal:** Implement cleaning, sanitation, and pest control per **CAC/RCP 1-1969, Section VIII (Establishment: Maintenance and Sanitation)**. - Cleaning frequency: Daily for food-contact surfaces; weekly for non-food-contact. - Sanitizer concentration check: typically **chlorine 50–200 ppm** OR **quaternary ammonium 200–400 ppm**. - Pest control: bait stations at **≤ 6 m spacing** along perimeter; **integrated pest management (IPM)** approach. ### Step 5: Personnel Hygiene & Training **Goal:** Comply with **CAC/RCP 1-1969, Section X (Personal Hygiene)**. - Health screening: **No worker with communicable disease** (typhoid, hepatitis A, cholera, tuberculosis, diarrhoea) shall handle food. - Handwashing: **≥ 20 seconds** with potable water + soap at **≥ 38 °C**. - Protective clothing: **Head cover, clean uniform, closed footwear, gloves** (where specified). ### Step 6: Process Control & CCP Monitoring **Goal:** Maintain critical limits within the established HACCP plan. Example — CCP for milk pasteurization: $$ T_{\text{milk}} \geq 71.7\,^{\circ}\text{C} \quad \text{for} \quad t \geq 15\, \text{seconds} \quad \text{(HTST, per CAC/RCP 57-2004 equivalent standards)} $$ Equivalent bacterial kill is calculated using $D$-value and $z$-value: $$ \log \left( \frac{N_0}{N_f} \right) = \frac{T_{\text{ref}} - T_{\text{actual}}}{z} $$ Where: - $N_0$ = initial microbial load - $N_f$ = final microbial load (target) - $T_{\text{ref}}$ = reference temperature (e.g., 71.7 °C) - $T_{\text{actual}}$ = actual process temperature - $z$ = temperature increase (°C) required to reduce $D$-value by 90% (typically **z = 6.5 °C** for *Listeria monocytogenes*) For a 5-log reduction target in milk at $T_{\text{actual}} = 71.7\,^{\circ}\text{C}$: $$ \log\left(\frac{N_0}{N_f}\right) = 5 $$ $$ \text{Required holding time} = 5 \times D_{71.7\,^{\circ}\text{C}} $$ If $D_{71.7\,^{\circ}\text{C}} = 3$ seconds for *Listeria monocytogenes*: $$ t = 5 \times 3 = 15 \text{ seconds} $$ This confirms the Codex-recommended **HTST** benchmark. ### Step 7: Microbiological Testing & Verification **Goal:** Apply **CAC/GL 21-1997** for two-class and three-class sampling plans. A **two-class plan** uses the formula: $$ n = \frac{\ln(\alpha)}{\ln(A_c + 1) - \ln(N)} \quad \text{approx.} $$ Where: - $n$ = number of samples - $\alpha$ = probability of accepting a defective lot (typically **0.05**) - $A_c$ = acceptance number (number of defective samples allowed) - $N$ = total units in lot For a 2-class plan with **n = 5, c = 0** (zero tolerance): $$ P_{\text{accept}} = (1-p)^n = (1-0.05)^5 \approx 0.7738 $$ Meaning there is a **77.38%** probability of rejecting a lot with 5% defective units — i.e., the plan still allows some defective units to slip through, which is why **n is increased** for tighter tolerance. ### Step 8: Labelling & Traceability **Goal:** Comply with **CXS 1-1985** (Pre-packaged Foods Labelling) and **CAC/GL 60-2006** (Traceability). - **Mandatory label elements:** 1. Name of the food 2. List of ingredients (in descending order of weight) 3. Net contents (in metric units) 4. Name and address of manufacturer/packer/distributor 5. Country of origin 6. Lot identification 7. Date marking (manufacture / "use by" / best-before) 8. Storage conditions 9. Instructions for use 10. Allergen declaration (per CXS 1-1985 Rev. 2010) ### Step 9: Pre-export Inspection by Competent Authority **Goal:** Comply with **CAC/GL 30-1999 (Rev. 2014)** — Principles for Food Import/Export Inspection and Certification Systems. The 10 design principles are: $$ \text{National Inspection System} = f(\text{Legal authority, Risk basis, Transparency, Equivalence, ...}) $$ Key requirements: - Inspection must be **risk-based**, not arbitrary. - Exporter must obtain a **Health Certificate / Export Certificate** from the national competent authority (e.g., **FSSAI in India, FDA in USA, EFSA in EU**). - The certificate must follow the **Codex Model Export Certificate for Food and Feed (CXS 38-2001)**. ### Step 10: Port-of-Entry Clearance in Importing Country **Goal:** Match the **importing country's border requirements**. - Customs declaration + commercial invoice + packing list + health certificate + Certificate of Analysis (COA). - Sampling by importing authority for verification analysis. - Decision: **Release / Detain / Re-export / Destroy**. - Right of appeal under SPS Agreement if rejection is scientifically unjustified. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Codex Export Decision Logic The Codex export framework can be summarized in the following decision flow: $$ \text{Exporter Intent} \rightarrow \text{Check Codex Standard for Commodity} \rightarrow \text{Implement HACCP} $$ $$ \rightarrow \text{Apply GHP/GMP} \rightarrow \text{Conduct Labelling Audit} \rightarrow \text{Request Inspection} $$ $$ \rightarrow \text{Receive Health Certificate} \rightarrow \text{Submit at Port-of-Entry} \rightarrow \text{Outcome: Accept / Reject} $$ If **rejected**, the **SPS Agreement Article 5 (Risk Assessment)** permits the importing country to apply **higher protection** only if it has **scientific justification**. This is the **Equivalence Clause**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Codex Export Compliance Architecture > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following flowchart maps the 10-step Codex export workflow with its associated document references. Each node is named with an alphanumeric prefix to comply with Mermaid syntax.** ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Start: Export Intent] --> A2[Step 1: Primary Production Compliance
CAC or RCP 1-1969 Section IV] A2 --> A3[Step 2: Establishment Design
CAC or RCP 1-1969 Section VI] A3 --> A4[Step 3: HACCP Plan Implementation
12 Codex Steps] A4 --> A5[Step 4: SSOP Implementation
CAC or RCP 1-1969 Section VIII] A5 --> A6[Step 5: Personnel Hygiene and Training
CAC or RCP 1-1969 Section X] A6 --> A7[Step 6: Process Control and CCP Monitoring
Per HACCP Plan] A7 --> A8[Step 7: Microbiological Verification
CAC or GL 21-1997] A8 --> A9[Step 8: Labelling and Traceability
CXS 1-1985 and CAC or GL 60-2006] A9 --> A10[Step 9: Pre-Export Inspection
CAC or GL 30-1999 and CXS 38-2001] A10 --> A11{Compliance Check
Pass or Fail} A11 -- Pass --> A12[Step 10: Port-of-Entry Clearance
SPS Agreement] A11 -- Fail --> A13[Corrective Action
Root Cause Analysis] A13 --> A4 A12 --> A14[End: Goods Released to Market] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Codex Three-Tier Document Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR B1[Codex Alimentarius Commission
FAO and WHO Joint Body] B1 --> B2[Tier 1: Codex Standards
CXS Series - Mandatory Limits] B1 --> B3[Tier 2: Codes of Practice
CAC or RCP Series - How to Operate] B1 --> B4[Tier 3: Guidelines
CAC or GL Series - Interpretive Guidance] B2 --> B2a[CXS 193-1995 Contaminants] B2 --> B2b[CXS 1-1985 Labelling] B3 --> B3a[CAC or RCP 1-1969 Hygiene] B3 --> B3b[CAC or RCP 57-2004 Fresh Produce] B4 --> B4a[CAC or GL 21-1997 Micro Criteria] B4 --> B4b[CAC or GL 30-1999 Inspection] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Codex in the WTO Trade Framework ```mermaid flowchart TB C1[Codex Alimentarius Commission] --> C2[Standards and Guidelines] C2 --> C3[SPS Agreement Reference] C3 --> C4[WTO Member States] C4 --> C5[National Competent Authority
e.g., FSSAI] C5 --> C6[Export Inspection System] C6 --> C7[Health Certificate Issuance] C7 --> C8[Exporter] C8 --> C9[Shipping and Logistics] C9 --> C10[Importing Country Border] C10 --> C11{Verification Test} C11 -- Accept --> C12[Market Release] C11 -- Reject --> C13[Dispute under SPS Article 5] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — HACCP Decision Tree (Codex Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD D1[Identify Hazard at Process Step] --> D2{Q1: Do preventive control measures exist?} D2 -- No --> D3[Not a CCP - Modify process] D2 -- Yes --> D4{Q2: Is this step specifically designed
to eliminate or reduce the hazard?} D4 -- Yes --> D5[CCP] D4 -- No --> D6{Q3: Could contamination occur
in excess of acceptable level?} D6 -- No --> D3 D6 -- Yes --> D7{Q4: Will subsequent steps eliminate
or reduce the hazard?} D7 -- No --> D8[CCP] D7 -- Yes --> D3 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define the Codex Alimentarius Commission. State its parent organizations and the year of establishment.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** is the international body responsible for developing harmonized international food standards, guidelines, and codes of practice to protect consumer health and ensure fair practices in food trade. - **Parent Organizations:** Joint body of the **Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO)** and the **World Health Organization (WHO)** of the United Nations. - **Year of Establishment:** **1963**. **(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for FAO + WHO, 1 mark for 1963.)** --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **List the three Codex documents that govern food hygiene, labelling, and microbial limits for exported foods.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Food Hygiene:** **CAC/RCP 1-1969 (Rev. 2020)** — General Principles of Food Hygiene. 2. **Labelling:** **CXS 1-1985** — General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods. 3. **Microbial Limits:** **CAC/GL 21-1997** — Principles for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria for Foods. **(1 mark per correct answer with reference number.)** --- ## 5.2 PART B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` ### Question A **(a)** Explain in detail the **principles, structure, and significance of the Codex Alimentarius Commission** for international food trade. Discuss its role under the WTO/SPS Agreement. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **HACCP system as adopted by Codex (1993)**. List the **5 preliminary tasks and 7 principles**, and explain the **Codex Decision Tree** for CCP determination with a suitable example. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer for Question A **(a) Codex Alimentarius Commission: Principles, Structure, Significance** The **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** was established in **1963** by the **FAO** and the **WHO** to develop internationally recognized food standards, guidelines, and codes of practice. The term "Codex Alimentarius" is Latin for "Food Code." **Structure of CAC:** - **Plenary Session:** The supreme decision-making body, convened every 2 years (alternating with FAO/WHO regional conferences). - **Executive Committee:** Oversees work between plenary sessions; comprises a Chairperson, Vice-Chairpersons, and regional coordinators. - **General Subject Committees:** e.g., CCFH (Food Hygiene), CCFA (Food Additives), CCPR (Pesticide Residues), CCRVDF (Veterinary Drug Residues), CCMAS (Methods of Analysis and Sampling). - **Commodity Committees:** e.g., Fish & Fishery Products, Fresh Fruits & Vegetables, Fats & Oils, Cocoa Products. - **Regional Coordinating Committees:** Africa, Asia, Europe, Latin America & Caribbean, Near East, North America, South-West Pacific. - **Ad hoc Task Forces:** Created for time-bound issues (e.g., antimicrobial resistance, food fraud). **Core Principles of Codex:** 1. **Science-based decision making** using risk analysis (risk assessment, management, communication). 2. **Transparency:** All meetings are open to member states and observer NGOs. 3. **Consensus-based adoption:** Standards are adopted only after consensus among member countries. 4. **Consumer health protection as primary aim.** 5. **Fair trade promotion.** **Significance under WTO/SPS Agreement (1995):** Under the **Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (SPS)**, the Codex standards are the **international benchmark** for food safety measures. **Article 3.1** of the SPS Agreement explicitly recognizes Codex standards as the reference point. Any country that adopts Codex-equivalent measures is presumed to comply with SPS obligations. Conversely, any country imposing **stricter measures than Codex** must provide **scientific justification** under Article 5 (Risk Assessment). This makes Codex standards **legally and politically central to international food trade.** **(Mark Allocation for part (a):** - CAC establishment & structure: 3 marks - Core principles: 2 marks - WTO/SPS significance: 2 marks **= 7 marks.)** --- **(b) HACCP System as Adopted by Codex (1993)** The **Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point (HACCP)** system was endorsed by the Codex Alimentarius Commission in **1993 (ALINORM 93/13A)** as the international reference framework for food safety control. **5 Preliminary Tasks (D1 to D5):** - **D1:** Assemble the HACCP team (multidisciplinary: production, quality, microbiology, engineering). - **D2:** Describe the product (composition, raw materials, processing, packaging, storage, distribution). - **D3:** Identify intended use and consumer (general public, infants, immunocompromised, etc.). - **D4:** Construct the process flow diagram. - **D5:** On-site verification of the flow diagram. **7 Principles (P1 to P7):** - **P1:** Conduct hazard analysis (identify biological, chemical, physical hazards at each step). - **P2:** Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs) using the **Codex Decision Tree**. - **P3:** Establish critical limits for each CCP (e.g., **75 °C for 1 minute** in canned foods). - **P4:** Establish monitoring procedures (who monitors, how, frequency). - **P5:** Establish corrective actions when critical limits are exceeded. - **P6:** Establish verification procedures (audits, microbiological testing, review records). - **P7:** Establish documentation and record-keeping (manual + digital logs). **Codex Decision Tree for CCP Determination (4 Questions):** 1. **Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard? — If **No**, the step is **not a CCP**; modify the process. 2. **Q2:** Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the hazard to an acceptable level? — If **Yes**, it is a **CCP**. 3. **Q3:** Could contamination occur in excess of acceptable level(s), or could these reach unacceptable level(s)? — If **No**, the step is **not a CCP**. 4. **Q4:** Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the hazard? — If **No**, the step is a **CCP**. **Example — CCP in Milk Pasteurization:** - **Hazard:** *Listeria monocytogenes* (Biological). - **Critical Limit:** **71.7 °C for 15 seconds (HTST)**. - **Decision Tree Application:** - Q1: Preventive control exists (thermal pasteurization) → Yes. - Q2: Step designed to reduce hazard → Yes → **CCP identified**. - **Monitoring:** Continuous temperature recording chart (every 5 seconds) + flow diversion valve. - **Corrective Action:** Flow diversion if temperature falls below 71.7 °C. **(Mark Allocation for part (b):** - 5 preliminary tasks: 2 marks - 7 principles: 2 marks - Decision Tree + example: 3 marks **= 7 marks.)** --- ## 5.3 PART B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` ### Question B **(a)** Discuss the **Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)**. Explain the sections covering **Primary Production, Establishment Design, Sanitation, and Personal Hygiene**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Codex Standard for Contaminants (CXS 193-1995)** and outline the **10 design principles** of the **Codex Guidelines for Import and Export Inspection and Certification Systems (CAC/GL 30-1999, Rev. 2014)**. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer for Question B **(a) Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)** The **CAC/RCP 1-1969** is the foundational Codex document that lays down the hygiene framework for the entire food chain — from primary production to consumption. It is recognized as the basis for **HACCP implementation** worldwide. **Sections of CAC/RCP 1-1969:** **Section IV — Primary Production:** - Covers environmental hygiene, water quality, agricultural inputs (pesticides, veterinary drugs), and worker hygiene at the farm level. - Water for primary production must comply with **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality**. - Soil and air quality must be monitored to prevent chemical contamination. **Section VI — Establishment: Design and Facilities:** - Internal structures: **smooth, impervious, washable, non-toxic** materials; **rounded corners** to facilitate cleaning. - Floors: **impervious, non-absorbent, non-slip**, with adequate drainage (**gradient ≥ 1:60**). - Walls: **light-coloured, smooth, washable**. - Ceilings: designed to prevent condensation and mould. - Lighting: **≥ 540 lux** at inspection points, **≥ 220 lux** in workrooms. - Ventilation: adequate to prevent heat, condensation, and airborne contamination. - Sanitary facilities: separate for raw and processed product areas. **Section VIII — Establishment: Maintenance and Sanitation:** - **SSOPs (Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures)** must be implemented. - Cleaning: Daily for food-contact surfaces; periodic for non-food-contact. - Disinfection: Effective sanitizers used at correct concentrations (e.g., **chlorine 50–200 ppm**, **QAC 200–400 ppm**). - Pest control: **Integrated Pest Management (IPM)** with bait stations, insect electrocutors, and rodent traps. - Waste management: segregated collection, daily removal, and proper disposal. **Section X — Personal Hygiene:** - **Medical fitness:** No worker with communicable disease (typhoid, hepatitis A, cholera, TB, diarrhoea) shall handle food. - **Hand washing:** ≥ **20 seconds** with soap and potable water at **≥ 38 °C**. - **Protective clothing:** Head cover, clean uniform, closed footwear, gloves (where specified). - **Behavioural rules:** No smoking, eating, or chewing in food handling areas. - **Training:** All workers must undergo periodic food safety training. **(Mark Allocation for part (a):** - Section IV Primary Production: 2 marks - Section VI Establishment: 2 marks - Section VIII Sanitation + Section X Hygiene: 3 marks **= 7 marks.)** --- **(b) Codex Standard for Contaminants (CXS 193-1995) and Import/Export Inspection Guidelines** **CXS 193-1995 — General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed:** This standard defines **maximum levels (MLs)** and **maximum residue levels (MRLs)** for various contaminants in food and feed. Key contaminants covered include: - **Mycotoxins:** Aflatoxin B1, B2, G1, G2, M1; Ochratoxin A; Patulin; Fumonisins. - **Heavy Metals:** Lead (Pb), Cadmium (Cd), Arsenic (As), Mercury (Hg), Tin (Sn). - **Industrial Contaminants:** 3-Monochloropropane-1,2-diol (3-MCPD), Glycidol, Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs). - **Radionuclides:** Cs-134, Cs-137, I-131 (post-Chernobyl and post-Fukushima updates). - **Nitrates:** Maximum levels in vegetables (especially spinach and lettuce). **Examples of Limits:** - **Lead in milk:** **0.02 mg/kg**. - **Aflatoxin B1 in peanuts for further processing:** **15 µg/kg**; for direct human consumption: **10 µg/kg**; total aflatoxins: **15 µg/kg**. - **Cadmium in polished rice:** **0.4 mg/kg**. **CAC/GL 30-1999 (Rev. 2014) — Principles for Food Import and Export Inspection and Certification Systems (10 Design Principles):** 1. **Legal basis and authority** for inspection. 2. **Risk-based approach** to inspection frequency. 3. **Transparency** in inspection procedures. 4. **Equivalence** — accepting the measures of exporting countries that achieve the same level of protection. 5. **Non-discrimination** between domestic and imported products. 6. **Special and differential treatment** for developing countries. 7. **Information exchange** between exporting and importing competent authorities. 8. **Capacity building** — technical assistance to developing countries. 9. **Recognition of certification systems** including **third-party certification**. 10. **Documentation and review** — continuous improvement. **Practical Application for an Indian Exporter (Example: Basmati Rice to Saudi Arabia):** - Pre-shipment inspection by **EIC (Export Inspection Council of India)** or **FSSAI**. - Health certificate issued in **Codex Model Format (CXS 38-2001)**. - Sampling for aflatoxin, pesticide residues, heavy metals. - COA accompanies the consignment. - Customs clearance via the **Saudi Food and Drug Authority (SFDA)** with bilateral recognition of **Codex-equivalent** inspection. **(Mark Allocation for part (b):** - CXS 193 contaminants and limits: 3 marks - 10 design principles: 4 marks **= 7 marks.)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Do NOT confuse CAC/RCP numbers with CXS numbers.** CXS = Codex Standard (commodity-specific). CAC/RCP = Recommended Code of Practice. CAC/GL = Guideline. Mixing them up costs **2–3 marks** instantly. > 2. **The year 1963 is for the founding of CAC, not for CAC/RCP 1.** CAC/RCP 1-1969 means it was first adopted in 1969 (later revised in 2020). Students frequently write 1963 — this is a common mark-loss trap. > 3. **HACCP has 5 preliminary tasks + 7 principles = 12 steps.** Writing only "7 principles" without the preliminary tasks is incomplete and loses **1–2 marks**. > 4. **Do not skip the Codex Decision Tree.** Many students describe HACCP without the CCP decision tree — this is the most-tested sub-question in KTU papers. > 5. **Always cite the source document** (e.g., "as per CAC/RCP 1-1969, Section VI") rather than writing generic statements. Citation earns full marks; vague answers lose marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Codex Alimentarius Protocols for Export** - **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC):** Established **1963**; joint body of **FAO + WHO**; **190 members** as of 2024. - **Three-Tier Hierarchy:** **CXS** (Standards), **CAC/RCP** (Codes of Practice), **CAC/GL** (Guidelines). - **Foundational Documents to Memorize:** - **CAC/RCP 1-1969 (Rev. 2020)** — General Principles of Food Hygiene. - **CXS 193-1995** — Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed. - **CXS 1-1985** — Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods. - **CAC/GL 21-1997** — Microbiological Criteria. - **CAC/GL 30-1999 (Rev. 2014)** — Import/Export Inspection Systems. - **HACCP Formula:** **5 Preliminary Tasks (D1–D5) + 7 Principles (P1–P7) = 12 Steps**, adopted by Codex in **1993**. - **HACCP Decision Tree:** 4-question logic leading to CCP or not-CCP classification. - **Critical Limits Examples:** Milk HTST = **71.7 °C × 15 s**; Lead in milk = **0.02 mg/kg**; Aflatoxin B1 in peanuts (ready-to-eat) = **5 µg/kg**. - **Codex Model Export Certificate:** **CXS 38-2001**. - **WTO/SPS Agreement (1995):** Codex standards are the **legal reference** for international food trade disputes. - **Codex Committees (General Subject):** CCFH, CCFA, CCPR, CCRVDF, CCFICS, CCMAS. - **Labelling Mandatory Elements (CXS 1-1985):** 10 elements including name, ingredients, net contents, origin, lot ID, date marking, allergens. - **Personnel Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1):** **Handwash ≥ 20 s, water ≥ 38 °C;** no worker with communicable disease. - **Two-Class Sampling Plan (CAC/GL 21):** Uses **n, c, m, M** parameters; **n = sample size, c = acceptance number, m = maximum acceptable, M = upper threshold.** - **Risk Analysis Framework:** **Risk Assessment → Risk Management → Risk Communication** (Codex 2003). - **Key Inspection Principles (CAC/GL 30):** Risk-based, transparent, equivalent, non-discriminatory, science-based. - **For KTU: Always cite the document number and year** for full marks. - **Avoid generic statements; use numerical values and regulatory citations.**

topics.title: Principles of Food Commodity Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT758 - FOOD SAFETY AND SANITATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Food Commodity Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principles of Food Commodity Storage** can be formally defined as the scientific discipline that applies physico-chemical, biological, and engineering principles to preserve the quality, safety, nutritional value, and economic value of food raw materials and processed products from the point of harvest/manufacture until final consumption, by manipulating environmental parameters such as temperature, relative humidity, atmospheric composition, and storage duration.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, food commodity storage encompasses the **systematic control of intrinsic and extrinsic factors** that influence post-harvest/post-production deterioration. It is built upon the foundational understanding that food is a **perishable biological substrate** and that degradation is governed by:

1. **Intrinsic parameters** — water activity ($a_w$), pH, redox potential ($E_h$), enzymatic activity, microbial load, and chemical composition.
2. **Extrinsic parameters** — storage temperature ($T$), relative humidity (RH), gaseous atmosphere ($O_2$, $CO_2$, $N_2$, $C_2H_4$), light exposure, and sanitation status of the storage environment.

The **Unit Operations of Storage** form a critical link in the food supply chain — bridging production, processing, distribution, and consumption — and directly influence **food safety**, **food security**, and **public health**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Time Capsule" Concept**

Imagine food commodity storage as placing a living, breathing system into a carefully designed **time capsule**.

Think of freshly harvested rice, a bunch of bananas, and a piece of cheese. Each is a tiny biological universe containing:
- **Water** (the medium where all reactions happen),
- **Nutrients** (food for microbes and enzymes),
- **Living cells** (that continue respiration and ripening),
- **Microorganisms** (waiting for the right conditions to multiply).

Storage is essentially the art of **slowing down the universe's clock**. Just as refrigeration slows a ticker's metabolism, **lowering temperature**, **reducing moisture**, **excluding oxygen**, and **controlling pests** all work together to "pause" the biological clock inside food.

The four enemies of stored food are:
- 🦠 **Microbes** (bacteria, molds, yeasts)
- 🌡️ **Temperature abuse** (accelerated reactions)
- 💧 **Moisture migration** (spoilage, caking)
- 🐛 **Pests** (insects, rodents, mites)

Effective storage is essentially a **four-front war** against these enemies.

---

## 1.3 Core Standard Metrics in Food Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorize**

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value/Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water Activity | $a_w$ | 0.0 – 1.0 (dimensionless) | Microbial growth threshold |
| Safe $a_w$ for bacteria | $a_w$ | **< 0.86** | Inhibits most pathogenic bacteria |
| Safe $a_w$ for yeasts | $a_w$ | **< 0.70** | Inhibits most yeasts |
| Safe $a_w$ for molds | $a_w$ | **< 0.60** | Inhibits most xerophilic molds |
| Safe storage moisture (cereals) | $m$ | **≤ 14%** wet basis | Critical for long-term grain storage |
| Equilibrium Relative Humidity | ERH | $= a_w \times 100$ (%) | Defines ambient humidity equilibrium |
| Recommended RH for dried foods | RH | **55–70%** | Prevents moisture pickup |
| Recommended RH for fresh produce | RH | **85–95%** | Prevents wilting |
| Cold chain temperature | $T$ | **0 – 4 °C** | Chilled storage |
| Frozen storage temperature | $T$ | **≤ −18 °C** | Frozen storage |
| Controlled Atmosphere $O_2$ | $O_2$ | **1–5%** | For apples, pears |
| Modified Atmosphere $CO_2$ | $CO_2$ | **5–20%** | For MAP of fresh produce |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Food Commodities by Storage Requirements

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to **classify foods by perishability**. Memorize the categories below — it is a guaranteed high-yield topic.

### 1.4.1 By Perishability
1. **Highly Perishable** — Fish, meat, milk, fresh berries, leafy vegetables (shelf life: days)
2. **Semi-perishable** — Potatoes, apples, cheese, eggs (shelf life: weeks to months)
3. **Non-perishable (Shelf-stable)** — Cereals, pulses, sugar, salt, dried legumes, oils (shelf life: months to years)

### 1.4.2 By Storage Temperature Requirement
1. **Ambient Storage** — Cereal grains, pulses, dried foods, canned goods
2. **Cool Storage** — Potatoes, onions, citrus fruits (10–15 °C)
3. **Chilled Storage** — Fresh fruits, vegetables, dairy, fresh meat (0–4 °C)
4. **Frozen Storage** — Meat, fish, prepared foods, ice cream (≤ −18 °C)
5. **Controlled Atmosphere Storage (CA)** — Apples, kiwifruit, pears (0 °C + modified gases)

---

## 1.5 Why Food Storage Matters — The KTU Perspective

Food losses in developing nations including India are alarmingly high — estimated at **30–40%** for fruits and vegetables and **10–15%** for cereals (as per ICAR and FAO post-harvest loss reports). Effective storage:
- Reduces **food insecurity**
- Maintains **nutritional adequacy**
- Stabilizes **market prices** (price seasonality elimination)
- Supports **export competitiveness**
- Prevents **mycotoxin contamination** (e.g., aflatoxin in maize, ochratoxin in stored coffee)
- Aligns with **FSSAI regulations** and **Codex Alimentarius** standards

---

## 1.6 Module 2 Scope Recap

This topic under Module 2 typically covers:
- Principles governing storage of **grains/cereals, pulses, oilseeds**
- Storage of **fruits and vegetables** (cold storage, CA/MAP)
- Storage of **dairy products**
- Storage of **meat, fish, poultry**
- Storage of **processed and packaged foods**
- **Warehousing** principles, **Fumigation**, **Insect control**, **Storage losses**

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles Governing Food Deterioration During Storage

The science of food storage is built on the understanding of **deterioration kinetics**. Five primary mechanisms cause loss of food quality during storage:

### 2.1.1 Microbial Deterioration
Microbes (bacteria, yeasts, molds) grow when:
$$a_w > a_{w,\text{min}}, \quad T \in [T_{\text{min}}, T_{\text{max}}], \quad \text{pH} \in [\text{pH}_{\text{min}}, \text{pH}_{\text{max}}]$$

The **water activity principle** is foundational — no microorganism can grow below its critical $a_w$ threshold.

### 2.1.2 Chemical Deterioration
- **Lipid oxidation** — produces rancidity
- **Non-enzymatic browning** (Maillard reaction)
- **Vitamin degradation** (especially vitamin C)
- **Pigment breakdown** (chlorophyll → pheophytin)

These reactions generally follow:
$$\text{Rate} = k \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$$
(Arrhenius-type kinetics)

### 2.1.3 Enzymatic Deterioration
Native enzymes (lipases, proteases, polyphenol oxidases, pectinases) continue activity post-harvest causing softening, browning, off-flavors.

### 2.1.4 Physical Deterioration
- Moisture migration (caking, crust formation)
- Crystallization (sugar bloom, fat bloom in chocolate)
- Mechanical damage
- Freezer burn (sublimation loss)

### 2.1.5 Biological Deterioration (Pests)
- Insects (weevils, beetles, moths)
- Rodents
- Mites
- Birds

---

## 2.2 Core Principles of Storage (The Five Pillars)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The five foundational principles form the cornerstone of all food storage systems and are heavily tested in KTU exams.

### Principle 1: **Temperature Control (T)**
Temperature is the **single most powerful lever** in food storage. The **$Q_{10}$ rule** states that for every 10 °C rise in temperature, the rate of chemical/microbial reactions approximately **doubles or triples**.

$$Q_{10} = \frac{k_{(T+10)}}{k_T} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3$$

For perishables: storage at **0 °C** vs **10 °C** can extend shelf life by **2–3 fold**.

### Principle 2: **Moisture / Water Activity Control ($a_w$)**
Lowering $a_w$ below the critical microbial threshold inhibits growth. Achieved via:
- Drying/dehydration
- Freezing (immobilizes water as ice)
- Addition of solutes (sugar, salt — via **Raoult's Law**)

$$\ln(a_w) = -\frac{V_w \cdot \Pi}{RT} \approx -\nu \cdot k_m \cdot m$$

Where $\nu$ = dissociation constant, $k_m$ = molal elevation constant, $m$ = molality.

### Principle 3: **Atmospheric / Gaseous Control**
- **Aerobic** conditions favor molds and aerobic bacteria
- **Anaerobic** conditions favor Clostridium, lactic acid bacteria
- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** typically uses 1–5% $O_2$, 5–20% $CO_2$, balance $N_2$
- **Controlled Atmosphere (CA)** maintains precise gas ratios dynamically

### Principle 4: **Sanitation & Pest Exclusion**
- Hermetic sealing
- Fumigation (aluminum phosphide → phosphine gas, $PH_3$)
- Insect-proof packaging
- Rodent control programs

### Principle 5: **Time Minimization**
- First-In-First-Out (FIFO)
- Just-In-Time (JIT) inventory
- Reducing storage chain length

---

## 2.3 Storage of Cereals and Pulses — A Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Cereal Grain Storage
Cereals (rice, wheat, maize, sorghum, millet) are stored as **dry commodities** with $a_w$ **< 0.65** (typically moisture content **< 14% w.b.**).

**Critical Moisture Calculation:**
$$M_{\text{safe}} = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{1 + \frac{14}{86}}\right) = 14\%$$

**Storage Structures:**
- **Bag storage** (jute bags, 50–100 kg) — short term
- **Bulk silos** (metal/concrete) — long term, mechanized
- **CAP storage (Cover and Plinth)** — outdoor covered storage
- **Hermetic storage** (Purdue Improved Crop Storage — PICS bags, Cocoons) — for smallholder farmers

### 2.3.2 Safe Storage Moisture by Grain Type

| Commodity | Safe Moisture (%) | Critical $a_w$ |
|---|---|---|
| Rice (milled) | **≤ 14** | 0.65 |
| Wheat | **≤ 13–14** | 0.65 |
| Maize (corn) | **≤ 13–14** | 0.65 |
| Sorghum | **≤ 12–13** | 0.62 |
| Pulses (lentils, chickpeas) | **≤ 10–12** | 0.60 |
| Oilseeds (mustard, groundnut) | **≤ 8–10** | 0.55 |

### 2.3.3 Grain Respiration & Heat Generation
Stored grains are not dead — they respire. Respiration produces heat and moisture:
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{Energy (heat)}$$

This **interstitial heat buildup** can create "hot spots" in bulk silos, leading to localized spoilage and even **spontaneous combustion** in oilseeds (moisture content **> 14%**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often assume grain is "dead" and inert. WRONG. Grains are living seeds with measurable respiration, which is why silo monitoring (temperature cables, $CO_2$ sensors) is critical.

---

## 2.4 Storage of Fruits and Vegetables

### 2.4.1 Post-Harvest Physiology
Fruits and vegetables are **living tissues** that continue:
- **Respiration** ($O_2$ in, $CO_2$ out)
- **Transpiration** (water loss → wilting)
- **Ripening** (ethylene-driven climacteric vs non-climacteric)

**Respiration Rate Classification:**
- Low: Apple, citrus, grape, potato (2–4 mL $CO_2$ /kg·h at 5 °C)
- Moderate: Banana, peach, tomato (5–10)
- High: Strawberry, mushroom, broccoli (10–20)
- Very High: Asparagus, pea, sweet corn (> 20)

### 2.4.2 Climacteric vs Non-Climacteric

| Type | Climacteric Fruits | Non-Climacteric Fruits |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Show respiratory burst & ethylene peak at ripening | No respiratory burst; no ethylene peak |
| Examples | Banana, apple, tomato, mango, papaya | Citrus, grape, strawberry, pineapple, cherry |
| Storage strategy | Harvest at mature-green; control ripening via $C_2H_4$ | Harvest ripe; minimize delays |

### 2.4.3 Cold Storage Conditions for Selected Produce

| Commodity | Optimum Temperature (°C) | Optimum RH (%) | Approx. Shelf Life |
|---|---|---|---|
| Apples (CA) | 0 – 4 | 90 – 95 | 6 – 12 months |
| Banana (green) | 13 – 15 | 85 – 90 | 1 – 3 weeks |
| Tomato (mature green) | 10 – 13 | 85 – 90 | 2 – 4 weeks |
| Potato (table) | 4 – 10 | 90 – 95 | 4 – 9 months |
| Onion (dry) | 0 – 4 | 65 – 70 | 6 – 8 months |
| Mango | 10 – 13 | 85 – 90 | 2 – 4 weeks |
| Citrus | 5 – 10 | 85 – 90 | 1 – 3 months |
| Grapes | 0 – 1 | 90 – 95 | 1 – 6 months |
| Leafy greens | 0 – 2 | 95 – 100 | 1 – 2 weeks |

### 2.4.4 Chilling Injury — The Critical Trap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Chilling injury occurs when tropical/subtropical produce is stored below their critical temperature (typically **< 10–13 °C** for tropical species). Symptoms include pitting, surface browning, failure to ripen, off-flavor.

**Chilling-sensitive commodities:** Banana, mango, papaya, pineapple, tomato (ripe), cucumber, sweet potato, avocado.

---

## 2.5 Controlled Atmosphere (CA) and Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)

### 2.5.1 Principles

**CA** = Storage in which the gas atmosphere is precisely maintained throughout (active dynamic control).
**MAP** = Pre-set gas mixture flushed into package at packing; passive drift thereafter.

**Typical CA Conditions for Apples:**
- $O_2$: **1.5–2.5%**
- $CO_2$: **1–3%**
- $N_2$: balance
- $T$: **0 °C**
- RH: **90–95%**

### 2.5.2 Beneficial Effects of CA/MAP
- Reduces respiration rate
- Delays ripening (suppresses ethylene action)
- Inhibits microbial growth
- Preserves color, texture, nutritional quality
- Extends shelf life **2 to 4 times** compared to air storage

### 2.5.3 Gas Permeability Equation
For MAP film with permeability $P$:
$$\text{Gas transmission rate} = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

where $A$ = area, $L$ = film thickness, $\Delta p$ = partial pressure difference.

---

## 2.6 Storage of Dairy Products

| Product | Storage Temp (°C) | Shelf Life | Critical Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw milk | 0 – 4 | 1 – 2 days | Highly perishable; cold chain critical |
| Pasteurized milk | 2 – 4 | 5 – 7 days | Refrigerated, hygienic container |
| UHT milk | ambient / 4 | 3 – 6 months | Aseptic packaging |
| Butter | 0 – 4 | 1 – 3 months | Salted for longer life |
| Cheese (hard) | 4 – 8 | 6 – 24 months | Wax-coated for ripening |
| Cheese (soft) | 0 – 4 | 1 – 3 weeks | High moisture |
| Ghee | ambient | 6 – 12 months | Low $a_w$ (≤ 0.85) |
| Yogurt | 0 – 4 | 2 – 3 weeks | Live cultures |

---

## 2.7 Storage of Meat, Fish, and Poultry

### 2.7.1 Fresh Meat Storage
- **Chilled:** 0 – 1 °C, RH 85–90%, shelf life 5–7 days
- **Superchilled:** −1 to −2 °C (partial ice formation)
- **Frozen:** −18 °C, shelf life 6–12 months
- **Frozen at −30 °C:** Long-term, 12–24 months

### 2.7.2 Fish Storage
Fish is **most perishable** due to:
- High $a_w$ (0.99+)
- Neutral pH (~6.5)
- High unsaturated fat (oxidation)
- Rapid microbial autolysis
- High non-protein nitrogen

**Methods:**
- **Ice chilling** (1:1 fish:ice ratio)
- **Refrigerated sea water (RSW)**
- **Modified atmosphere** (60% $CO_2$ + 40% $N_2$)
- **Freezing** at **−35 °C** then storage at **−25 to −30 °C**

### 2.7.3 Poultry
- Chilled: 0 – 4 °C, 5–7 days
- Frozen: −18 °C, 9–12 months
- Critical: **Salmonella, Campylobacter** control

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is your master cheat sheet for numerical problems in KTU examinations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Water Activity → Moisture Relationship | $a_w = p/p_0 = f(\text{MC}, T)$ | dimensionless | Predicting storage stability |
| 2 | Sorption Isotherm | $m = f(a_w, T)$ | % d.b. | Selecting storage RH |
| 3 | Equilibrium RH | $\text{ERH} = a_w \times 100$ | % | Storage RH decision |
| 4 | Arrhenius Equation | $k = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | varies | Shelf life prediction |
| 5 | $Q_{10}$ Temperature Coefficient | $Q_{10} = (k_2/k_1)^{10/(T_2-T_1)}$ | dimensionless | Effect of temperature |
| 6 | Shelf Life Ratio | $\theta_2/\theta_1 = Q_{10}^{(T_1-T_2)/10}$ | days/weeks | Temperature effects |
| 7 | Heat Transfer in Cold Store | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W (or kJ/h) | Refrigeration load |
| 8 | Latent Heat of Freezing | $Q_f = m \cdot L_f$ | kJ | Freezer capacity |
| 9 | Psychrometric Relation | $W = 0.622 \cdot (p_v/(p-p_v))$ | kg water/kg dry air | Cold storage humidification |
| 10 | Refrigeration Load | $Q_{total} = Q_{prod} + Q_{trans} + Q_{res}$ | W | Cold room design |
| 11 | Respiration Heat | $R = r \cdot V_c \cdot 10.7$ | mW/kg | Live produce cooling |
| 12 | Gas Transmission (Film) | $\text{GTR} = (P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p)/L$ | cm³/m²·day | MAP design |
| 13 | Moisture Loss | $\Delta m = K \cdot (p_{int}-p_{ext}) \cdot A \cdot t$ | kg | Transpiration control |
| 14 | Equilibrium Moisture (Henderson) | $1 - RH = \exp(-c \cdot T \cdot M^n)$ | various | Grain drying/storage |
| 15 | Mycelial Growth Threshold | $a_w < 0.65$ for xerophiles | dimensionless | Mold prevention |

> **Memory Anchor:** "AQUIReST" — Arrhenius, $Q_{10}$, U-value, Respiration, Storage time.

---

## 2.9 Storage Pests and Their Control

### 2.9.1 Major Stored Grain Pests
- **Rice weevil** (*Sitophilus oryzae*) — primary pest of rice/wheat
- **Khapra beetle** (*Trogoderma granarium*) — quarantine pest
- **Indian meal moth** (*Plodia interpunctella*) — surface feeder
- **Red flour beetle** (*Tribolium castaneum*) — secondary pest
- **Lesser grain borer** (*Rhyzopertha dominica*)
- **Rodents:** *Rattus rattus*, *R. norvegicus*, *Mus musculus*

### 2.9.2 Pest Control Methods

| Method | Examples | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Hermetic storage, sieving, freezing | Exclusion, mortality |
| **Mechanical** | Traps, light traps, sonic deterrents | Capture/repulsion |
| **Chemical (Fumigation)** | Aluminum phosphide → $PH_3$, Methyl bromide (banned), Ethylene oxide | Respiratory poison |
| **Controlled Atmosphere** | High $CO_2$ (> 60%), Low $O_2$ (< 2%) | Suffocation |
| **Biological** | *Trichogramma*, parasitoid wasps | Predation/parasitism |
| **Botanical** | Neem oil, eucalyptus | Repellency, anti-feedant |
| **Radiation** | Gamma irradiation | Sterilization (approved dose: 0.15–0.7 kGy for disinfestation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Methyl bromide is a Montreal Protocol-controlled substance — **banned** due to ozone depletion. Phosphine ($PH_3$) from aluminum phosphide is the modern fumigant of choice.

### 2.9.3 Aluminum Phosphide Fumigation Chemistry
$$2\text{AlP} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{PH}_3 \uparrow$$

The liberated phosphine gas ($PH_3$) is a potent respiratory inhibitor for insects.

---

## 2.10 Warehouse Management Principles

### 2.10.1 Key Warehouse Requirements
1. **Site selection** — elevated, well-drained, away from flood zones
2. **Construction** — rodent-proof, damp-proof, well-ventilated
3. **Layout** — proper spacing, aisles, FIFO flow
4. **Temperature & humidity control** — appropriate for commodity
5. **Pest surveillance** — pheromone traps, sticky boards
6. **Fire safety** — sprinklers, alarms, no smoking
7. **Documentation** — stock registers, FSSAI records

### 2.10.2 FSSAI Warehouse Standards (India)
- Registration under **FSSAI License** for storage of food products
- Compliance with **Schedule 4** of FSSAI (Hygiene & Sanitation)
- Adoption of **HACCP** principles for high-risk foods
- Compliance with **BIS standards** for warehousing (IS 6076, IS 6159, IS 14256)

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Water Activity and Moisture Content (BET Sorption Model)

The **Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation** describes the sorption behavior of food:

$$\frac{a_w}{(1-a_w) \cdot M} = \frac{1}{M_0 \cdot C} + \frac{(C-1) \cdot a_w}{M_0 \cdot C}$$

Where:
- $a_w$ = water activity (dimensionless)
- $M$ = moisture content (% d.b.) at given $a_w$
- $M_0$ = **monolayer moisture content** (%, the most stable moisture)
- $C$ = BET constant (related to heat of sorption)

> **Engineering significance:** Foods stored at moisture levels corresponding to $M_0$ have maximum storage stability. Above $M_0$, free water becomes available for microbial/enzymatic activity.

### Derivation Steps (Logic):

**Step 1:** Start with general gas adsorption theory on surfaces.
The rate of condensation on empty surface sites = rate of evaporation from filled monolayer.

$$k_c \cdot p \cdot (1-\theta) = k_e \cdot \theta \cdot e^{E_1/RT}$$

**Step 2:** Extend to multilayer adsorption.
The BET equation sums condensation onto all $n$ layers.

**Step 3:** Normalize by total moisture sorbed:
$$\frac{a_w}{(1-a_w) M} = \frac{1}{M_0 C} + \frac{(C-1)a_w}{M_0 C}$$

**Step 4:** Plot $\frac{a_w}{(1-a_w)M}$ vs $a_w$:
- Linear region: $0.05 < a_w < 0.35$
- Slope = $\frac{C-1}{M_0 C}$
- Intercept = $\frac{1}{M_0 C}$

**Step 5:** Solve for $M_0$:
$$M_0 = \frac{1}{(\text{Slope} + \text{Intercept})}$$

> **Real-world use:** $M_0$ is used to identify the **optimum safe storage moisture** for cereals, where shelf life is maximized.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shelf Life Using $Q_{10}$ Concept

Given: $Q_{10}$ is defined as the ratio of reaction rates at temperatures differing by 10 °C.

$$Q_{10} = \frac{k_{T+10}}{k_T}$$

For temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ differing by $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$:

$$Q_{\Delta T} = \left(\frac{k_{T_2}}{k_{T_1}}\right) = Q_{10}^{\Delta T/10}$$

Since shelf life $\theta$ is inversely proportional to rate constant $k$:
$$\theta \propto \frac{1}{k}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = Q_{10}^{(T_1 - T_2)/10}$$

### Worked Example:

**Problem:** A fresh milk sample has a shelf life of **7 days at 5 °C**. If stored at **15 °C**, what is the expected shelf life? Assume $Q_{10} = 3$.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $\theta_1 = 7$ days
- $T_1 = 5$ °C
- $T_2 = 15$ °C
- $Q_{10} = 3$

Step 1: Calculate temperature difference.
$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 15 - 5 = 10 \text{ °C}$$

Step 2: Apply shelf life equation.
$$\frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = Q_{10}^{(T_1 - T_2)/10}$$

$$\frac{\theta_2}{7} = 3^{(5-15)/10} = 3^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Step 3: Solve for $\theta_2$.
$$\theta_2 = \frac{7}{3} = 2.33 \text{ days}$$

> **Interpretation:** A 10 °C rise reduces shelf life from **7 days to 2.33 days** — a 67% reduction. This is precisely why cold chain integrity is non-negotiable for dairy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Refrigeration Load for a Cold Store

The total refrigeration load ($Q_{total}$) for a cold storage chamber comprises:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{transmission} + Q_{product} + Q_{respiration} + Q_{misc}$$

### 3.3.1 Transmission Heat Load ($Q_{trans}$)

Heat conducted through walls, ceiling, floor:
$$Q_{trans} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = surface area (m²)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference (K)

For composite walls:
$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum \frac{L_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

### 3.3.2 Product Heat Load ($Q_{product}$)

$$Q_{product} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_f \cdot x$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of product (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat (kJ/kg·K) — for foods ≈ **3.5 kJ/kg·K** above freezing
- $L_f$ = latent heat of freezing (**~335 kJ/kg** for water-bound water)
- $x$ = fraction of water frozen
- $\Delta T$ = temperature reduction (K)

### 3.3.3 Respiration Heat Load ($Q_{res}$)

For living produce:
$$Q_{res} = m \cdot r \cdot E_{resp}$$

Where:
- $r$ = respiration rate (mL $CO_2$/kg·h)
- $E_{resp}$ = energy equivalent (~**10.7 J per mL $CO_2$**)

### 3.3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Design a cold store for 5 tonnes of apples. Calculate the daily heat load.

**Given:**
- Storage temp = 0 °C
- Outside temp = 35 °C
- Product entry temp = 20 °C
- $c_p$ of apples = **3.6 kJ/kg·K**
- $r$ = 3 mL $CO_2$/kg·h at 0 °C
- Surface area $A$ = 50 m², $U$ = 0.4 W/m²·K

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Transmission load.
$$Q_{trans} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.4 \times 50 \times (35 - 0) = 700 \text{ W}$$

Convert to kJ/day:
$$Q_{trans} = 700 \times 86400 / 1000 = 60{,}480 \text{ kJ/day}$$

**Step 2:** Product cooling load.
$$Q_{prod} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 5000 \times 3.6 \times (20 - 0) = 360{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

(Assuming cooling completed in 1 day)

**Step 3:** Respiration load.
$$Q_{res} = m \cdot r \cdot E_{resp} = 5000 \times 3 \times 10.7 \text{ mW/kg equivalent}$$

Convert: $Q_{res} = 5000 \times 3 \times 10.7 \times 10^{-3}$ W = 160.5 W

In kJ/day:
$$Q_{res} = 160.5 \times 86.4 = 13{,}867 \text{ kJ/day}$$

**Step 4:** Total daily load (excluding misc).
$$Q_{total} = 60{,}480 + 360{,}000 + 13{,}867 = 434{,}347 \text{ kJ/day}$$

Add ~10% safety factor for misc loads:
$$Q_{design} = 434{,}347 \times 1.10 = 477{,}782 \text{ kJ/day}$$

Equivalent refrigeration tonnage:
$$\text{TR} = \frac{477{,}782}{3.517 \times 24 \times 60} = 0.94 \text{ TR}$$

(1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3.517 kJ/s)

> **Engineering Significance:** This calculation determines the compressor capacity, refrigerant pipe sizing, and energy cost — central to cold chain economics.

## 3.5 Conceptual Problem-Solving Framework for KTU Numericals

### Framework Template (Use this for every numerical):

1. **Identify the commodity** and its key parameters ($a_w$, $T_{opt}$, $RH_{opt}$, respiration rate).
2. **Identify the storage method** in question (CA, MAP, cold, ambient).
3. **Apply the relevant formula** (e.g., $Q_{10}$, $Q_{total}$, $a_w$).
4. **Substitute values with units**.
5. **Solve step by step**.
6. **Interpret the result** in engineering/food safety context.

> **Example Framework Application:** If asked about shelf life extension by temperature reduction → apply $Q_{10}$ equation. If asked about cold store sizing → apply $Q_{total}$ equation. If asked about fungal growth inhibition → apply $a_w$ threshold.

## 3.6 Derivation: Respiration Rate Determination

The respiration rate is measured by $CO_2$ evolution or $O_2$ consumption in a closed chamber:

$$r = \frac{\Delta C_{CO_2} \cdot V}{m \cdot \Delta t}$$

Where:
- $\Delta C_{CO_2}$ = change in $CO_2$ concentration (mL/L)
- $V$ = free volume of chamber (L)
- $m$ = mass of produce (kg)
- $\Delta t$ = time interval (h)

> **Application:** Standard method for grading produce into "low," "moderate," "high," "very high" respiration classes for cold storage design.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define water activity. Explain its significance in food storage with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — crisp, KTU-valuation-style):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Water activity ($a_w$) is defined as the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a food ($p$) to the vapor pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature:

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity ranging from 0 (bone dry) to 1.0 (pure water).

**Significance (2 marks):**
- $a_w$ determines **microbial growth** — most bacteria need $a_w > 0.86$, yeasts $> 0.70$, molds $> 0.60$.
- $a_w$ governs **chemical reactions** — lipid oxidation is lowest at monolayer moisture ($a_w$ ~0.2–0.3); Maillard browning peaks at $a_w$ 0.6–0.8.
- It is a **better indicator of perishability** than total moisture content.

**Examples:**
- Dry grains stored at $a_w < 0.65$ → safe from mold and bacterial spoilage.
- Jams and jellies with high sugar → low $a_w$ (~0.80) → shelf stable.
- Fresh meat ($a_w > 0.98$) → requires refrigeration.

> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award **0.5 marks for definition**, **1 mark each** for two valid significance points, **0.5 for example**. Don't skip the formula — it's free marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between Controlled Atmosphere (CA) storage and Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CA Storage | MAP |
|---|---|---|
| Gas control | Continuously monitored and adjusted dynamically | Pre-set at packaging; passive drift |
| Application scale | Bulk warehouse / cold rooms | Individual retail packages |
| Equipment cost | Very high (gas analyzers, scrubbers) | Moderate |
| Precision | ±0.1% precision | ±2–5% typical |
| Examples | Apple, kiwifruit, pear | Fresh-cut salads, packaged meat |

> **One-line Definition (KTU tip — write this first):** CA = **dynamic** gas control in bulk; MAP = **static** pre-mixed gas in package.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — **14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **principles and methods of storage of food grains** with reference to safe moisture content, temperature, and pest control measures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cold storage facility is to be designed for **10 tonnes of apples**. The product enters at **25 °C** and must be cooled to **0 °C** within 24 hours. Given:
- Specific heat of apple = 3.6 kJ/kg·K
- Respiration heat = 4 mL $CO_2$/kg·h at 0 °C
- Energy equivalent of $CO_2$ = 10.7 J/mL
- Cold room surface area = 80 m²
- $U$-value = 0.35 W/m²·K
- Ambient temperature = 35 °C

Calculate the **total refrigeration load in kJ/day** and **compressor capacity in TR (Tons of Refrigeration)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks)

**(i) Introduction to Grain Storage (1 mark):**
Food grains (cereals, pulses, oilseeds) are stored in dry form to maintain $a_w$ below the microbial growth threshold and prevent quantitative + qualitative losses.

**(ii) Safe Moisture Content (2 marks):**

| Commodity | Safe Moisture (% w.b.) |
|---|---|
| Rice (milled) | ≤ 14 |
| Wheat | ≤ 13–14 |
| Maize | ≤ 13–14 |
| Pulses | ≤ 10–12 |
| Oilseeds | ≤ 8–10 |

The safe moisture is the level at which $a_w \leq 0.65$, preventing mold and insect activity.

**(iii) Temperature Control (1 mark):**
Maintain storage temperature below **25 °C** to minimize respiration and pest proliferation. Insect metabolism halts below ~13 °C; below 10 °C, reproduction ceases.

**(iv) Pest Control Measures (2 marks):**

1. **Fumigation with Aluminum Phosphide** — releases $PH_3$ gas.
$$2\text{AlP} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{PH}_3$$

2. **Hermetic Storage** — PICS bags, IRRI Super Bags — creates low $O_2$ / high $CO_2$ atmosphere naturally.

3. **Controlled Atmosphere** — High $CO_2$ (> 60%) for 2–4 weeks → kills all life stages.

4. **Biological Control** — *Trichogramma* wasps, *Beauveria bassiana* fungal biopesticide.

5. **Mechanical** — grain cleaning, sieving, sun-drying, traps.

**(v) Conclusion (1 mark):**
Proper grain storage protects **food security**, **export value**, and **public health** by preventing mycotoxin contamination (e.g., aflatoxin in maize).

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Mass $m$ = 10,000 kg
- Initial $T_i$ = 25 °C, Final $T_f$ = 0 °C
- $c_p$ = 3.6 kJ/kg·K
- $r$ = 4 mL $CO_2$/kg·h
- $E_{resp}$ = 10.7 J/mL
- $A$ = 80 m², $U$ = 0.35 W/m²·K
- $T_{amb}$ = 35 °C, $T_{cold}$ = 0 °C

**Step 1: Transmission Load (2 marks)**

$$Q_{trans} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$
$$Q_{trans} = 0.35 \times 80 \times (35 - 0) = 980 \text{ W}$$

Convert to kJ/day:
$$Q_{trans} = 980 \times \frac{86400}{1000} = 84{,}672 \text{ kJ/day}$$

**Step 2: Product Cooling Load (3 marks)**

$$Q_{prod} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$
$$Q_{prod} = 10{,}000 \times 3.6 \times (25 - 0)$$
$$Q_{prod} = 900{,}000 \text{ kJ/day}$$

**Step 3: Respiration Load (2 marks)**

$$Q_{res} = m \cdot r \cdot E_{resp} \cdot \text{conversion}$$
$$Q_{res} = 10{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 4 \text{ mL/kg·h} \times 10.7 \text{ J/mL}$$

First, energy per second:
$$Q_{res} = 10{,}000 \times 4 \times 10.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 428 \text{ W}$$

Convert to kJ/day:
$$Q_{res} = 428 \times 86.4 = 36{,}979 \text{ kJ/day}$$

**Step 4: Total Refrigeration Load**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{trans} + Q_{prod} + Q_{res}$$
$$Q_{total} = 84{,}672 + 900{,}000 + 36{,}979$$
$$Q_{total} = 1{,}021{,}651 \text{ kJ/day}$$

Add **10% safety factor** for door openings, lights, occupancy:
$$Q_{design} = 1{,}021{,}651 \times 1.10 = 1{,}123{,}816 \text{ kJ/day}$$

**Step 5: Compressor Capacity in TR**

1 TR (Ton of Refrigeration) = 3.517 kW = 3.517 kJ/s

Convert total load to kJ/s:
$$\text{kJ/s} = \frac{1{,}123{,}816}{86{,}400} = 13.01 \text{ kJ/s}$$

Convert to TR:
$$\text{TR} = \frac{13.01}{3.517} = 3.70 \text{ TR}$$

> **Final Answer:** $Q_{total} \approx$ **1.02 × 10⁶ kJ/day**, Compressor = **~3.70 TR** (recommended: select a **4 TR** unit).
