## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Lab Contaminant" in the context of food safety analysis. List any **four** major categories of lab contaminants with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **lab contaminant** is any foreign physical, chemical, biological, or radiological agent that is inadvertently introduced into a food sample during **pre-analytical, analytical, or post-analytical** stages in a food testing laboratory, compromising the validity of the test result.

**Four major categories with examples:**

| Category | Example |
|----------|---------|
| Physical | Glass fragments, dust, hair |
| Chemical | Solvent residues, BPA from plasticware |
| Biological | Bacterial spores (Bacillus subtilis) |
| Radiological | Trace ¹³⁷Cs isotopes (rare) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the difference between **% Recovery** and **LOD** in analytical method validation? State the FSSAI-accepted range for % Recovery in pesticide residue analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> - **% Recovery** measures the **accuracy** of an analytical method by comparing the observed concentration of a spiked sample to the known spiked concentration, expressed as a percentage. The **FSSAI-accepted range is 80–120%**.
> 
> - **LOD (Limit of Detection)** is the **lowest concentration** of an analyte that can be reliably distinguished from background noise, calculated as $LOD = \mu_{blank} + 3.3 \cdot \sigma_{blank}$. It represents the **sensitivity floor** of the method.
> 
> Recovery = **accuracy indicator**; LOD = **sensitivity indicator**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **sources of lab contaminants** in a food testing laboratory. Classify them using the **"5M + E" framework** and explain how each source can be controlled. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food testing lab performed a recovery study on a **milk sample** spiked with **lead (Pb) at 1.0 mg/kg**. The observed value was **0.87 mg/kg**, blank reading **0.02 mg/kg**, and standard deviation of 10 blank readings **0.005 mg/kg**. Calculate the **% Recovery, LOD, and LOQ**, and comment on the FSSAI compliance of the method. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sources of Lab Contaminants (7 marks):**

The **"5M + E" framework** adapted from Ishikawa's fishbone model is universally used in ISO/IEC 17025-accredited food labs to trace contamination sources.

**1. Man (Analyst) — 2 marks:**
- Skin flora, respiratory droplets (sneeze releases ~40,000 droplets at 15 m/s).
- Cosmetics, hand lotions, perfumes.
- **Control:** Strict PPE (nitrile gloves, lab coat, hair net, mask), 70% ethanol hand sanitization, restricted lab access during sample handling.

**2. Machine (Equipment) — 1.5 marks:**
- GC-MS injection port carryover, HPLC column bleed, balance drift.
- **Control:** Routine calibration, blank runs between samples, maintenance logs.

**3. Material (Reagents/Glassware) — 1.5 marks:**
- Plasticware leaches BPA, phthalates; Type III glassware leaches Na⁺/Ca²⁺.
- **Control:** Use HPLC-grade solvents, Type I borosilicate glass, acid-washed (10% HNO₃) glassware, dedicated reagent stocks.

**4. Method (Procedure) — 1 mark:**
- Improper blank correction, inadequate homogenization.
- **Control:** SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures), validated methods, internal audits.

**5. Mother Nature (Environment) — 0.5 marks:**
- Lab air particulates, humidity, temperature fluctuations.
- **Control:** HEPA filtration, climate control (20–25°C, 40–60% RH).

**6. Environment (Cross-Contamination) — 0.5 marks:**
- Shared equipment, foot traffic, open waste bins.
- **Control:** Zoning (high vs. low concentration areas), unidirectional workflow, sealed waste containers.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $C_{spiked} = 1.0\ \text{mg/kg}$
- $C_{observed} = 0.87\ \text{mg/kg}$
- $C_{blank} = 0.02\ \text{mg/kg}$
- $\sigma_{blank} = 0.005\ \text{mg/kg}$

**Step 1: % Recovery (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%R &= \frac{C_{observed} - C_{blank}}{C_{spiked}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{0.87 - 0.02}{1.0} \times 100 \\
&= 0.85 \times 100 = 85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: LOD (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LOD &= \mu_{blank} + 3.3 \cdot \sigma_{blank} \\
&= 0.02 + 3.3 \times 0.005 \\
&= 0.02 + 0.0165 \\
&= 0.0365\ \text{mg/kg} \approx 0.037\ \text{mg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: LOQ (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LOQ &= \mu_{blank} + 10 \cdot \sigma_{blank} \\
&= 0.02 + 10 \times 0.005 \\
&= 0.02 + 0.05 = 0.07\ \text{mg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: FSSAI Compliance Check (1 mark)**

| Parameter | Calculated | FSSAI Standard | Status |
|-----------|------------|----------------|--------|
| % Recovery | 85% | 80–120% | ✅ Compliant |
| LOD | 0.037 mg/kg | < 0.02 mg/kg (FSSAI Pb MRL in milk = 0.02 mg/kg) | ⚠️ Above MRL — cannot reliably detect at regulatory limit |
| LOQ | 0.07 mg/kg | < 0.05 mg/kg | ⚠️ Marginal |

> **Conclusion:** While recovery is acceptable, the **LOD exceeds the FSSAI MRL for lead in milk (0.02 mg/kg)**. The method is **NOT fit-for-purpose** for regulatory compliance and requires **pre-concentration** (e.g., microwave digestion + ICP-MS) to lower LOD below 0.005 mg/kg.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** What is **GLP (Good Laboratory Practice)**? Explain its **ten core principles** as applicable to food testing laboratories. How does GLP help in controlling lab contaminants? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **rice sample** was tested for **pesticide residue (Malathion)** using GC-ECD. The **MRL prescribed by FSSAI** is **4.0 mg/kg**. The lab's GC-ECD reported **5.2 mg/kg**, but the **re-analysis on LC-MS/MS** gave **3.6 mg/kg**. Discuss the possible reasons for this discrepancy and calculate the **% Deviation**. What corrective actions should the lab undertake? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) GLP and Its 10 Core Principles (7 marks):**

> **GLP (Good Laboratory Practice)** is a quality system concerned with the **organizational process and conditions** under which non-clinical safety studies and analytical tests are planned, performed, monitored, recorded, reported, and archived. It is mandated by **OECD GLP Principles (1997, revised 2016)** and **Schedule L-II of the Drugs and Cosmetics Act (India)** for food testing labs.

**Ten Core Principles of GLP:**

1. **Test Facility Organization and Personnel** — Qualified, trained staff with documented responsibilities.
2. **Quality Assurance Program (QAU)** — Independent monitoring of every study.
3. **Facilities** — Adequate space, environmental control, separation of activities.
4. **Apparatus, Materials, and Reagents** — Calibrated instruments, certified reference materials.
5. **Test Systems** — Validated biological/chemical test systems.
6. **Test and Reference Items** — Characterized, stored, handled to prevent contamination.
7. **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** — Written, approved, version-controlled.
8. **Performance of Study** — Planned, conducted, and documented in raw data.
9. **Reporting of Study Results** — Final reports validated by QAU and study director.
10. **Storage and Retention of Records** — Archives maintained for **at least 10 years** for food studies.

> **How GLP Controls Lab Contaminants (2 marks):**
> - **SOPs** standardize every step, eliminating procedural variability.
> - **QAU audits** detect contamination events before report release.
> - **Equipment logs** ensure timely calibration, preventing false readings.
> - **Reference item storage** prevents degradation and cross-contamination.

---

**(b) Discrepancy Analysis — Malathion in Rice (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- MRL (FSSAI) = **4.0 mg/kg**
- GC-ECD result = **5.2 mg/kg** (apparent non-compliance)
- LC-MS/MS result = **3.6 mg/kg** (compliant)
- True value (LC-MS/MS is more selective) = **3.6 mg/kg**

**Step 1: % Deviation of GC-ECD (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% Deviation &= \frac{|GC\text{-}ECD - LC\text{-}MS/MS|}{LC\text{-}MS/MS} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{|5.2 - 3.6|}{3.6} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1.6}{3.6} \times 100 \\
&\approx 44.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is an **unacceptably high deviation** indicating a probable **false positive** from GC-ECD.

**Step 2: Possible Reasons for Discrepancy (3 marks)**

1. **Co-eluting matrix interferent** — GC-ECD is a non-specific detector; it responds to all **electron-capturing compounds**, including organochlorines or sulfur-containing volatiles in rice, producing a false-high signal.
2. **Contaminated GC inlet liner** — Septum bleed or previous high-concentration sample carryover.
3. **Incorrect blank correction** — Blank value not subtracted properly.
4. **Column degradation** — Active sites in the column cause peak tailing and over-integration.
5. **Matrix enhancement effect** — In GC-ECD, matrix components can enhance the analyte signal by **20–50%**, a well-documented phenomenon.

**Step 3: Corrective Actions (2 marks)**

1. **Switch primary method** to LC-MS/MS for confirmatory analysis (already done correctly).
2. **Use matrix-matched calibration** for GC-ECD to compensate for matrix enhancement.
3. **Replace inlet liner and septum**; condition column for 30 min at 250°C.
4. **Re-analyze in triplicate** with blank and spike controls.
5. **Document the incident** as a Non-Conformity Report (NCR) and trigger a QAU audit.
6. **Train analyst** on detector-specific limitations.

> **Final Verdict:** The rice sample is **COMPLIANT with FSSAI MRL (3.6 < 4.0 mg/kg)**. The GC-ECD result was a **false positive** due to matrix interference. The LC-MS/MS confirmatory result is the regulatory-accepted value.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — KTU Valuation Key Patterns**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract blank value** in % Recovery calculation — costs **1.5 marks** (students often write directly: 0.87/1.0 × 100 = 87% instead of 85%). Always apply $\%R = \frac{C_{observed} - C_{blank}}{C_{spiked}} \times 100$.
> 
> 2. **Confusing LOD with LOQ** — LOD uses **3.3σ**, LOQ uses **10σ**. Examiners specifically check whether the student knows the **3.3 vs. 10** multiplier.
> 
> 3. **Listing categories without examples** — In 3-mark definition questions, **listing 4 categories WITHOUT examples** invites partial marking (typically 1.5/3).
> 
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always state **mg/kg, µg/kg, %, ppm** alongside numerical values. Omission = **0.5 mark** deduction.
> 
> 5. **Writing "lab contaminant" and "food contaminant" interchangeably** — These are **distinct concepts**. Lab contaminants enter the food sample **inside the laboratory**, whereas food contaminants are present in the food **at source**.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting the "Why" in theory answers** — KTU 2024 scheme values **explanation over memorization**. A 7-mark question answered as a list (without explaining control measures) typically scores **4–5/7** maximum.
> 
> 7. **Not mentioning FSSAI/CAC/ISO 17025 standards** in regulatory questions — Standards act as **anchor keywords** and significantly boost marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Lab Contaminants (PESFT758 Module 4)**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Lab contaminants are **foreign agents** introduced into food samples **within the lab environment** (not in the field/supply chain).
- ☐ Four primary types: **Physical, Chemical, Biological, Radiological** + **Cross-contamination** as the fifth operational category.
- ☐ **5M + E framework**: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Mother Nature, Environment.
- ☐ **LOD** = $\mu_{blank} + 3.3 \cdot \sigma_{blank}$ (sensitivity).
- ☐ **LOQ** = $\mu_{blank} + 10 \cdot \sigma_{blank}$ (quantification floor).
- ☐ **% Recovery** = $\frac{C_{observed} - C_{blank}}{C_{spiked}} \times 100$; FSSAI range = **80–120%**.
- ☐ **Carryover coefficient** ($k_{co}$) must be **< 0.001** for trace analysis.
- ☐ **CCI** = $\frac{C_{residue}}{MRL}$; CCI > 1 = regulatory non-compliance.

**Regulatory & Quality Standards:**
- ☐ **ISO/IEC 17025:2017** — General requirements for lab competence.
- ☐ **OECD GLP Principles (1997, revised 2016)** — 10 core principles.
- ☐ **FSSAI** — Acceptable recovery **80–120%**, LOD < MRL/2.
- ☐ **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** — International MRL standards.
- ☐ **ISO 14644-1** — Cleanroom classification for food testing labs.

**High-Yield Keywords for KTU Answer Writing:**
- ☐ Blank correction, matrix-matched calibration, matrix enhancement effect.
- ☐ HEPA filtration, laminar flow hood, PPE.
- ☐ Acid-washed glassware, HPLC-grade solvents, Type I borosilicate.
- ☐ SOPs, QAU, NCR (Non-Conformity Report), chain of custody.
- ☐ GC-ECD non-specificity vs. LC-MS/MS selectivity.

**Common Numerical Questions to Practice:**
- ☐ % Recovery calculation (with and without blank subtraction).
- ☐ LOD and LOQ from blank mean and standard deviation.
- ☐ % Deviation between two methods.
- ☐ Carryover coefficient and CCI.
- ☐ Z-score for proficiency testing ($Z = \frac{x - x_{assigned}}{\sigma_{pt}}$).

**Conceptual Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ☐ Lab contaminants ≠ food contaminants ≠ environmental contaminants.
- ☐ High % Recovery does NOT guarantee low LOD.
- ☐ A single method (e.g., GC-ECD) is not enough for regulatory confirmation — always use a **second, more selective method** (LC-MS/MS) for confirmatory analysis.
- ☐ Cross-contamination is **preventable**, not eliminable — goal is **minimization**, not absolute removal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PI, PII, PIII Guidelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT758 - FOOD SAFETY AND SANITATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PI, PII, PIII Guidelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of PI, PII, PIII Guidelines

In the architecture of **Food Safety Management Systems (FSMS)** aligned with **Codex Alimentarius**, **ISO 22000**, and **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** regulatory frameworks, the term **PI, PII, PIII Guidelines** refers to the **three-tier classification of Pre-requisite Programs (PRPs)** that form the foundational operational layer upon which the **HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points)** system is built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Compliant):**
> The **P-I, P-II, P-III Guidelines** are the **hierarchically structured Pre-requisite Program (PRP) protocols** mandated by Codex Alimentarius (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020) and adopted by FSSAI under the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Safety Management System) Regulations, 2011**. They categorize the operational, infrastructural, and managerial controls in a food processing facility into **three logical tiers**, each addressing a distinct domain of food safety risk mitigation.

### The Three Tiers — At a Glance

| Tier | Full Form | Primary Domain |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| **P-I** | Pre-requisite Program – Infrastructure & Facility | Premises, Equipment, Sanitary Facilities |
| **P-II** | Pre-requisite Program – Operations & Process | Production, Process Control, Sanitation |
| **P-III** | Pre-requisive Program – People & Management | Personnel Hygiene, Training, Documentation, Recall |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Layer Cake" of Food Safety

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine you are building a **three-layer wedding cake** for a wedding. Before the cake can be served, three things must be guaranteed:
>
> 1. **The Bakery Kitchen (P-I)** — The walls must be clean, the oven must work, the floor must be hygienic, and the utensils must be food-grade. You cannot bake in a dirty kitchen.
> 2. **The Baking Process (P-II)** — The recipe must be followed precisely, the temperature must be controlled, the ingredients must be measured correctly, and the oven timer must be set.
> 3. **The Baker (P-III)** — The baker must wear clean clothes, a hairnet, wash hands, be trained in food hygiene, and maintain records of what was baked.
>
> Only when all three layers are perfect can you place the **decorative icing — the HACCP plan — on top**. PI, PII, PIII are the three "baking layers" without which HACCP would collapse.

## 1.3 Why Are PRPs Classified into Three Tiers?

The **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)**, **FSSAI**, and the **Global Food Safety Initiative (GFSI)** recognized that food safety risks arise from **three distinct sources**:

- **Source 1 — The Environment (P-I):** Poorly designed premises, contaminated water, inadequate equipment.
- **Source 2 — The Operation (P-II):** Cross-contamination during processing, incorrect time/temperature control, inadequate cleaning.
- **Source 3 — The Human & Management Factor (P-III):** Untrained staff, poor personal hygiene, missing records, weak traceability.

By segregating these into **P-I, P-II, P-III**, auditors and food safety teams can:
- **Systematically audit** each domain independently.
- **Assign responsibility** to specific departments (Engineering, Production, HR/Quality).
- **Trace root causes** of food safety failures to the correct tier.
- **Comply with FSSAI Schedule 4** requirements on hygienic and sanitary practices.

## 1.4 Regulatory Backing of PI/PII/PIII Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The P-I/P-II/P-III tiered PRP structure is mandated by:
> - **Codex Alimentarius** — *General Principles of Food Hygiene (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)*
> - **FSSAI** — *Food Safety and Standards (Licensing and Registration of Food Businesses) Regulations, 2011*
> - **ISO 22000:2018** — Clause 8 (Operation), specifically **8.2 (Prerequisite programmes)**
> - **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** — *IS 2491:2013* (Code for Hygiene in Food Processing Units)
> - **WHO** — *Five Keys to Safer Food* (used as PRP backbone)

The classification into **PI, PII, PIII** is **NOT an arbitrary academic division** — it is a **globally accepted industry convention** for structuring food safety audits and prerequisite program implementation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 P-I Guidelines — Infrastructure & Facility Pre-requisites

The **P-I tier** addresses the **static, physical, and engineering controls** of a food establishment. These are the **"bricks-and-mortar"** conditions that must exist *before* any food is processed.

### 2.1.1 Core Components of P-I

> [!NOTE]
> **P-I covers the following ten sub-domains (FSSAI Schedule 4, Part II):**

- **Premises Design & Layout** — Zoning (high-risk/low-risk), linear product flow, separation of raw and cooked areas.
- **Floors, Walls & Ceilings** — Smooth, non-absorbent, easy-to-clean, light-colored materials.
- **Lighting** — Minimum **300 lux** in inspection areas; **540 lux** in critical zones; protected bulbs to prevent glass contamination.
- **Ventilation** — Adequate airflow to prevent condensation; **15 air changes/hour** in processing rooms.
- **Water Supply** — **Potable water** with residual chlorine of **0.2–0.5 ppm**; tested every 6 months.
- **Drainage** — Sloped floors (1:60 gradient), back-flow preventers, covered drains.
- **Equipment & Utensils** — Food-grade stainless steel (**SS 304/316**), non-toxic, non-corroding.
- **Pest Control** — Rodent stations every **6–10 meters**, insectocutors at entry points.
- **Waste Management** — Segregated bins (color-coded), daily removal, designated waste yard.
- **Sanitary Facilities** — Separate toilets for men/women, foot-operated taps, hand-wash stations at every entry.

### 2.1.2 Engineering Standards for P-I

The following **boundary conditions** are commonly assessed in KTU examinations:

$$\text{Floor Gradient} = \frac{1}{60} \quad \text{(minimum slope for drainage)}$$

$$\text{Hand-wash Stations} \geq 1 \text{ per } 25 \text{ workers}$$

$$\text{Water Storage Capacity} = 2 \times \text{Daily Consumption}$$

## 2.2 P-II Guidelines — Operations & Process Pre-requisites

The **P-II tier** covers the **dynamic, process-level controls** — the procedures and operational routines that are executed **during** food production.

### 2.2.1 Core Components of P-II

> [!NOTE]
> **P-II covers the following operational sub-domains:**

- **Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SSOPs)** — Pre-operational and operational cleaning protocols.
- **Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP)** — Time-temperature control, allergen segregation, foreign body control.
- **Process Control** — Cooking temperatures (e.g., **≥ 75 °C** for meat products), chilling rates, hold times.
- **Cross-Contamination Control** — Color-coded chopping boards (red=raw, green=veg, blue=fish, yellow=cooked), separate utensils.
- **Pest Control Operations** — Fumigation schedule, baits, monitoring logs.
- **Chemical Control** — Cleaning chemicals (**quat-based, chlorine-based, iodine-based** sanitizers at correct concentrations).
- **Storage & Warehousing** — FIFO (First-In-First-Out), FEFO (First-Expiry-First-Out), cold chain maintenance.
- **Water & Ice Control** — Daily residual chlorine checks, ice sourced from potable water.
- **Allergen Management** — Separate storage, dedicated lines, validated cleaning between runs.
- **Process Water Recycle/Reuse** — Subject to microbiological validation.

### 2.2.2 Critical Operational Limits for P-II

$$\text{Cooking Core Temperature (Meat)} \geq 75 \, °C$$

$$\text{Hot Holding Temperature} \geq 63 \, °C$$

$$\text{Cold Holding Temperature} \leq 5 \, °C$$

$$\text{Freezer Temperature} \leq -18 \, °C$$

$$\text{Sanitizer Concentration (Chlorine)} = 50 - 200 \, ppm$$

$$\text{Sanitizer Concentration (Quaternary Ammonium)} = 200 - 400 \, ppm$$

## 2.3 P-III Guidelines — People & Management Pre-requisites

The **P-III tier** is often called the **"soft infrastructure"** of food safety. Even with perfect premises and flawless processes, food safety fails if **people are untrained, records are missing, or management is uninvolved**.

### 2.3.1 Core Components of P-III

> [!NOTE]
> **P-III covers the following human and managerial sub-domains:**

- **Personal Hygiene** — Hand-washing protocol, jewelry ban, hairnet, clean uniforms, illness reporting.
- **Medical Fitness** — Annual health check-ups, no open wounds, communicable disease screening.
- **Training & Competence** — **Minimum 16 hours/year** of food safety training per worker; HACCP awareness for supervisors.
- **Visitor Control** — Sign-in logs, protective clothing, escorted access.
- **Document Control & Record-Keeping** — Batch records, cleaning logs, CCP monitoring records, calibration logs.
- **Traceability & Recall** — One-step-forward/one-step-back traceability, **mock recall every 6 months**, **100% traceability within 4 hours**.
- **Management Review & Internal Audits** — Quarterly internal audits, annual management review meetings.
- **Supplier Control & Approval** — Approved vendor list (AVL), Certificate of Analysis (CoA) verification, supplier audits.
- **Customer Complaint Handling** — Logging, root cause analysis (RCA), corrective action (CA), preventive action (PA).
- **Crisis Management & Communication** — Food safety incident escalation matrix, media communication plan.

### 2.3.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for PI/PII/PIII

| **Parameter / Metric** | **Standard / Limit** | **Applicable Tier** |
|------------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Hand-wash frequency | Before & after every task | P-III |
| Training hours per worker per year | $\geq 16$ hours | P-III |
| Lighting in inspection areas | $\geq 540 \, lux$ | P-I |
| Cooking core temperature (meat) | $\geq 75 \, °C$ | P-II |
| Cold chain temperature | $\leq 5 \, °C$ | P-II |
| Residual chlorine in water | $0.2 - 0.5 \, ppm$ | P-I |
| Floor gradient | $\geq 1:60$ | P-I |
| Mock recall frequency | Every $6$ months | P-III |
| Traceability window | $\leq 4$ hours | P-III |
| Sanitizer (chlorine) | $50 - 200 \, ppm$ | P-II |
| Rodent stations spacing | $6 - 10 \, m$ | P-I |
| Pest control frequency | Monthly (minimum) | P-II |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In a KTU viva or a FSSAI audit, students must be able to **instantly classify** a given scenario into P-I, P-II, or P-III. For example:
> - "The floor is cracked" → **P-I** (Infrastructure)
> - "The cooking temperature dropped to 60 °C" → **P-II** (Process)
> - "The worker did not wash hands" → **P-III** (Personnel)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The PRP Decision-Tree

> [!NOTE]
> The following decision-tree is the **analytical backbone** for classifying any food safety control into the correct PRP tier (P-I, P-II, or P-III). This is a **frequently tested concept** in KTU Module 4 examinations.

### 3.1.1 Decision Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the nature of the control:**
- If it relates to **structure, environment, equipment, or physical infrastructure** → classify as **P-I**.
- If it relates to **operational procedure, process step, or routine activity** → classify as **P-II**.
- If it relates to **human behavior, training, records, or management oversight** → classify as **P-III**.

**Step 2 — Cross-verify with the FSSAI Schedule 4 mapping:**

$$\text{Control Element} \xrightarrow{\text{Identify Nature}} \begin{cases} \text{Static/Physical} \Rightarrow P\text{-I} \\ \text{Dynamic/Process} \Rightarrow P\text{-II} \\ \text{Human/Managerial} \Rightarrow P\text{-III} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the "5-W Test":**
- *What* is the control about? (Equipment, Process, Person)
- *Where* does it apply? (Premise, Production Floor, Office)
- *When* is it executed? (Pre-operation, During operation, Post-operation)
- *Who* is responsible? (Engineering, Production, QA/HR)
- *Why* is it needed? (Prevent environmental contamination, prevent process failure, prevent human error)

### 3.1.2 Worked Examples (Step-by-Step Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example 1:** "The stainless steel mixing tank in the dairy plant has developed surface rust."
>
> **Step 1** — Nature: Physical infrastructure issue (equipment condition).
> **Step 2** — Mapping: Equipment falls under P-I.
> **Step 3** — 5-W Test: What = Equipment, Where = Process floor, When = Pre-operation, Who = Engineering, Why = Prevent metal/rust contamination.
> **Conclusion:** This is a **P-I non-conformance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example 2:** "The pasteurization temperature was recorded at 68 °C instead of the mandated 75 °C."
>
> **Step 1** — Nature: Process control deviation (operational).
> **Step 2** — Mapping: Process temperature is a P-II control.
> **Step 3** — 5-W Test: What = Process, Where = Pasteurizer, When = During operation, Who = Production, Why = Pathogen kill.
> **Conclusion:** This is a **P-II non-conformance** and likely a **CCP failure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example 3:** "The newly joined packaging-line worker was not given food safety induction training."
>
> **Step 1** — Nature: Human resource / training gap.
> **Step 2** — Mapping: Training and competence is a P-III control.
> **Step 3** — 5-W Test: What = Training, Where = HR/QA, When = Pre-employment, Who = HR, Why = Ensure competent workforce.
> **Conclusion:** This is a **P-III non-conformance**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the PRP Effectiveness Score (For Audit Scoring)

In advanced food safety auditing, the **overall compliance** of a facility is sometimes quantified using a weighted score across the three PRP tiers.

### 3.2.1 Definition of Variables

Let:
- $S_{PI}$ = Compliance score for P-I (in %)
- $S_{PII}$ = Compliance score for P-II (in %)
- $S_{PIII}$ = Compliance score for P-III (in %)
- $w_1, w_2, w_3$ = Weightage factors for each tier

### 3.2.2 Standard Weightage (Per Codex & FSSAI Practice)

The industry-accepted weightage allocation is:

$$w_1 = 0.30 \quad \text{(P-I weight)}$$

$$w_2 = 0.40 \quad \text{(P-II weight — highest, as it directly affects the product)}$$

$$w_3 = 0.30 \quad \text{(P-III weight)}$$

### 3.2.3 Overall PRP Compliance Score (PRP-OACS)

The **Overall Audit Compliance Score** is calculated as:

$$\text{PRP-OACS} = (w_1 \times S_{PI}) + (w_2 \times S_{PII}) + (w_3 \times S_{PIII})$$

Substituting the weightage values:

$$\text{PRP-OACS} = (0.30 \times S_{PI}) + (0.40 \times S_{PII}) + (0.30 \times S_{PIII})$$

### 3.2.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A food processing unit was audited and the following compliance scores were obtained:
- P-I Score = $80\%$
- P-II Score = $90\%$
- P-III Score = $75\%$

**Step 1** — Identify the formula:

$$\text{PRP-OACS} = (0.30 \times S_{PI}) + (0.40 \times S_{PII}) + (0.30 \times S_{PIII})$$

**Step 2** — Substitute the values:

$$\text{PRP-OACS} = (0.30 \times 80) + (0.40 \times 90) + (0.30 \times 75)$$

**Step 3** — Compute each term:

$$\text{PRP-OACS} = 24 + 36 + 22.5$$

**Step 4** — Final sum:

$$\text{PRP-OACS} = 82.5\%$$

**Interpretation:**
- A score $\geq 90\%$ → **Excellent compliance**, audit ready.
- A score $75\% - 89\%$ → **Acceptable**, minor corrective actions needed.
- A score $60\% - 74\%$ → **Marginal**, significant improvements required.
- A score $\leq 60\%$ → **Non-compliant**, FSSAI license at risk.

In this example, the facility scores **82.5%** — *Acceptable* but with scope for improvement, particularly in P-III (only 75%).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — P-I vs P-II vs P-III

| **Attribute** | **P-I** | **P-II** | **P-III** |
|---------------|---------|----------|-----------|
| **Focus** | Infrastructure | Operations | People & Management |
| **Type of Control** | Preventive (static) | Preventive (dynamic) | Preventive (behavioral) |
| **Primary Hazard Addressed** | Environmental contamination | Process-related contamination | Human-error & management failure |
| **Responsible Department** | Engineering / Maintenance | Production / Sanitation | HR / Quality Assurance |
| **Audit Frequency** | Quarterly | Monthly | Quarterly |
| **Documents Required** | Layout drawings, equipment specs | SSOPs, GMP records, cleaning logs | Training records, medical reports, recall records |
| **Failure Consequence** | Structural contamination | Product contamination | System-wide failure |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | Moderate | High | High |

## 3.4 The PRP-HACCP Hierarchy (Conceptual Derivation)

The **food safety control hierarchy** can be derived as a four-tier pyramid:

$$\text{Tier 4 (Top)} = \text{HACCP Plan (CCP control)}$$

$$\text{Tier 3} = \text{P-III (People \& Management)}$$

$$\text{Tier 2} = \text{P-II (Operations \& Process)}$$

$$\text{Tier 1 (Base)} = \text{P-I (Infrastructure)}$$

**Logical derivation:**
- HACCP (Tier 4) is built *on top of* P-III.
- P-III (Tier 3) is built *on top of* P-II.
- P-II (Tier 2) is built *on top of* P-I.
- P-I (Tier 1) is the *foundation* (premises, equipment, environment).

$$\text{If P-I fails} \Rightarrow \text{All higher tiers collapse}$$

$$\text{If P-I and P-II are sound} \Rightarrow \text{P-III is easier to implement}$$

$$\text{If P-I, P-II, P-III are sound} \Rightarrow \text{HACCP plan is effective and auditable}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are short-answer conceptual questions of the type that appear in **KTU ESE Part A**. Each carries **3 marks**, with marks distributed as: *Definition (1) + Explanation (1) + Example (1)*.

### Question 1: Define Pre-requisite Programs (PRPs) and explain the role of P-I in food safety.

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

Pre-requisite Programs (PRPs) are the **foundational practices and conditions** that must be established in a food establishment before the HACCP plan can be effectively implemented. They provide the **basic environmental and operational framework** that ensures food safety.

**P-I (Infrastructure and Facility PRP)** addresses the **physical and engineering aspects** of a food facility, including:
- Design and layout of premises (zoning, product flow)
- Water supply (potable, tested, residual chlorine **0.2–0.5 ppm**)
- Equipment and utensils (food-grade **SS 304/316**)
- Lighting ($\geq 300$ lux) and ventilation
- Pest-proofing and drainage systems

**Example:** Installing **SS 304 stainless steel tables** in a meat processing unit is a P-I control, because it addresses the *physical infrastructure* that prevents contamination from corroded surfaces.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for PRP definition + 1 mark for P-I components + 1 mark for example)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between P-II and P-III guidelines with one example each.

**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **P-II Guidelines** | **P-III Guidelines** |
|------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Focus | Operational procedures | People and management |
| Domain | Process control, sanitation, GMP | Training, records, traceability |
| Nature | Dynamic (executed during production) | Behavioral and managerial |
| Example | Time-temperature control of pasteurization at $75 \, °C$ | Annual food safety training of $\geq 16$ hours per worker |

**P-II Example:** Monitoring cooking temperature to ensure meat reaches a core temperature of $\geq 75 \, °C$ is a P-II control.

**P-III Example:** Conducting a mock recall every 6 months to validate traceability is a P-III control.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for tabular difference + 1 mark for P-II example + 1 mark for P-III example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B carries **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Below, **Option A and Option B** are provided as **independent, complete alternatives**. Each option is divided into **Part (a) — 7 marks** and **Part (b) — 7 marks**, with full model solutions.

---

### ✅ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**"Explain the P-I, P-II, P-III classification of Pre-requisite Programs. Discuss any FIVE control measures under each tier."**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

The P-I, P-II, P-III classification is a **structured framework** recommended by **Codex Alimentarius (CAC/RCP 1-1969, Rev. 2020)** and adopted by **FSSAI** for organizing all prerequisite controls in a food establishment.

**P-I — Infrastructure and Facility (5 controls):**
1. **Premises Layout:** Linear product flow, zoning (high-risk vs low-risk), separation of raw and cooked areas.
2. **Water Supply:** Potable water with residual chlorine maintained at **0.2–0.5 ppm**; tested every 6 months.
3. **Equipment Material:** All food contact surfaces must be **SS 304/316** grade stainless steel, non-toxic, non-corroding.
4. **Lighting:** Minimum **300 lux** in production areas; **540 lux** at inspection points; all bulbs protected/shatter-proof.
5. **Pest Control:** Rodent bait stations every **6–10 m**; insectocutors at all entry points; sealed doors and windows.

**P-II — Operations and Process (5 controls):**
1. **SSOPs:** Pre-operational cleaning of all equipment with foam cleaner, followed by sanitizer rinse (**chlorine 100–200 ppm**).
2. **Time-Temperature Control:** Cooking to $\geq 75 \, °C$, hot holding $\geq 63 \, °C$, cold storage $\leq 5 \, °C$.
3. **Cross-Contamination Control:** Color-coded chopping boards (red=raw meat, green=vegetables, yellow=cooked food, blue=fish).
4. **Allergen Management:** Dedicated storage, separate production lines, validated cleaning between allergen runs.
5. **Pest Control Operations:** Monthly fumigation, daily inspection of bait stations, log maintenance.

**P-III — People and Management (5 controls):**
1. **Personal Hygiene:** Mandatory hand-washing before and after every task; no jewelry; clean uniforms and hairnets.
2. **Training:** $\geq 16$ hours/year of food safety training; HACCP awareness for all supervisors.
3. **Medical Fitness:** Annual health check-ups; no worker with communicable disease allowed in food area.
4. **Record-Keeping:** Batch production records, CCP monitoring logs, cleaning records, calibration logs.
5. **Traceability & Mock Recall:** One-step-forward/one-step-back traceability; mock recall every 6 months; 100% traceability within 4 hours.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for classification concept + 5 marks for the 5 controls under each tier = 5 × 1 mark)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**"A fruit juice processing unit was audited. The following observations were made:**
- **(i) Floor in the production area was chipped and water was pooling.**
- **(ii) The pasteurizer temperature was recorded at 65 °C against the required 95 °C.**
- **(iii) The newly appointed quality supervisor had no HACCP training.**
- **(iv) No pest control records were available for the last 3 months.**

**Classify each observation into P-I, P-II, or P-III and justify your classification."**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

| **Observation** | **Classification** | **Justification** |
|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| (i) Chipped floor with water pooling | **P-I** | Floor integrity and drainage are **infrastructure and facility** issues. Chipped floors harbor microorganisms; water pooling indicates inadequate drainage gradient (should be $\geq 1:60$). |
| (ii) Pasteurizer at 65 °C instead of 95 °C | **P-II** | Pasteurization temperature is a **process control** measure. This is a direct deviation in operational parameters and may be a **CCP failure** (under-processing, pathogen survival risk). |
| (iii) New QA supervisor without HACCP training | **P-III** | Training and competence of personnel is a **people and management** control. Untrained staff is a human-error risk. |
| (iv) No pest control records for 3 months | **P-II** (operational) or **P-III** (documentation) | While pest control *operations* are P-II, the **absence of records** makes this primarily a P-III failure (document control and record-keeping). |

**Overall Audit Conclusion:** The unit has **3 critical non-conformances** (P-I floor issue, P-II process deviation, P-III training gap) and **1 documentation failure**. Immediate corrective action is required; license renewal at risk.

*(7 marks: 4 × 1.5 marks per observation (0.5 classification + 1.0 justification) + 1 mark for audit conclusion)*

---

### ✅ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**"Discuss the regulatory framework supporting the PI, PII, PIII guidelines. Explain how ISO 22000:2018 and FSSAI Schedule 4 mandate these three tiers."**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

The **P-I, P-II, P-III** tiered PRP structure is anchored in multiple regulatory frameworks:

**1. Codex Alimentarius — CAC/RCP 1-1969 (Rev. 2020):**
- The Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene define PRPs in **Section 4** as *"practices and conditions needed prior to and during the implementation of HACCP"*.
- The codex organizes these into **Primary Production (P-I aligned), Processing (P-II aligned), and Training/Management (P-III aligned)**.

**2. ISO 22000:2018 — Clause 8.2 (Prerequisite programmes):**
- ISO 22000 explicitly mandates that organizations must:
  - **8.2.1:** Establish, implement, and maintain PRPs.
  - **8.2.2:** Establish PRP criteria.
  - **8.2.3:** Establish a PRP-based monitoring system.
  - **8.2.4:** Verify the effectiveness of PRPs through internal audit and review.
- The PI/PII/PIII structure maps to ISO 22000 Clause 8.2.1 as:
  - P-I → Infrastructure (building, equipment, utilities)
  - P-II → Operational PRPs (cleaning, sanitation, pest control)
  - P-III → Managerial PRPs (training, documentation, communication)

**3. FSSAI — Schedule 4 (Part II) of the FSSAI Regulations, 2011:**
- Schedule 4 specifies **"General Hygienic and Sanitary Practices to be followed by Food Business Operators"**.
- It is organized into:
  - **Part II A:** Premises and facilities → **P-I** controls
  - **Part II B:** Sanitary and hygienic practices → **P-II** controls
  - **Part II C:** Personal hygiene and training → **P-III** controls

**4. BIS IS 2491:2013:**
- The Indian Standard on "Code for Hygiene in Food Processing Units" follows the same three-tier logic.

**5. WHO Five Keys to Safer Food:**
- The WHO's *Five Keys* (Clean, Separate, Cook, Chill, Safe water/materials) collectively address PI/PII/PIII controls.

**Synthesis:**
$$\text{Codex} \rightarrow \text{ISO 22000} \rightarrow \text{FSSAI Schedule 4} \rightarrow \text{BIS IS 2491} \rightarrow \text{Industry PRP Manual (PI/PII/PIII)}$$

The P-I, P-II, P-III classification is **globally harmonized** and provides a common language for food safety audits across jurisdictions.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for Codex + 2 marks for ISO 22000 + 2 marks for FSSAI + 1 mark for synthesis)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**"Calculate the Overall PRP Audit Compliance Score (PRP-OACS) for a biscuit manufacturing unit with the following compliance data:**

- **P-I Score = 85%**
- **P-II Score = 78%**
- **P-III Score = 92%**

**Use the standard weightage of 0.30, 0.40, 0.30 respectively. Interpret the result and recommend two specific improvement actions for the lowest-scoring tier."**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$\text{PRP-OACS} = (0.30 \times S_{PI}) + (0.40 \times S_{PII}) + (0.30 \times S_{PIII})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**

$$\text{PRP-OACS} = (0.30 \times 85) + (0.40 \times 78) + (0.30 \times 92)$$

**Step 3 — Calculate each term:**

$$\text{PRP-OACS} = 25.5 + 31.2 + 27.6$$

**Step 4 — Final sum:**

$$\text{PRP-OACS} = 84.3\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (as per KTU grading scale):**

| **Score Range** | **Classification** |
|-----------------|---------------------|
| $\geq 90\%$ | Excellent (Audit-ready) |
| $75\% - 89\%$ | Acceptable (Minor CAPA needed) |
| $60\% - 74\%$ | Marginal (Major improvements needed) |
| $\leq 60\%$ | Non-compliant |

The unit scores **84.3%** → **Acceptable category**, but below the 90% excellence benchmark.

**Step 6 — Identify the lowest tier:**
- P-I = 85%, P-II = 78%, P-III = 92%
- **Lowest tier = P-II (78%)**

**Step 7 — Recommend two improvement actions for P-II:**

1. **Strengthen SSOPs and Sanitation Schedules:** Conduct a full SSOP review, install ATP swab testing for cleaning validation, and increase cleaning frequency from once-per-shift to between-product-changeover.

2. **Improve Time-Temperature Control:** Install continuous data loggers on the baking oven and cooling conveyor; train operators on critical limit monitoring; implement a real-time alarm system for any deviation from the $180 \, °C$ baking temperature for $12$ minutes.

**Conclusion:** The unit is in the "Acceptable" zone but must focus on **P-II** (operations) to elevate its compliance score to the 90% "excellence" benchmark and reduce product safety risk.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for formula + 1.5 marks for substitution and computation + 1.5 marks for interpretation + 2 marks for two improvement actions)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Examinations (PI/PII/PIII Questions):**
>
> 1. **❌ Mixing up tiers:** Students often classify a *training* question as P-II or an *equipment* question as P-III. **Remember the test:** "Is it about **structure** (P-I), **operation** (P-II), or **people** (P-III)?"
> 2. **❌ Writing only definitions, no examples:** A 7-mark question on P-I/P-II/P-III **must** include specific control measures and examples. Generic answers like "P-I is about infrastructure" will not fetch full marks.
> 3. **❌ Ignoring numerical questions:** KTU frequently tests the **PRP-OACS formula**. Students who do not memorize the weightage (0.30, 0.40, 0.30) and the formula lose 3-4 marks easily.
> 4. **❌ Not mentioning regulatory backing:** Always cite **Codex Alimentarius, FSSAI Schedule 4, ISO 22000 Clause 8.2** — this fetches 1-2 extra marks.
> 5. **❌ Confusing PRP with CCP:** PRPs (P-I/P-II/P-III) are **general preventive controls**; CCPs are **specific critical control points** within the HACCP plan. Mixing these is a common 2-mark deduction error.
> 6. **❌ Forgetting units and standards:** Always quote specific numbers — **75 °C cooking**, **0.2–0.5 ppm chlorine**, **300 lux lighting**, **16 hours training** — these are KTU-favorite high-yield values.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for PI/PII/PIII Guidelines:**

- ✅ **PI/PII/PIII = Three-Tier Pre-requisite Program (PRP) Classification** under Codex, FSSAI & ISO 22000.
- ✅ **P-I = Infrastructure & Facility** → Premises, water, equipment, lighting, pest-proofing, drainage.
- ✅ **P-II = Operations & Process** → SSOPs, GMP, time-temperature control, cross-contamination, sanitation.
- ✅ **P-III = People & Management** → Training ($\geq 16$ hr/yr), personal hygiene, records, traceability, mock recall.
- ✅ **Standard weightage** for PRP scoring: $w_1 = 0.30$, $w_2 = 0.40$, $w_3 = 0.30$.
- ✅ **PRP-OACS formula:** $(0.30 \times S_{PI}) + (0.40 \times S_{PII}) + (0.30 \times S_{PIII})$.
- ✅ **Key regulatory references:** Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969, FSSAI Schedule 4, ISO 22000:2018 Clause 8.2, BIS IS 2491:2013.
- ✅ **High-yield values to memorize:**
  - Cooking (meat): $\geq 75 \, °C$
  - Cold storage: $\leq 5 \, °C$
  - Hot holding: $\geq 63 \, °C$
  - Freezer: $\leq -18 \, °C$
  - Residual chlorine: $0.2 - 0.5 \, ppm$
  - Sanitizer (chlorine): $50 - 200 \, ppm$
  - Lighting (production): $\geq 300 \, lux$
  - Lighting (inspection): $\geq 540 \, lux$
  - Training: $\geq 16$ hr/yr
  - Mock recall: Every 6 months
  - Traceability: $\leq 4$ hours
  - Floor gradient: $\geq 1:60$
- ✅ **The PRP-HACCP Pyramid:** P-I (base) → P-II → P-III → HACCP CCPs (apex).
- ✅ **Decision rule for classification:** *Structure = P-I; Process = P-II; People = P-III.*
- ✅ **Audit grading scale:** $\geq 90\%$ Excellent; $75$–$89\%$ Acceptable; $60$–$74\%$ Marginal; $\leq 60\%$ Non-compliant.
- ✅ **One-line memory hook:** *"P-I builds the kitchen, P-II cooks the food, P-III trains the cook."*
